2016-12-14

Hervé Pagès (00:59:16): > @Hervé Pagès has joined the channel

Sean Davis (10:29:52): > Is this what you were thinking of,@Levi Waldron?http://bioconductor.org/packages/MLSeq - Attachment (Bioconductor): MLSeq > This package applies several machine learning methods, including SVM, bagSVM, Random Forest and CART, to RNA-Seq data.

Sean Davis (10:32:15): > Well, probably not yet, unless you are working on text mining, image analysis, or designing intelligent chat bots (we should investigate that one with IBM at some point!). - Attachment: Attachment > Should we be interested?

2016-12-16

Sean Davis (12:10:50): > Some nice presentations here:http://nationalacademies.org/hmd/Activities/Disease/NCPF/2016-DEC-12.aspx - Attachment (nationalacademies.org): The Drug Development Paradigm in Oncology: A Workshop : Health and Medicine Division > This workshop will examine the challenges of improving the efficiency of the drug development and regulatory review process, while ensuring the safety of new therapies.

2016-12-19

Sean Davis (08:46:23): > Just a note that > > /remind me to do something tomorrow > > will have slack create a reminder for you “to do something” and will send you a private slack message tomorrow (9am is the default). It can do much more complicated reminders as well for others and for whole channels.https://get.slack.help/hc/en-us/articles/208423427-Set-a-reminder - Attachment (Slack Help Center): Set a reminder

Levi Waldron (08:46:57): > Wow that’s nice!

Sean Davis (08:47:56): > Yeah. Pretty simple but very effective.

Levi Waldron (08:48:29): > Yeah, very nice.

2016-12-22

Sean Davis (13:12:47): > Title: Big Data processing & Medicine – a use case presentation > Date: January 17, 2017, 10:00-12:00pm > Location: Porter Neuroscience Building, 35A Room 610 > Videocast:https://videocast.nih.gov/Registration: No registration is required >
> Audience: This is intended for a general scientific audience with an interest in modern computing. No programming is required. >
> Abstract: Big Data processing with Hadoop is revolutionizing medicine. Enabling Genomic Analysis on much larger scale, providing the backbone for precision medicine by unifying research & clinical silos, and enabling bedside data to be kept for retrospective analysis. This talk will cover real uses cases in Genomics and Medicine for big data and Hadoop. It will also touch on the technology used to accomplish the results. >
> Additional Details > This presentation focuses on biomedical challenges such as: > 1. Predicting clinical events in order to: > a. Reduce hospital readmissions. Hospital systems want to understand the spending and causes on hospital readmissions and reduce those readmissions. They also want to predict the probability of a discharged patient being re-admitted for the same or a similar condition. > b. Reduce preventable death from issues such as sepsis that require real time inputs as well as retrospective data through scalable machine learning. > 2. Gaining clinical insight on acute patients from data generated from multiple streaming sensors. > 3. Getting meaningful clinical value from genomic data (Whole Genome or Gene-Wide Association Studies) and speeding up analyses.

Lucas Schiffer (13:41:13): > AWESOME!

2017-01-06

Sean Davis (10:46:47): > Authorea is beginning to look like an interesting alternative to Google Docs for collaborative editing.https://www.authorea.com - Attachment (authorea.com): Authorea > Authorea is the leading platform to write, cite, collaborate, host, and publish.

Kasper D. Hansen (10:54:13): > perhaps I should have another look. Last time I considered it I ended up liking overleaf a lot more. I don’t recall exact reasons though

Sean Davis (10:56:51): > I came to the same conclusion before. I put this out there as just a suggestion. I’d definitely be interested to hear a critical evaluation from an Overleaf user. For completeness, here is the overleaf link:https://www.overleaf.com/ - Attachment (overleaf.com): Overleaf: Real-time Collaborative Writing and Publishing Tools with Integrated PDF Preview > The online platform for scientific writing. Overleaf is free: start writing now with one click. No sign-up required. Great on your iPad.

Manage your To-Do lists inside your Slack chat wihout leaving your application.

2017-02-09

Lori Shepherd (14:41:59): > @Lori Shepherd has joined the channel

2017-02-10

Sean Davis (19:16:57): > There are three highly active and related Containerized Tool/Workflow groups: GA4GH (Containers and Workflows Task Team), NCI (NCI Containers and Workflows Interest Group), and NIH (Commons Framework Working Group on Workflow Sharing and Docker Registry). In the NIH group, there has been a call to get concrete about demonstrating tool portability. We have proposed to organize an exercise (Phase 1) whereby a small group of interested parties download a CWL/WDL tool described on Dockstore via the GA4GH Tool Registry API, run it in their system, and then upload the results back to a central registry on Synapse along with a description of how they ran in their system of choice. Groups that complete this will be included in a forthcoming paper where we describe metadata and the GA4GH API standard for exchanging Docker-based tools and workflows in CWL/WDL. Longer term, we’d like to perform a Phase 2 challenge where we will use a more complex workflow, ideally one of the GDC pipelines and this will culminate in a presentation at ISMB/BOSC 2017. We will formalize this into an infrastructure Dream Challenge in conjunction with Sage (but that takes time and we’d like to finish Phase 1 in ~30 days and Phase 2 by April 2017). A Phase 3 will exercise the Task and Workflow execution APIs currently being developed in the GA4GH group.

Sean Davis (19:17:06): > https://www.synapse.org/#!Synapse:syn8080249

Sean Davis (19:17:47): > The note and link above are to an Dream Challenge around reproducible workflows.

Lucas Schiffer (21:19:01): > @Sean Davisthis sounds great and like a really nice push for the NCI & NIH. I will definitely try the docker tool and report back (to the central registry that is).

2017-02-20

Aedin Culhane (12:51:59): > Thanks@Sean Davis

2018-02-15

Sean Davis (03:56:32): > I had heard a talk about this and found it interesting–new from Lior Pachtor on single cell “marker genes” without transcript/gene quantification:https://www.biorxiv.org/content/early/2018/02/14/258566 - Attachment (bioRxiv): Identification of transcriptional signatures for cell types from single-cell RNA-Seq > Single-cell RNA-Seq makes it possible to characterize the transcriptomes of cell types and identify their transcriptional signatures via differential analysis. We present a fast and accurate method for discriminating cell types that takes advantage of the large numbers of cells that are assayed. When applied to transcript compatibility counts obtained via pseudoalignment, our approach provides a quantification-free analysis of 3’ single-cell RNA-Seq that can identify previously undetectable marker genes.

Sean Davis (14:01:05): > I’m a big fan of python, but I had to share this XKCD:https://xkcd.com/1987/ - Attachment: Python Environment > [Title text] “The Python environmental protection agency wants to seal it in a cement chamber, with pictoral messages to future civilizations warning them about the danger of using sudo to install random Python packages.”

Federico Marini (14:15:30): > saw that too, and felt related quite immediately!

Federico Marini (14:20:11): > still he forgets that some pain can be saved with the bioconda repo

Martin Morgan (14:23:03): > I think you mean that bioconda can make the cartoon 2x more complicated!

Vince Carey (14:24:16): > I circulated this in my lab. It was not well-received. The fact that it is about “the danger of using sudo…” is perhaps worth keeping in mind.

2018-05-02

Mike Smith (11:53:15): > Does any one know a supplier for getting hex stickers printed - preferably in Europe? Maybe@Laurent Gatto

Federico Marini (11:54:11): > I recall Stickermule being proposed? But I did not try yet

Peter Hickey (12:18:23): > https://twitter.com/lincolnmullen/status/951169094367240192 - Attachment (twitter): Attachment > (1) Do any of you have recommendations for a vendor to print hex stickers? and (2) Can any of you recommend where I can get #rstats related hex stickers? Dario Righelli (10:05:51): > Where do you print your exagonal stickers? which is the “official” dimension?

Davide Risso (10:10:30): > Seehttps://github.com/Bioconductor/BiocStickers/blob/master/README.mdfor instructions/templates/examples - Attachment (GitHub): Bioconductor/BiocStickers > BiocStickers - Stickers for some Bioconductor packages - feel free to contribute and/or modify.

Davide Risso (10:12:52): > For printing, I think people use stickermule but I have no direct experience…

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (10:14:00): > I’ve gone through StickerMule, delivery was fast and nothing to complain about the quality.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (10:14:56): > For the template, the first time, I’ve taken one of the existing one in PDF format, opened it up in Illustrator, edited it, and saved it under a different name.

Davide Risso (10:19:14): > Another option is to create it directly in R with the hexSticker package (https://github.com/GuangchuangYu/hexSticker) - Attachment (GitHub): GuangchuangYu/hexSticker > hexSticker - :sparkles:Hexagon sticker in R

Dario Righelli (10:21:59): > ok, thanks!

Dario Righelli (10:22:45): > I was looking to some vendors, and stickermule seems to be the best one…

Aaron Lun (10:17:54): > ARGHH forgot my Bioconductor password and my WEHI email password. Can’t log in and answer questions until I get home…

Aaron Lun (10:18:05): > Need my fix man.

Levi Waldron (13:20:36): > I can’t remember any passwords anymore:laughing:

Aaron Lun (13:23:09): > Yes, normally it would have been okay, but I haven’t turned on my mac in so long that it seems to have forgotten.

Davide Risso (13:42:22): > I guess you already tried keychain?

Kasper D. Hansen (13:58:03): > 1password. Expensive but has revolutionized my life

Stephanie Hicks (18:31:01): > big +1 for 1password

Stephanie Hicks (18:32:10): > theCommand + Backslashoperation of filling it passwords islife changing

Aaron Lun (20:33:21): > Got my fix.

Kasper D. Hansen (20:41:23): > perhaps we should add the condition that if@Aaron Lundoesn’t log into the site for 48 hours, his reputation gets reset

2018-08-30

Levi Waldron (14:22:16): > Is 1password better than Lastpass?

Levi Waldron (14:33:48): > Based on my quick google search, they seem fairly equivalent… I already I think >500 passwords on Lastpass so I’ll stick with it!

Kasper D. Hansen (14:57:56): > Lastpass is cheaper

Kasper D. Hansen (14:58:08): > It also had a security breach AFAIK some years ago

Kasper D. Hansen (14:58:27): > Apple gives out 1password for free to their employees

Kasper D. Hansen (14:58:52): > Lastpass is >>>> no manager

Levi Waldron (14:59:33): > heh, that is for sure

Kasper D. Hansen (14:59:58): > 1password has a dropbox version (not sure if it was lost in the latest update which I don’t have)

Levi Waldron (15:04:54): > I like their options for multifactor authentication which include a grid of codes that I printed on a piece of paper. Somehow relying on some data that exists only on paper as one step gives me some security peace of mind.

Levi Waldron (15:10:28): > grid multifactor authentication - cool right? - File (PNG): Pasted image at 2018-08-30, 3:10 PM

Kasper D. Hansen (21:06:33): > So you use MFA to unlock Lastpass?

Kasper D. Hansen (21:06:41): > I just have a password and/or fingerprint

Kasper D. Hansen (21:06:50): > But I also unlock it like 30 times a day

Kasper D. Hansen (21:07:19): > The fact that I can put my data on my own dropbox is a big deal for me, since that is much less attrative to hackers.

2018-09-03

Aaron Lun (15:51:20): > I see@Levi Waldrona lot on the slack loading screen. Who gets to put messages there?

Aaron Lun (15:51:35): > just curious. Well, mostly just curious.

Levi Waldron (15:51:50): > I do :)

Aaron Lun (15:52:29): > ah, I see it.

2018-09-04

Sean Davis (09:48:51): > Just FYI:https://twitter.com/seandavis12/status/1036974009865449473 - Attachment (twitter): Attachment > Talk today by @stevetsa1 at 11am EDT: “Containerization for Reproducible #Bioinformatics Research” available via Webex: > http://bit.ly/2NLmDpL > @NIHDataScience @IRPatNIH > #DataScience https://pbs.twimg.com/media/DmQSVhEW4AAnvV_.jpg

Sean Davis (09:49:22): > Our own@Steve Tsangis giving the talk.

Kasper D. Hansen (10:01:06): > slides?

Sean Davis (11:57:56): > @Steve Tsang?

Steve Tsang (16:18:53): > Here is the deck -https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/16f8_z6tlULBc9nXLghCp04TGyVlZzKzazUNbdprROhs/edit

Steve Tsang (16:19:47): > The presentation was delivered using the Jupyter notebook version with a docker container running in the back end. Here is the repo -https://github.com/stevetsa/nlmreproducibility - Attachment (GitHub): stevetsa/nlmreproducibility > Contribute to stevetsa/nlmreproducibility development by creating an account on GitHub.

Sean Davis (16:22:59): > Sweet!

2018-09-05

Kasper D. Hansen (10:52:22): > That was interesting, but IMO also a bit uncritical focus on containers

Sean Davis (12:05:18): > Are you using containers for anything, Kasper? Just curious.

Kasper D. Hansen (14:27:51): > Occasionally for running specific stuff. Like I use bioconda, and I have been using docker to replicate some issues wrt. compiler errors. I don’t use containers in production but I see a clear usecase here.

Kasper D. Hansen (14:28:31): > My issues are with containers as a solution to reproducibility. My understanding is that Docker changes a lot. And it is not clear to me that a workflow in a container in any way is guranteed to run the same 5 or 10 years later

Kasper D. Hansen (14:29:45): > For example the Dockerfile on p19 of the presentation does not specific specific versions of everything (although I know you can)

Sean Davis (14:29:56): > Agreed that technologies have lifespans. That said, containers make some things that are hardtodaysomewhat easier.

Kasper D. Hansen (14:30:09): > Docker and containers seem to me to solve the issue of ceploying a complicated workflow on heterogenous systems. Thats a major feat

Kasper D. Hansen (14:30:32): > My issue is specific to containers and reproducibility

Sean Davis (14:30:42): > And Steve definitely pointed out that a best practice for containers is to specify versions of everything and to version the containers themselves.

Sean Davis (14:31:11): > Full reproducibility is hard, no matter what tech is used.

Kasper D. Hansen (14:31:13): > Yeah, so do we know we can run 2 yer old docker workflows

Kasper D. Hansen (14:31:28): > Yes, I agree that full repro is hard. I also don’t think we need to strive for full reproducibility

Kasper D. Hansen (14:31:49): > And I do not have much experience with containers and no experience with containers over time.

Kasper D. Hansen (14:33:28): > Like are these repositories where you pull the software from, actually persistently, long-term maintained?

Kasper D. Hansen (14:34:13): > But I think I get that containers are massively useful for deploying code on systems

James Taylor (15:01:50): > @Kasper D. HansenDockerfiles definitely do not solve reproducibility. The actual containers need to be archived. That preserves versions. But well specified packages inside containers is better still.

James Taylor (15:02:42): > Bioconda has no Dockerfiles at all.

Steve Tsang (15:05:06): > @Kasper D. HansenPoints well taken. As an introduction, the presentation focused more on how we can leverage this technology (with other technologies) to increase reproducibility. Just FYI - I am still able to run dockerized tools I created more than 2 years ago on the Seven Bridges platform.

Steve Tsang (15:06:13): > @James TaylorYes, Docker images is the key. Wouldn’t it be better if you also have the dockerfile associated with that particular image though so you know exactly the steps/contents in the image?

James Taylor (15:08:25): > @Steve TsangYes, if there was such a Dockerfile. Bioconda just skips that step. The code and metadata to generate the containers is all in github though and can be reconstructed. Dockerfiles are just less well specified metadata. Incomplete version info and such.

Steve Tsang (15:10:56): > I was thinking the other day, if Docker goes under and Docker Hub goes away today………[fill in the blank]

2018-09-06

Vince Carey (10:52:05): > Slide 16 is a candidate for cheat-sheet of the year. However there is a place where I generally break down owing to rarity of contact with docker: It is fine for establishing a software environment, but how do I make use of local data? I recall some concept of file system mounting and of course I can google it and cobble something together as needed. Some kind of soup-to-nuts Bioconductor package could be useful … I don’t have a compelling use case at present – some exotic open source tool that I’d rather not build locally but can get some genomics mileage with.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:00:42): > Having little experience with containers in a production environment (I’ve only set up an automatic build to distribute the iSEE package and some instances of Shiny apps), I’m also curious about applications of Docker containers to process data (if that’s indeed what we’re talking about here). I seem to recall@Davis McCarthytweeting about his experience:https://twitter.com/davisjmcc/status/999059677089583104How painful would it be to put together a MWE (e.g. even a ‘not so exotic’ workflow that most people could picture easily like RNA-seq quantitation or even basic QC)? - Attachment (twitter): Attachment > I mean, I don’t want to spend my entire working life pre-processing data, but it is pretty satisfying to set hundreds of thousands of jobs running in a now completely containerised workflow :ant::ant::ant::ant:

Sean Davis (11:03:57): > @Vince Carey, using local data is pretty simple. Just “mount” the local directory into the container. > > docker run -v /data/abc:/data/abc_in_container … > > will mount the/data/abcdirectory into the container at/data/abc_in_container.

Sean Davis (11:06:53): > @Kevin Rue-AlbrechtToolkits like NextFlow, Snakemake, Toil, and Cromwell integrate nicely with docker (and some with singularity) containers. I have run 12k RNA-seq samples entirely on the google cloud using Cromwell (and wdlRunR) in about 6 hours using 24k cores (about $0.10 per sample, Salmon). The same workflow will run with other Cromwell-supported backends.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:08:54): > Ow, nice. Can I naively ask what sort of learning curve a newcomer to Docker and cloud platforms would be looking at?

Sean Davis (11:44:46): > If you want to just use Docker to build a reusable image, about 1 hour should get you going.

Sean Davis (11:45:53): > If you want to adopt cloud-scale processing, the process isn’t too involved, but troubleshooting and debugging go to a whole new level.

Kasper D. Hansen (11:51:05): > One issue with Docker is that it is a no-go on local HPC resources because of the requirement for root

Kasper D. Hansen (11:51:10): > In my understanding

Kasper D. Hansen (11:51:16): > That is not an issue on AWS

Kasper D. Hansen (11:51:20): > or your laptop

James Taylor (12:28:43): > Singularity works pretty nicely for HPC needs though, and can import docker images (as unprivileged users).

Kasper D. Hansen (13:33:07): > Thats my understanding too - you’re the expert. Is there anything needed at the systems level for a user to deploy singularity (I have never tried it)?

James Taylor (13:49:55): > I believe it does need to be installed by the admin (at the very least, it needs certain modules like squashfs to be installed).

James Taylor (13:50:35): > But, lots of HPC folks are buying into it (e.g. we have it on MARCC, and you know how conservative our folks are). It’s working well for me. Changing my life honestly.

James Taylor (13:50:52): > There is also udocker for a 100% user space solution. That I have not tried.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (13:57:26): > Joining back the conversation - thanks for the pointers! > Our Oxford-based group is currently centred around the Python-based Ruffus module (http://www.ruffus.org.uk) to build workflows, using the another Python module developed by the CGAT group (https://github.com/cgat-developers/cgat-core) to interface with HPC queue managers (e.g. writing Python functions that get turned into SLURM jobs). > It’s got good and bad sides - as everything - with version control of dependencies partially addressed by Conda environments, so I am genuinely curious about pros and cons of alternative methods. Thanks again for the pointers - Attachment (ruffus.org.uk): Ruffus — ruffus 2.6.3 documentation > Ruffus is a Computation Pipeline library for python. It is open-sourced, powerful and user-friendly, and widely used in science and bioinformatics - Attachment (GitHub): cgat-developers/cgat-core > Core functionality of the CGAT code. Contribute to cgat-developers/cgat-core development by creating an account on GitHub.

Artem Sokolov (14:12:55) (in thread): > It’s worth mentioningdocker-compose(https://docs.docker.com/compose/overview/), which can simplify adocker runcommand if you have a large number of local directories that need to be mounted inside the container. It also resolves local paths, whereasdocker run -vrequires full paths, which can be daunting to specify. Here’s a “Hello world” example:https://github.com/datarail/concat-docker - Attachment (Docker Documentation): Overview of Docker Compose > Looking for Compose file reference? Find the latest version here. Compose is a tool for defining and running multi-container Docker applications. With Compose, you use a YAML file to configure… - Attachment (GitHub): datarail/concat-docker > An example of using Docker and Docker compose in our semi-standardised pipeline - datarail/concat-docker

Sean Davis (14:42:56): > We are heavy users of snakemake at NIH, but NextFlow is gaining popularity for HPC environments as well. I had used Ruffus for a while but moved on to Snakemake several years ago.

Sean Davis (14:47:16) (in thread): > Great point,@Artem Sokolov.

2018-09-10

Sean Davis (15:02:05): > This might be of interest to a few folks on this list:https://precision.fda.gov/challenges - Attachment (precision.fda.gov): PrecisionFDA Challenges – precisionFDA > A community for NGS assay evaluation

Sean Davis (15:02:40): > In collaboration with NIH/NCI and Nature Medicine, precisionFDA is launching yet another challenge – the NCI Mislabeling Challenge!​ Please note that you can read the commentary about this exciting challenge on Nature Medicine athttps://www.nature.com/articles/s41591-018-0180-x. > > So why are we doing this? In biomedical research, sample mislabeling or incorrect annotation has been a long-standing issue contributing to irreproducible results and invalid conclusions. These issues are particularly prevalent in large scale multi-omics studies, in which multiple different omics experiments are carried out at different time periods and/or in different labs and human errors can arise during sample transferring, sample tracking, large-scale data generation, and data sharing/management. On the other hand, simultaneous use of multiple types of omics platforms to characterize a large set of biological samples, as utilized in The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) and the Clinical Proteomic Tumor Analysis Consortium (CPTAC) projects, is becoming a powerful and popular approach to precision medicine. > > Thus, there is a pressing need to have computation programs that automatically screen for and remedy such incorrectly sample labels or annotations. This is possible because parallel data sets for the same samples actually provide better information to identify and correct mislabeled samples than what we usually have in traditional biological/medical studies. The goal of this challenge is to develop computational algorithms that can accurately detect and correct mislabeled data in large-scale multi-omics studies. - Attachment (Nature Medicine): Right data for right patient—a precisionFDA NCI–CPTAC Mult > To address a critical roadblock that can occur in translational and clinical research, the National Cancer Institute and the Food and Drug Administration, in coordination with the DREAM Challenges, are launching the first computational challenge using multi-omics datasets to detect and correct specimen mislabeling.

2018-09-13

Kayla Interdonato (11:09:10): > @Kayla Interdonato has joined the channel

Federico Marini (05:20:01): > @Levi Waldronwhen I read the login messages I almost want to logout and login again:slightly_smiling_face:

2018-09-19

Stian Lågstad (10:50:52): > Might be interesting to some:https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/announcing-azure-pipelines-with-unlimited-ci-cd-minutes-for-open-source/They’re offering free builds on linux, mac, and windows for open source projects. Makes we want to move from using travis to using azure:slightly_smiling_face: - Attachment (Microsoft Azure): Announcing Azure Pipelines with unlimited CI/CD minutes for open source > With the introduction of Azure DevOps today, we’re offering developers a new CI/CD service called Azure Pipelines that enables you to continuously build, test, and deploy to any platform or cloud.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (10:53:17): > Arf.. I didn’t take that in note properly, but someone at the Genome Informatics 2018 conference acknowledged using this platform in their presentation today. Just to say it is definitely viable in production; although I don’t have experience myself.

Federico Marini (10:54:46): > circleCI might be also a nice option

Federico Marini (10:55:02): > the conda people did the migration a while ago

Stian Lågstad (13:24:01): > Can I ignore this warning in my package build? The package doesn’t useshiftby itself, onlyIRanges::shift. > > * checking whether package 'chimeraviz' can be installed ... WARNING > Found the following significant warnings: > Warning: replacing previous import 'IRanges::shift' by 'data.table::shift' when loading 'chimeraviz' > > (Fromhttps://www.bioconductor.org/checkResults/3.7/bioc-LATEST/chimeraviz/tokay2-checksrc.html)

Stian Lågstad (13:24:30) (in thread): > (Wasn’t sure where to ask)

Aaron Lun (13:29:07) (in thread): > Do you needdata.table::shift?

Stian Lågstad (13:30:32) (in thread): > No. I only usedata.table::fread(example reference:https://github.com/stianlagstad/chimeraviz/blob/master/R/import_defuse.R#L58).data.tableis listed inDESCRIPTIONhere:https://github.com/stianlagstad/chimeraviz/blob/master/DESCRIPTION#L42

Aaron Lun (13:32:07) (in thread): > Sounds like you should have aimportFrom(data.table, fread)somewhere in your NAMESPACE, then.

Stian Lågstad (13:43:02) (in thread): > Thanks! That led me to the solution, I think:slightly_smiling_face:

2018-09-20

JiefeiWang (12:44:35): > @JiefeiWang has joined the channel

Aaron Lun (06:37:36): > Just out of curiosity… is it possible to create a Rda file that performs a hostile action uponload? For example, deleting the contents of~/.

Sean Davis (07:11:13): > Definitely interested in hearing the answer to this.

Aaron Lun (09:10:06): > The very least one can do is to defineT <- FALSEandF <- TRUEto punish anyone who doesn’t follow the style guide.

Federico Marini (09:15:13): > oh, for that there was the evilR package

Federico Marini (09:15:20): > or at least I think that was the name

Aaron Lun (09:15:55): > Yeah, doing it from a package is relatively easy.

Federico Marini (09:16:45): > the T/F inversion is nothing compared to make itT <- sample(c(TRUE,FALSE),1)

Federico Marini (09:17:59): > uh, here’s the full list:

Federico Marini (09:18:00): > https://purrple.cat/blog/2017/05/28/turn-r-users-insane-with-evil/ - Attachment (purrple.cat): Turn R users insane with evil > purrple

Aaron Lun (09:23:16): > I remember seeing that. I have thought about setting up the T/F thing on my group’s R installation.

Aaron Lun (09:23:42): > … which would only trigger some of the time, leading to “it was failing just a second ago”.

Federico Marini (09:26:42): > We’d need an:evil_parrot:emoji here

Leonardo Collado Torres (10:33:33) (in thread): > hehe, if you find the gif we can add it

Federico Marini (10:34:37) (in thread): > will check:slightly_smiling_face:

Martin Morgan (14:56:39): > @Hervé Pagèsprovidesx = structure(list(), class="evil"); print.evil = function(x, ...) cat("Hello Hal\n"); save(x, print.evil, file = (fl <- tempfile()))and thenrm(x, print.evil)andload(fl); xso not quite ‘hostile on load’.

Nitesh Turaga (11:22:49): > braindr is actually very interesting!

2018-11-01

Aaron Lun (06:55:56): > Just got back from SCG 2018.

Aaron Lun (06:55:59): > Am absolutely busted.

Aaron Lun (06:56:21): > Haven’t slept for 24 hours. And I need to keep this up for another ~10 hours to get back on my regular sleep cycle.

Aaron Lun (06:57:10): > The take-home from the conference was that we need to really step up our#bigdata-repefforts.

Martin Morgan (08:12:09) (in thread): > Examples of what needs to be done?

Aaron Lun (08:37:10) (in thread): > Some sleep, hopefully.

Aaron Lun (08:37:28) (in thread): > Oh, you mean the big data stuff.

Aaron Lun (08:38:18) (in thread): > The immediate concern (as previously discussed with@Hervé Pagès) is to have%*%performant for DAs, ideally with sparse matrix-enabled efficiencies and parallelization.

Aaron Lun (08:47:37) (in thread): > I would also like to haveBiocSingulartested and used in as many sc-related packages as possible.

Aaron Lun (08:48:32) (in thread): > Finally, getting Rtsne to run as fast as possible - I have made some PRs but this needs more work.

Davide Risso (08:55:46) (in thread): > Do wereallyneed Rtsne?

Aaron Lun (08:56:50) (in thread): > Why? You want to UMAP?

Aaron Lun (08:57:13) (in thread): > Well, like it or not, there’s still a whole heap of people using t-SNEs (including me), so I’d like it faster rather than not.

Davide Risso (08:58:00) (in thread): > Why not? If it really is faster, and we just need a fancy way to plot cells… one method or the other seems the same to me

Davide Risso (08:58:51) (in thread): > Sure, I’m just saying that it’s esthetic and not really a cornerstone of anything we do

Aaron Lun (09:01:25) (in thread): > The whole field is about aesthetics.

Aaron Lun (09:01:42) (in thread): > And I want a t-SNE plot.

Davide Risso (09:02:21) (in thread): > Sure, not saying that we shouldn’t do it

Aaron Lun (09:02:22) (in thread): > UMAP is clearly different from t-SNE, so they aren’t swap-in replacements.

Davide Risso (09:03:15) (in thread): > I was just thinking that in terms of priorities %*% seems higher than tsne

Aaron Lun (09:04:02) (in thread): > Well, yes, that’s why I had it as number 3.

Aaron Lun (09:04:21) (in thread): > I’ve gotRtsneworking with neighbour inputs, so it’s plug and play with approximate searches inBiocNeighbors.

Aaron Lun (09:04:29) (in thread): > All that remains is to optimize the iterative steps.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (10:16:31): > Btw, HCA stream is live (started ~1h ago) here:http://web.mit.edu/webcast/hca/

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (10:17:19) (in thread): > Not sure if that’ll help you stay awake or just bore you to sleep@Aaron Lun

Aaron Lun (10:17:44): > :sleepy:

Aaron Lun (10:18:02): > I can barely stay awake as it is.

Aaron Lun (10:18:20): > On the plus side, I did talk to a few DCP people at SCG.

Aaron Lun (10:18:32): > Some of them from John’s “secret family”.

Stephanie Hicks (10:53:37): > @Kevin Rue-Albrechtcan you post updates on here if you think there is something relevant? I will be away from my laptop most of the afternoon today and tomorrow. Really bummed to miss it the stream.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:05:48): > Can do for another while. “Updates from the atlases” were a bit broad brush to my taste, they also didn’t have that much time, to be fair.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:43:55): > Sten Linnarsson on the Brain Atlas: function, development and disease right now

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (12:06:51): > perhaps most relevant now: “Data Coordination Platform Demo”

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (12:09:00): > pilot data: mouse/human, 10x/smartseq/mars/indrop, immune/…/other tissues

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (12:11:03): > 82k cells (82 projects)

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (12:18:31): > “Donwstream analysis, example: Jupyter notebooks”:sob:

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (12:20:28): > At least the interface seems to be fairly convenient to batch download either data matrix, BAM files, FASTQ files (and a few other data modalities)

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (12:24:25): > https://github.com/HumanCellAtlas/data-consumer-vignettes - Attachment (GitHub): HumanCellAtlas/data-consumer-vignettes > Simple walk-throughs of interacting with the DCP as a downstream data consumer. - HumanCellAtlas/data-consumer-vignettes

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (12:32:00): > Uberon sounding as a discussion favorite for ontology-related workhttps://www.ebi.ac.uk/ols/ontologies/uberon - Attachment (ebi.ac.uk): Uber-anatomy ontology > Uberon is an integrated cross-species anatomy ontology representing a variety of entities classified according to traditional anatomical criteria such as structure, function and developmental lineage. The ontology includes comprehensive relationships to taxon-specific anatomical ontologies, allowing integration of functional, phenotype and expression data.

Aaron Lun (12:37:03): > I wouldn’t be too disheartened about their heavy emphasis on Python, the CZI + DCP teams are very over-represented for compsci folks who do a lot of Python work, so a Jupyter notebooks is probably just what they’re most comfortable with.

Aaron Lun (12:37:19): > That said, we should make it easy for them to use R, and currently we haven’t delivered what we promised in the 1st RFA.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (12:37:21): > i was just teasing:slightly_smiling_face:

Martin Morgan (13:58:15) (in thread): > what do you see as the missed deliverables?

Raphael Gottardo (14:54:30): > I just talked to a few people within the DCP including Ambrose Carr at CZI. They are very supportive of R, and acknowledge that most tools are still in R. Their view is that Jupyter is cross platform, so R is supported.

Raphael Gottardo (14:55:35): > This being said, they will be providing a data API, and think we should simply plug that in in Bioconductor (Again they are really keen in us doing this).@Martin MorganI remember that this part of what you proposed to do. They suggested that we coordinate with@Marcus Kinsellato get early access to the beta, or whatever they can propose.

Raphael Gottardo (14:56:33): > They are looking for use cases, and I think we should create workflows that make use of the HCA data (ideally using the API).

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (14:58:52): > I’m personally curious about plugging an API like this intoiSEEfor immediate visualisation, like we did for ourExperimentHubdemo:grin:

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (15:03:19): > then, more ambitious workflow I imagine would be about clustering and annotating cell types/states, as mentioned among objectives of the atlases today

Marcus Kinsella (15:07:37): > can you describe what that interface would ideally look like for your tools?

Marcus Kinsella (15:11:15): > we have a fair bit of flexibility in the api design, but i’m much less familiar with how bioconductor expects to interact with remote data. several months ago, i think we had talked about some options, and i thought a lot of it came down to just putting some flavor of hdf5 file in s3

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (15:24:47): > I’m probably not the best placed to talk about big-data formats yet, but I’m just wondering about a potential package that would build queries emulating those in the DCP web interface, leading up to the download of a data matrix

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (15:25:02): > (.. accompanied with relevant metadata)

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (15:25:57): > as an R user, I’d imagine data set delivered as aSummarizedExperimentwithcolDataandrowDatafilled where possible

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (15:28:11): > I suppose the data could be cached on the client-side in hdf5 format, with theSummarizedExperimentslots pointing to the relevant matrices

Aaron Lun (15:56:10) (in thread): > The big one for me is the counts-to-clusters of the 1.3 mil data set. This could be broken up into various smaller missed targets, of which the immediate sticking point is what I discussed above.

Aaron Lun (15:57:42) (in thread): > I also haven’t heard anything related to the other aims.

Aaron Lun (16:03:47): > I suspect@Vince Careywould have the most experience playing with remote data stores, but IMO, all I would really want is an API that gives me the requested submatrix of expression values, e.g., as a JSON or binary. Then we would wrap it in a matrix class on our end and we’re away.

Aaron Lun (16:06:01): > I don’t do a lot of network stuff, but I could imagine submitting a POST request from any language (I wonder if I could even do it from C++?) and getting a JSON in return. Or any other standard data format. This could be pretty efficient if sparsity is exploited and recognised on both ends.

Sean Davis (16:25:15): > You might all take a look at restfulSE.http://bioconductor.org/packages/restfulSE/ - Attachment (Bioconductor): restfulSE > This package provides functions and classes to interface with remote data stores by operating on SummarizedExperiment-like objects.

Sean Davis (16:26:51): > For folks thinking about hosting, this is the HDF5 cloud hosting solution:https://www.hdfgroup.org/solutions/hdf-kita/hdf-kita-overview/

Vince Carey (16:28:45): > What do we know about the DCP API? We could use code in restfulSE or rhdf5client to generate DelayedArray objects that compose the RESTful queries based on subscripting operations.

Aaron Lun (16:50:24): > Would it make sense to have aRestfulMatrixinstead? This would plug in more easily with our S4 matrix framework. I can’t see anything special about the SE for data transfer - surely 1D col/rowData would be so cheap we could just grab those in one go.

Aaron Lun (16:51:04): > And I guess I started this, but we should take this conversation back to#bigdata-rep

Marcus Kinsella (16:56:55): > (happy to move this out of#randomif that would be helpful) > presently the API to expression matrices is very simple, you POST a request with a bunch of IDs that refer to “bundles” in the DCP, and it gives you a s3 bucket and prefix for a file or set of files. this satisfies the simplest “data browser” use case, where someone is looking at the website and wants to download an expression matrix based on what they see

Marcus Kinsella (16:58:34): > for scanpy, we can output the matrices in “zarr” format, which has some nice parallelism properties and i think achieves some of the things that delayedarray does in bioconductor

Sean Davis (16:59:21): > I thinkDelayedArraymight be close,@Aaron Lun?

Marcus Kinsella (17:01:37): > i guess there are two parts: the batching of operations, which scanpy does not do, though i suspect you could get it to work with dask, and efficient partial i/o of remote data, which is what they’ve made progress on

Aaron Lun (18:11:08): > What’s the granularity of these bundles? I’m not familiar with the format, but it seems natural to have bundles split by cell (i.e., each file is a collection of column vectors). They might be analogous to chunks in file-backed storage, in which case@Hervé Pagèsalready has a lot of infrasturcutre in place to handle it.

Marcus Kinsella (18:11:59): > it’s pretty much all over the place. for smartseq2 every bundle is one cell, for 10x a bundle may have many thousands of cells

Aaron Lun (18:12:16): > Oh. 1 cell! That’s… interesting.

Marcus Kinsella (18:12:28): > yes it’s very interesting

Aaron Lun (18:12:31): > Okay, but by and large, each bundle contains expression values for all genes for 1 (or many) cells.

Marcus Kinsella (18:12:55): > yeah plus a bunch of metadata

Aaron Lun (18:13:00): > Right.

Aaron Lun (18:13:51): > This might be fatigue speaking (haven’t slept for 36 hours since I woke up in Boston) but it all sounds very feasible with our existing infrastructure.

Marcus Kinsella (18:14:25): > i’m very interested in how that would work

Aaron Lun (18:14:43): > I suppose there is documentation floating around somewhere for the API? Certainly the DCP slack channel makes enough noise.

Marcus Kinsella (18:14:59): > i assume we would needsomeadaptation of the existing storage system API, but if it’s not too major that would be great

Marcus Kinsella (18:15:17): > well there are multiple APIs

Aaron Lun (18:15:43): > I was thinking of the expression matrix API you mentioned earlier, with POST requests for bundles.

Aaron Lun (18:16:27): > @Vince Carey, how much work would it be to roll outRestfulMatrixor some such thing?

Marcus Kinsella (18:16:36): > https://github.com/HumanCellAtlas/data-store/blob/master/dss-api.ymlandhttps://github.com/HumanCellAtlas/matrix-service/blob/master/config/matrix-api.yml - Attachment (GitHub): HumanCellAtlas/data-store > Design specs and prototypes for the HCA Data Storage System (“blue box”) - HumanCellAtlas/data-store - Attachment (GitHub): HumanCellAtlas/matrix-service > Contribute to HumanCellAtlas/matrix-service development by creating an account on GitHub.

Marcus Kinsella (18:17:55): > you should think of the matrix API as very much a WIP, it served its first matrices a few days ago. the data store API has had some time to settle

Aaron Lun (18:22:47): > Okay. Ah… reading these docs reminds me how long it’s been since I dealt with anything network-y.

Aaron Lun (18:26:10): > Taking this convo back to#bigdata-rep.

Raphael Gottardo (21:14:16) (in thread): > The 1-cell thing through me off. Because in that case, it’s not really a matrix@Marcus KinsellaI don’t really see why cells from a single experiment wouldn’t be in a matrix even if done on Smart-Seq2.

Marcus Kinsella (22:36:07) (in thread): > so there are a few competing concerns with how data is organized in the HCA data store: ingestion and upload, metadata structure, pipeline efficiency, enabling normalization, etc

Marcus Kinsella (22:36:39) (in thread): > so things won’t often be optimized for downstream data consumers, hence the desire to build special services and interfaces like the matrix service

Marcus Kinsella (22:37:30) (in thread): > now for SS2, tbh i feel like the competing concerns mostly argue for bundles that correspond to at least a plate, and i think that decision is going to be revisited now that this demo is done

Marcus Kinsella (22:37:36) (in thread): > but the general problem persists

Raphael Gottardo (14:30:29) (in thread): > Makes sense to me, a plate would be good.

Vladimir Kiselev (06:00:49): > Thanks@Sean Davis, is there anything similar for single cell RNAseq?

Sean Davis (07:49:13): > Take a look athttps://github.com/seandavi/awesome-single-cell? - Attachment (GitHub): seandavi/awesome-single-cell > List of software packages for single-cell data analysis, including RNA-seq, ATAC-seq, etc. - seandavi/awesome-single-cell

Sean Davis (07:50:08): > And the slicker, but more focused:https://www.scrna-tools.org/ - Attachment (scrna-tools.org): scRNA-tools > A catalogue of single-cell RNA-sequencing analysis tools

Sean Davis (07:54:37): > Additions via pull request to awesome-single-cell are encouraged and welcome. Note that the repo is not limited to lists of software and includes speaker suggestions, data resources, web applications, and benchmark and comparison links. Material gets added based on contribution, so if you see something missing or have a few favorite go-tos, make your mark with a pull request.

Sean Davis (14:56:45): > NCI CBIIT Speaker Series > Speakers: Rob Smith, Ph.D. > Date: December 19, 2018 > Time: 11:00 a.m. ET > Topic: Informatics for Computational Mass Spectrometry > In this presentation, Dr. Rob Smith will describe the work he and his lab are doing to progress the state of Informatics for Computational Mass Spectrometry to further proteomic research. > > Dr. Rob Smith will be presenting remotely. Please join us via WebEx and register now to attend the presentation. You may also join us in person at the NCI Shady Grove Campus, Rm. 2W032-34. > About the NCI CBIIT Speaker Serieshttps://wiki.nci.nih.gov/display/CBIITSpeakers/2018/11/02/Dec+19%2C+Rob+Smith%2C+Informatics+for+Computational+Mass+Spectrometry

Sean Davis (11:22:45): > Might be of interest to a few: Jeffrey Farrell, Ph.D. > Harvard University > Spatial and temporal reconstruction of embryogenesis using single-cell RNAseq > > on Tuesday January 15th at 10:00 a.m. in Frederick, Building 549, Auditorium > > Dr. Farrell’s research focuses on the recognition that single-cell RNA > sequencing (scRNAseq) can provide a new view of development that > uses all genes to find a global context for previous observations and to > uncover new aspects of embryogenesis. > > This seminar will also be available via WebEx. Please see below for information on the WebEx session. > > WebEx Details: > > JOIN WEBEX MEETINGhttps://cbiit.webex.com/cbiit/j.php?MTID=m1d2b194d5b22edb106fcc4c4313946caMeeting number (access code): 739 249 035 Meeting password: Stadtman011519! > > JOIN FROM A VIDEO SYSTEM OR APPLICATION > Dial sip:739249035@cbiit.webex.com > You can also dial 173.243.2.68 and enter your meeting number. > > JOIN BY PHONE > 1-650-479-3207 Call-in toll number (US/Canada) Tap here to call (mobile phones only, hosts not supported): tel:%2B1-650-479-3207,,01739249035%23%2301

Tim Triche (07:50:54) (in thread): > this could have been lifted word-for-word from my experience teaching a Python for Bioinformatics course at USC

Tim Triche (07:51:41) (in thread): > we decided to switch to iPython notebooks in the second lecture, and covered Ask Uncle Google in the first

Tim Triche (07:52:29) (in thread): > at one point I used the matrix exponential as an example of checking that libraries are doing what you expect, and that floating point isn’t quietly corrupting key steps

Tim Triche (07:53:07) (in thread): > the more things change… ? Thanks for sharing that link, I feel less bad now

Sowmya S Manian (10:31:12): > Hi Everyone, > > Package to do quality control on FASTQ File?

Federico Marini (10:34:26): > FastQC - although it is not a Bioc package?

Martin Morgan (10:35:00): > https://bioconductor.org/packages/ShortReadfor the die-hards - Attachment (Bioconductor): ShortRead > This package implements sampling, iteration, and input of FASTQ files. The package includes functions for filtering and trimming reads, and for generating a quality assessment report. Data are represented as DNAStringSet-derived objects, and easily manipulated for a diversity of purposes. The package also contains legacy support for early single-end, ungapped alignment formats.

Federico Marini (10:35:16): > or Rqc to wrap Martin’s proposal?

Federico Marini (10:35:57): > then on all the samples a one call to MultiQC and you should be served:wink:

Sowmya S Manian (11:22:37): > Thanks all of you. I am familiar with Rqc and also the FASTQC tool > I just wanted to see what all options we have from BioConductor.

Sowmya S Manian (11:22:55): > I will check ShortRead as well

Federico Marini (11:23:25): > Probably overkilling:http://bioconductor.org/packages/release/BiocViews.html#___QualityControl

Federico Marini (11:23:47): > Maybe also useful when writing up a report:http://bioconductor.org/packages/release/BiocViews.html#___ReportWriting

Sowmya S Manian (11:52:21): > @Federico MariniThanks, that helps for the overview I wanted.:+1:

Martin Morgan (12:53:26): > Also QuasRhttp://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/vignettes/QuasR/inst/doc/QuasR.html#612_create_a_quality_control_report

Tim Triche (13:35:57) (in thread): > multiQC usually, since one run by itself is not often of as much interest as several

Sowmya S Manian (13:49:28): > Have usedseqTools, checkingFastqCleanerGoing to use nowBioStringsandShortReadson fastq file. > Will also checkQuasRI want to do Duplicate removal

Sowmya S Manian (13:49:47): > Adapter Trimmer

2019-01-11

Stevie Pederson (01:45:49): > Hi@Sowmya S Manian. We’re aiming to submit this to BioC in the next few weeks but this package can load pre-existing FastQC reports:https://github.com/UofABioinformaticsHub/ngsReports

Sowmya S Manian (01:50:14): > Thanks@Stevie PedersonI’ll check out this as well:+1:

Leandro Roser (07:00:51): > @Leandro Roser has joined the channel

Diya Das (15:33:44): > Does anyone have experience sorting out C++ compilers for installing R packages on computing clusters? I’m specifically using PSC Bridges, and so far the help desk’s recommendation has been to downgrade R or not use BiocManager:grimacing:I’ve found the issue also persists with install.packages, but I have the vague idea that I should edit a makevars file? (it’s the -openmp flag it doesn’t like, because icpc deprecated that in favor of -qopenmp) I have no experience with C++ so I’d really appreciate some guidance.

Avi Srivastava (15:44:55): > if it’s a standard package, anaconda may be a quick work around:stuck_out_tongue_winking_eye:

Kasper D. Hansen (16:42:48): > Provided you have the right compilers, I would compile R from source

Sean Davis (17:01:04): > Or use a singularity-wrapped R.

Diya Das (17:07:09): > It turns out that a Makevars file with CXXFLAGS, CFLAGS, FFLAGS and FCFLAGS all set to -qopenmp appears to work…thanks for the suggestions though.

Diya Das (19:17:05) (in thread): > I ran this by someone who writes a lot about Rcpp and he said that you could also use the configure.args argument of install.packages, for reproducibility reasons.

2019-01-30

2019-02-03

Sean Davis (08:49:31): > Artificial Intelligence for Data Discovery and Reuse: > An NSF-supported conference aiming to find innovative solutions to accelerate the dissemination and reuse of scientific data in the data revolution. > Submission deadline Feb 22. > Conference May 13-15, 2019 at Carnegie Mellon University.events.library.cmu.edu/aidr2019/Supported by the NSF scientific data reuse initiative, AIDR (Artificial Intelligence for Data Discovery and Reuse) 2019 is a conference aiming to find innovative solutions to accelerate the dissemination and reuse of scientific data in the data revolution. The explosion in the volume of scientific data has made it increasingly challenging to find data scattered across various platforms. At the same time, increasing numbers of new data formats, greater data complexity, lack of consistent data standards across disciplines, metadata or links between data and publications makes it even more challenging to evaluate data quality, reproduce results, and reuse data for new discoveries. AIDR 2019 provides a platform for AI/ML researchers, data professionals, and scientists to come together and benefit from mutual expertise to address these data challenges and to facilitate the next breakthroughs in science and technology using the power of AI and scientific data.

Michael Lawrence (21:45:19): > I have to admit that the frequency of Slack activity is difficult to manage. Activity seems to come only in spurts, so actively checking for updates is not worth the time. The automatic notification settings aren’t very flexible: notify on mention/keyword or on every message. I guess on every message is the best compromise?

Martin Morgan (09:12:05) (in thread): > In the slack app and for this channel I set the ‘Sidebar’ preference to show unread and starred messages, and subscribed to a relatively large number of channels. Sean Davis (20:14:45): > https://seandavi.github.io/2019/06/omicidx-on-bigquery/ - Attachment (seandavi(s12)): OmicIDX on BigQuery | seandavi(s12) > OmicIDX is a project to democratize access to omics metadata. As the sizes of omics repositories have grown into the millions of available samples, thinking of the metadata themselves as Big Data seems reasonable. Additionally, by making the metadata more fit-for-use for text mining, natural language processing, ingestion into machine learning or search engines, OmicIDX aims to facilitate augmentation and analysis of these metadata. In practice, the OmicIDX mines data from the NCBI Sequence Read Archive (SRA) and NCBI Biosample databases (updated daily).

Matt Brauer (15:30:23): > How to support open-source software and stay sanehttps://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-019-02046-0 > > On 10 April, astrophysicists announced that they had captured the first ever image of a black hole. This was exhilarating news, but none of the giddy headlines mentioned that the image would have been impossible without open-source software. The image was created using Matplotlib, a Python library for graphing data, as well as other components of the open-source Python ecosystem.Just five days later, the US National Science Foundation (NSF) rejected a grant proposal to support that ecosystem, saying that the software lacked sufficient impact. - Attachment (nature.com): How to support open-source software and stay sane > Releasing lab-built open-source software often involves a mountain of unforeseen work for the developers.

Dario Strbenac (19:59:00): > Some sobering reading in Genome Biology for anyone doing research about variants in genomes titledIs it Time to Change the Reference Genome?https://genomebiology.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13059-019-1774-4It gives a good overview of what a reference genome is and how it is used in genomics research and what the pitfalls are. Of particular note, > > The Reference Genome is Not a Baseline > > Of the 20 donors the reference genome was meant to sample from, 70% of the sequence was obtained from a single sample, ‘RPC-11’, from an individual who had a high risk for diabetes. The remaining 30% is split 23% from 10 samples and 7% from over 50 sources. In practice, the current reference genome can be considered a well-defined and well-assembled haploid personal genome. This means, however, that the reference genome does not represent a default genome any more than any other arbitrarily chosen personal genome would.

Aaron Lun (19:59:35): > Are they taking volunteers?

Aaron Lun (20:00:12): > I’ll put my hand up to be hg39.

Dario Strbenac (20:15:23): > Hopefully, that’ll be a graph genome.

Aaron Lun (20:16:40) (in thread): > Not sure I have enough allelic heterogeneity in my body for that.

2019-08-10

Kasper D. Hansen (14:15:55): > No, hopefully it willnotbe a graph genome

Kasper D. Hansen (14:18:07): > There will not be a single graph genome which satisfies all use cases. What we need are high-quality individual genomes and then tools for making graphs out of select subsets. So that each analysis can be tailored in whatever way you want

Kasper D. Hansen (14:18:23): > The idea of a single graph genome is the idea of a single size fits all.

Kasper D. Hansen (14:19:05): > It is an ill-defined goal (what is a good graph genome, what degrees of variation should be contained in it). Whereas the constructing of high-quality individual genomes is a well defined scientific task

2019-08-14

Aedin Culhane (14:40:42): > The Vienna > Graduate School of Population Genetics is now accepting > > applications > for the course “Experimental evolution: bringing theory and > practice together” > at the University of Veterinary Medicine in Vienna, December > 2-6, 2019. > > Experimental > evolution is extensively used to address questions of > > evolutionary > biology by exposing evolving populations to different > > environmental > conditions. It offers the opportunity to replicate > > experiments > and test for convergent outcomes. With the recent drop in > > DNA sequencing > cost and the advance of sequencing technologies, the > > combination > of experimental evolution with next generation sequencing > > of pools of > individuals (Evolve & Resequence) has become a > > state-of-the-art > method to link phenotypic responses to genetic > > changes. Although > the availability of replicated time series data is > > one key advantage > of E&R, the analysis of such data sets is still in > > its infancy. > This course will introduce the participants to several > > new approaches > for the analysis of genomic time series data covering > > the latest > software tools as well as required statistical and > > computational > skills. > > The course > is targeted towards researchers interested in experimental > > evolution combined > with NGS and will cover the design of experimental > > evolution studies > as well as the analysis of Pool-Seq time series > > data. > > The course > aims to introduce participants to: > > > > State-of-the-art software packages > > > > Modeling of neutral data > > > > Identifying selected regions > > > > Comparison of n-point analysis with time series data > > > > Haplotypes reconstruction > from time-series data > > > > Inferring linkage structure of Pool-Seq data > > > > Estimating selection coefficients > > > > Calling transposable elements in time series data > > > > Inferring selection in transcriptomics data > > > > Morning lectures > by internationally renowned faculty will be followed > > by computer > practicals on the analysis of experimental evolution data > > in the afternoon. > > Faculty: > > Anthony Long > (California Irvine Univ., US) - > confirmed > > Christian Schlötterer > (Vetmeduni Vienna, A) - > confirmed > > Dmitri Petrov > (Stanford Univ., US) - > waiting for confirmation > > Henrique Teotónio > (IBENS, FR) - > confirmed > > Molly Burke > (Oregon State Univ., US) - > confirmed > > Neda Barghi > (Vetmeduni Vienna, A) - > confirmed > > Robert Kofler > (Vetmeduni Vienna, A) - > confirmed > > Susan Bailey > (Clarkson Univ., US) - > confirmed > > The course > is free > but will be restricted for the practical sessions (hands on computer lab). > > Additional > seats will be available for attending the lectures, only. > > Students and > researchers are invited to apply by submitting a single > > .pdf file containing > 1) a short CV, 2) a motivation letter and a > > statement of > computer skills topopgen.vienna@gmail.comby September 30, > 2019. > Confidence in working with Unix command line as well as in R, is strongly > recommended for the practicals. Participants are expected to > arrange their own accommodation. > > Further information > and updates available at:https://www.popgen-vienna.at/training/experimental-evolution-course/Best > regards, > > PhD students > of the Vienna Graduate School of Population Genetics - Attachment (popgen vienna - Vienna Graduate School of Population Genetics): Experimental Evolution Course - popgen vienna - Vienna Graduate School of Population Genetics

Dario Strbenac (20:00:05): > For your bemusement, a reviewer for Nature Communications asked for short variant calling to be done in the MHC region on chromosome 6 to identify indels which would cause a frameshift mutation in HLA genes. I recommended my collaborator to quote fromhttps://genomebiology.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13059-019-1774-4in the rebuttal, particularly the section titled The Reference Genome is Not a Baseline but they asked “can we do indel calling” anyway:face_palm:It’s estimated that there are 8 million to 9 million HLA alleles in the human populationhttps://journals.plos.org/plosgenetics/article?id=10.1371/journal.pgen.1006862It seems that Kevin’s efforts to inform the genomics research community about how to use the reference genome meaningfully are in vain.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (12:30:10): > I’m about to dig intoGenomicRangesagain but just in case someone has a smarter answer: > How do I chase down the definition ofuniqueforGRangesbeyond the following > > > showMethods("unique") > Function: unique (package BiocGenerics) > x="ANY" > x="character" > (inherited from: x="ANY") > x="CompressedList" > x="DataTable" > x="DelayedArray" > x="factor" > (inherited from: x="ANY") > x="List" > x="Rle" > x="RleList" > x="SimpleList" > x="Vector" > > showMethods("unique", classes = "ANY", includeDefs = T) > Function: unique (package BiocGenerics) > x="ANY" > function (x, incomparables = FALSE, ...) > UseMethod("unique") > > I can’t remember how to find the code for something that’s called usingUseMethod("unique")

Aaron Lun (12:31:07): > getMethod("unique", "GRanges")?

Aaron Lun (12:31:24): > Well, it would probably bounce all the way back down to theVectormethod.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (12:34:58): > oh stupid me in fact, I ran into that one when working onunisets.uniqueis basically defined as!duplicatedfor most things out there. So it’s rarely re-implemented, as developers just need to defineduplicatedand they generally getuniquefor free

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (12:35:41): > See howduplicatedhas a definition forGenomicRanges. That’s all I was after > > > showMethods("duplicated") > Function: duplicated (package BiocGenerics) > x="ANY" > x="CompressedAtomicList" > x="CompressedList" > x="DataTable" > x="Dups" > x="GenomicRanges" > x="GRanges" > (inherited from: x="GenomicRanges") > x="List" > x="PDict" > x="PreprocessedTB" > x="RangedSummarizedExperiment" > x="Rle" > x="Vector" > x="XRawList" >

Aaron Lun (12:36:39): > Read through?pcomparefor details on what you actually need to define for your own subclass.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (12:40:31): > Thanks. That’s probably the more elegant solution to me reading it as > > duplicatedIntegerQuads(as.factor(seqnames(x)), as.factor(strand(x)), > start(x), width(x), fromLast = fromLast, method = method) > > Also gave me the chance to spot a typo in the man page :P > > are considered equal iff they are

Aaron Lun (12:48:21): > NO, that’s right.

Aaron Lun (12:48:23): > if and only if.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (12:53:27): > damn i’ve been out of school for too longhttps://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/If_and_only_if - Attachment: If and only if > In logic and related fields such as mathematics and philosophy, if and only if (shortened iff) is a biconditional logical connective between statements, where either both statements are true or both are false. > The connective is biconditional (a statement of material equivalence), and can be likened to the standard material conditional (“only if”, equal to “if … then”) combined with its reverse (“if”); hence the name. The result is that the truth of either one of the connected statements requires the truth of the other (i.e. either both statements are true, or both are false). It is controversial whether the connective thus defined is properly rendered by the English “if and only if”, with its pre-existing meaning. > In writing, phrases commonly used as alternatives to P “if and only if” Q include: Q is necessary and sufficient for P, P is equivalent (or materially equivalent) to Q (compare material implication), P precisely if Q, P precisely (or exactly) when Q, P exactly in case Q, and P just in case Q. Some authors regard “iff” as unsuitable in formal writing; others consider it a “borderline case” and tolerate its use.In logical formulae, logical symbols are used instead of these phrases; see the discussion of notation.

Princy Parsana (08:47:16): > @Princy Parsana has joined the channel

mirna (18:43:38): > Hi. I have a statistical question, and I think maybe someone here can help me. I am looking for some suggestion on how to analyze transcriptomic data. The idea is to elucidate, which are the genes that participate in the morphogenesis of a holometabolous insect. For the design of this experiment, we produced RNA-seq libraries in each of the stages of development, egg, larva, pupa, and adult. In other RNA-seq experiments, I have contrasted the treatment samples against control in DESeq2. In this case, as I do not have a control condition, I am not quite sure of which contrasts could be appropriate. I could contrast each stage against all others, and thus, identify the genes that have differential expression in at least one condition. However, is this the proper way of doing it? If you have any advice or suggestions, I would greatly appreciate it. Regards.

Kasper D. Hansen (19:56:16): > You can have a factor with levels which are egg, larva etc. and then you can test if the factor is significant

Kasper D. Hansen (19:56:43): > Then you identify genes which do not have the same expression level across all developmental stages

Kasper D. Hansen (19:56:50): > However, that is bound to be a lot

Kasper D. Hansen (19:57:27): > Also, due to the developmental trajectory, it might not make sense to compare egg to adult, but only egg to larva and chain together a list of parwise comparisons

Jared Andrews (11:11:41) (in thread): > Any reason not to just use github pages? With a custom domain if you so choose? Otherwise, I can recommend Bluehost as being decent, especially if you go the wordpress route. Stay away from iPage - it is probably the cheapest option out there, and it quickly becomes apparent as to why.

Dario Strbenac (02:00:11) (in thread): > Will it be as exciting ashttp://madhadron.com/posts/2012-03-26-a-farewell-to-bioinformatics.html?

Mikhael Manurung (06:40:55) (in thread): > Thanks for your reply. > > If I understand correctly, you are using the reference samples as additional QC measures, right? However, you did not use it in any way to ‘adjust’ your measurements in order to remove batch effect. I’ve seen cases where a separate cluster could form due to dirty sensor that greatly reduce the machine’s sensitivity. It would be nice if there are tools to adjust for this instrument variation.

Martin Morgan (09:12:29) (in thread): > This Biostars posthttps://www.biostars.org/p/96951/talks about creating a chain file that can be used by liftOver (including inhttps://bioconductor.org/packages/rtracklayer) to map between sequences. The approach might be dated these days? - Attachment (Bioconductor): rtracklayer > Extensible framework for interacting with multiple genome browsers (currently UCSC built-in) and manipulating annotation tracks in various formats (currently GFF, BED, bedGraph, BED15, WIG, BigWig and 2bit built-in). The user may export/import tracks to/from the supported browsers, as well as query and modify the browser state, such as the current viewport.

Jared Andrews (00:01:25): > I do most R stuff in jupyter lab now. Mostly just because the ability to have multiple kernels in the same document works better out of the box than it seems to with R markdown docs. Definitely so on Windows. Still do all package dev in R Studio though, inspector is too valuable and I’ve yet to find a great one for Jupyter yet.

Hervé Pagès (01:10:34): > @Aaron LunCan’t believe people are still using vim. Feels good to know I’m not the only one and that it rhymes with WIN. LOL!

Aaron Lun (00:23:18): > Man, I was promised a flame war about DataFrames and tibbles and data.frames, but instead I look on#generaland what do I get?

Aaron Lun (00:23:41): > Polite, civil discussion with patient explanations and consensus reached from different viewpoints.

Aaron Lun (00:23:57): > Disgusting. Not even any ad hominem attacks.

Aaron Lun (00:24:54): > We just can’t have that, so let’s get a real flame war started right here.

Aaron Lun (00:25:21): > So, what about those C programmers, right? Macros… I mean, seriously.

Aaron Lun (00:26:12): > Now, C++, that’s a real programming language.

hcorrada (06:01:08): > Have you used rust?

Hervé Pagès (13:01:53): > Yes Andrew Gallant (aka BurntSushi) implemented ripgrep in Rust and he claims that ripgrep is “faster than anything else” thanks to Rust’s regex engine (see@Kevin Rue-Albrecht’s link above). That also made me curious about Rust. According to a recent Stack Overflow survey it’s also the most loved programming language.

Aaron Lun (13:03:42): > > According to a recent Stack Overflow survey it’s also the most loved programming language. > Well, I’m sure someone also said that about Java at some point in history.

Hervé Pagès (13:04:33): > or about C++:grin:

Aaron Lun (13:10:42): > I’m not sure I would ever say I love C++.

Aaron Lun (13:10:57): > Like a long marriage, I would say that it’s more of a grudging acceptance.

Kasper D. Hansen (13:22:57): > @Aaron Lun’s acquaintances need to spend more time on the internet, if they consider that a flame war

Hervé Pagès (13:27:57): > e.g. they could start by checking this onehttp://harmful.cat-v.org/software/c++/linus

Aaron Lun (16:07:12): > I mean, with all the macros in C, you might as well be “programming” in Excel.

Hervé Pagès (16:35:41): > ha ha, nice try

Artem Sokolov (16:45:47): > Linus Torvalds must be fun at parties.

Artem Sokolov (16:46:16): > (There’s your ad hominem,@Aaron Lun)

Rob Patro (21:46:21): > Rust is pretty awesome … from the PL geek perspective. Also, the compiler is amazing and the error messages make me realize how behind-the-times modern C++ compilers are with respect to interfacing with the developer.

sangsookim (22:31:55): > @sangsookim has joined the channel

Alan O’C (03:36:59): > > Well, I’m sure someone also said that about Java at some point in history. > I think the only people who truly love Java are middle managers. Although it’s at least less likely to end up with genuinely bad code (race conditions, memory leaks) than C++

hcorrada (06:54:39) (in thread): > It said ‘unsafe’ right there on the source!

Rob Patro (09:02:24) (in thread): > :rolling_on_the_floor_laughing:

Sean Davis (12:21:49) (in thread): > I’ve recently tried my hand at Rust as well and found it remarkably nice to work with. I only scratched the surface, of course, but the experience was enough to allay fears of perhaps using it for something useful down the road.

2020-01-30

Aaron Lun (03:10:31): > Kids these days, sheesh, don’t even have to manage their own memory.

Aaron Lun (03:10:53): > Can one even call themselves a programmer if they don’t have to debug segfaults?

2020-02-09

Dario Strbenac (22:35:23): > Can anyone recommend a good way to use normal/tumour RNA sequencing data to filter somatic variant calls from normal/tumour DNA sequencing data and also, I suppose, from germline calls? Or describe the frustration you had before giving up? If you don’t make use of RNA data to filter, you label every variant like Missense, Nonsense, etc. even though only a subset of genes are expressed in each tissue type (e.g. you’re analysing melanoma but you label variants in heart-specific genes as Missense). But, using RNA data, you could filter out important variants such as Nonsense, that are degraded by Nonsense Mediated Decay (I’ve seen a couple of examples in TP53 where the RNA has 0 reads containing the SNV), because the absence of reads with the SNV is support for its presence and action, which is the opposite of categories such as Missense. Then, there is the issue of whether the RNA sequencing is deep enough or undersampled, which is not so important for identifying the most differentially expressed genes, which is the main use case of the data set, but important for SNV calling.

Stuart Lee (22:52:30) (in thread): > @Anna Quaglierimight be helpful here, she has a preprint on RNA-seq variant callinghttps://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/2019.12.18.881870v2.full

2020-02-10

Ariel Hippen (14:21:40): > @Ariel Hippen has joined the channel

Sofie Dam Nielsen (16:44:38): > @Sofie Dam Nielsen has joined the channel

Dario Strbenac (20:00:06) (in thread): > Thanks, but I have a slight twist in my case. The collaborators are also wanting to detect Stop Gained mutations, which have a SNP in DNA data but not in RNA data because of nonsense-mediated decay degrading those transcripts and them not being RNA sequenced. It seems weird that it would biologically exist (how can the cell tell when a transcript has a premature stop codon compared to a normal position stop codon? The cell doesn’t use ENSEMBL Variant Effect Predictor like I do!), but I have seen a couple of examples with IGV in TP53 and everyone gets mad when deleterious TP53 variants aren’t reported to them. Also, this data is total RNA-seq, which catches a broader variety of transcripts (I bet you never heard of RN7SL1 / RN7SL2 genes; one sample has 22 million reads out of 44 million total originating from it:face_with_rolling_eyes:Compositional data analysis, eh?) and a lot of intronic reads. It’s a good PhD topic for her and I hope she enjoys it and finds some good solutions for the research community!

2020-02-11

Julien Roux (03:38:43): > @Julien Roux has joined the channel

Florian Geier (03:43:36): > @Florian Geier has joined the channel

Theresa Alexander (15:29:12): > @Theresa Alexander has joined the channel

Sean Davis (15:33:09): > Text Mining and Natural Language Processing SIG presents: >   > Speaker:****  Lars Juhl JensenDate:    February 13, 2020, at 11am-12pmLocation:**** Building 38A Visitor Center, 1st FloorWebEx:           WebEx information is provided belowMedical data and text mining: Linking diseases, drugs, and adverse reactions*****Abstract: > Clinical data describing the phenotypes and treatment of patients is an underused data source that has much greater research potential than is currently realized. Mining of electronic health records (EHRs) has the potential for revealing unknown disease correlations and for improving post-approval monitoring of drugs for adverse drug reactions. >   > In my presentation I will introduce the centralized Danish health registries and show how we use them for identification of temporal disease correlations and discovery of common diagnosis trajectories of patients. I will also describe how we use text mining of the clinical narrative from Danish electronic health records and use this for identification of new adverse reactions of drugs and drug combinations from data on 1.5 million inpatients. >   > The same text-mining tools are also used in the STRING protein interaction database and related databases, which will be the topic of a separate presentation at NIH on April 28th.About:Lars Juhl Jensen started his research career in Søren Brunak’s group at the Technical University of Denmark (DTU), from where he in 2002 received the Ph.D. degree in bioinformatics for his work on non-homology-based protein function prediction. During this time, he also developed methods for visualization of microbial genomes, pattern recognition in promoter regions, and microarray analysis. From 2003 to 2008, he was at the European Molecular Biology Laboratory (EMBL) where he worked on literature mining, integration of large-scale experimental datasets, and analysis of biological interaction networks. Since 2009, he has continued this line of research as a professor at the Novo Nordisk Foundation Center for Protein Research at the Panum Institute in Copenhagen and as a founder, owner and scientific advisor of Intomics A/S. He is a co-author of more than 200 scientific publications that have in total received more than 30,000 citations. He was awarded the Lundbeck Foundation Talent Prize in 2003, his work on cell-cycle research was named “Break-through of the Year” in 2006 by the magazine Ingeniøren, his work on text mining won the first prize in the “Elsevier Grand Challenge: Knowledge Enhancement in the Life Sciences” in 2009, and he was awarded the Lundbeck Foundation Prize for Young Scientists in 2010.Join WebEx meeting***Meeting Link :https://nih.webex.com/nih/j.php?MTID=m1308efb6cd07a00721c8624b49667f59Meeting Number (access code) : 621 918 729 >   > Meeting Password : YQxA7sAa76Q

FelixErnst (13:33:15): > Pokemon. Thats a healthy list of names I guess. Right’s issues aside: Silmarillion characters?

Federico Marini (14:51:33): > Tokay and malbec are… wine sorts?

Federico Marini (14:51:50): > Merida is not AFAIK, so there is another pattern

Federico Marini (14:52:07): > What about coffee pure sorts - mono-origin ones?

Federico Marini (14:52:57): > https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_coffee_varieties - Attachment: List of coffee varieties > Coffee varieties are the diverse subspecies derived through selective breeding or natural selection of coffee plants. While there is tremendous variability encountered in both wild and cultivated coffee plants, there are a few varieties and cultivars that are commercially important due to various unique and inherent traits such as disease resistance and fruit yield. These unique traits are what producers use to select breeds when developing crops. Therefore, at a micro level, breed selection is critical to the success of a producer and is one of the key components of cup quality.At a macro level, the viability of the coffee industry as a whole is dependent upon breed selection. Already, the majority of coffee produced originates from producers using selected breeds. For this reason, breed selection is an important aspect of sustainability within coffee production.
