2021-01-04

Lluís Revilla (03:03:58): > Why does Bioconductor exists as separate repository than CRAN?https://twitter.com/ozjimbob/status/1345947982210818052 - Attachment (twitter): Attachment > Hey #rstats folks, why does BioConductor exist? It’s super helpful that there is just the occasional random package with one useful function that isn’t on CRAN but is just…somewhere else instead.

Jared Andrews (08:44:32): > Different standards, different review process, different goals.

Hervé Pagès (12:58:36): > also: 6-month release cycles + rapid push/build/propagate mechanism for the devel branch + an overall size of 140GB (mostly due to some really big data packages) that wouldn’t be compatible with CRAN policy

Aaron Lun (13:00:13): > some developer-level sentiments athttps://github.com/Bioconductor/MatrixGenerics/issues/21

Michael Lawrence (15:09:04): > It’s also worth pointing out that Bioc is a lot more than a repository, contrary to what that tweet implies.

2021-01-05

Ralf Weber (06:10:55): > @Ralf Weber has joined the channel

Malte Thodberg (06:14:28): > Bioconductor also encourages reuse of common S4-classses that are maintained and optimized by the core team. CRAN is more of a wild west of S3 (perhaps with the exception of tidyverse packages)

Alan O’C (06:39:17): > Even tidyverse is kind of a wild west. I think Hadley’s written about how they’ve got a mad mix of class systems (ggproto and reference classes for example)

Federico Marini (09:28:57): > I don’t know about you, but I don’t think CRAN has much of an interest in having interoperable packages - and thereforeimprove your package, if you want to see it like that. > The review you get there is only technical - and I guess pretty much based on the R CMD check output, with the extra “as-cran” flags on. > Every package submitted to Bioc gets a human-made (andfor humans) review, which normally elevates the package one or two steps more. And this is potentially priceless in the academic software devel panorama

Lori Shepherd (09:30:59): > And as mentioned above… much more than a repository . you get this; a whole community to tap into and ask for advice and help.

Kasper D. Hansen (10:11:47): > I would say a critical factor is the dependency tree which was apparant from the get-go would be much bigger than for CRAN. A bigger dependency tree has clear immediate implications for testing and release

Kasper D. Hansen (10:13:25): > You can see the same with the tidyverse now, where I would argue they are starting to suffer from their approach of just updating each package as necessary. But because there are so many packages, tidyverse is essentially updated all the time. Not great for reproducibility. You can then argue that it is the users responsibility of making it reproducible, but that’s really just punting on a complicated problem.

Aparna Krishnan (13:56:31): > @Aparna Krishnan has joined the channel

Casey Greene (15:05:45): > I know@Sean Daviswon’t toot his own horn but he has a new gig:slightly_smiling_face:https://twitter.com/GreeneScientist/status/1346548082549391365?s=20 - Attachment (twitter): Attachment > I am thrilled to be joined by @seandavis12 at @CUAnschutz @CUMedicalSchool @CUCancerCenter to work together at the intersection of AI and health! > > https://news.cuanschutz.edu/cancer-center/sean-davis-data-science-ai https://pbs.twimg.com/media/Eq_mF_wXEAATmoE.jpg

Aaron Lun (15:06:46): > damn, I didn’t know sean was a MD.

Aaron Lun (15:07:25): > I’ll remember that the next time we’re at a conference together and I sprain my ankle or something.

Lluís Revilla (15:18:53): > Thanks all for the input, but I was thinking (and I think OP too) more on the historical events that lead to the creation of Bioconductor.

Sean Davis (16:08:05) (in thread): > Thanks,@Casey Greene. I could not be more excited! (except if, perhaps, I could go to an office sometime soon, but….)

Vince Carey (17:36:11): > @Lluís RevillaI can only speak for my view of the history, but just about every one of the points that has been raised was a concern at the inception of the project with a modest number of packages, back around 2001. We needed packages dealing with annotation that were large and “strange” relative to CRAN packages. We needed stronger standards for documentation than were obligatory at CRAN (vignettes). We needed two maintenance streams (release for stability and devel for experimentation). We needed a fixed schedule for synchronization with changes to R and for interoperation of packages in the ecosystem. We needed that schedule to be rapid enough to accommodate rapid change in biotech and slow enough to permit stabilization of user code. I credit Rob Gentleman with seeing all of those issues, and Jeff Gentry and Jianhua Zhang with starting it off. I am surely forgetting others who set the groundwork – Tony Rossini was our first release manager and his rigorous approach to dealing with stability is memorable. It may be illuminating to read thishttps://genomebiology.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/gb-2004-5-10-r80… very readable, and, IMHO, not that much has changed. - Attachment (Genome Biology): Bioconductor: open software development for computational biology and bioinformatics > The Bioconductor project is an initiative for the collaborative creation of extensible software for computational biology and bioinformatics. The goals of the project include: fostering collaborative development and widespread use of innovative software, reducing barriers to entry into interdisciplinary scientific research, and promoting the achievement of remote reproducibility of research results. We describe details of our aims and methods, identify current challenges, compare Bioconductor to other open bioinformatics projects, and provide working examples.

2021-01-06

Lluís Revilla (04:11:57) (in thread): > Many thanks for all the work since the beginning

The case study 3 inhttps://www.biorxiv.org/content/10.1101/794297v1.full.pdfmentions the ACMG guidelines but I am not sure whether there is an annotator in OpenCRAVAT that uses those attributes. - Attachment (vjcitn.github.io): Annotating variants with OpenCRAVAT, using Bioconductor > BiocOpenCRAVAT

Dario Strbenac (18:00:05): > Someone has proposed thatGenome Wide Association Study (GWAS) statistics should be stored in VCF format. > > To address these issues, we adapted the variant call format to store GWAS summary statistics (GWAS-VCF) and developed open-source tools to use this format in downstream analyses.

2021-01-15

Malte Thodberg (04:35:46): > That paper had me wondering: Is there a Bioconductor package that has developed infrastructure for storing and manipulating GWAS summary stats?

Dario Strbenac (06:00:04): > GWASToolsseems like the one you’re wondering about. The journal article about GWAS-VCF didn’t even mention its existence.

Malte Thodberg (06:23:42): > GWASTools seems to be storing actual genetic data for GWAS, I was thinking in terms of storing post-GWAS summary stats, like positions, rsID, effect/other allele, INFO, MAF, N, P-Value, Z-score, standard error, etc. Basically the type of data you would use for SNP heritability, finemapping and colocalization

2021-01-16

Aishwarya Mandava (17:18:53): > @Aishwarya Mandava has joined the channel

Brad Tremblay (20:00:10): > @Brad Tremblay has joined the channel

2021-01-17

rohitsatyam102 (10:07:31): > Hi everyone. I was curious if there are any remote/ virtual genomics internship intakes happening currently?

Vince Carey (20:20:44) (in thread): > @Malte Thodberg@Kevin BligheI have done a bit of work on “data warehousing” for multiple sets of GWAS summary statistics. The fields noted by Malte define records in SQLite databases. Should we identify some example summary statistics – for example, from the GIANT consortium – and start a package to tackle this problem? The colocalization task is of especial interest.

2021-01-18

Malte Thodberg (02:57:00): > Interesting: What are the advantages of using SQL? My immediate idea would be to use a RangedSummarizedExperiment, with a GPos as ranges and Z-scores/SEs/P-values stored as assays in some form of DelayedArray

Andrea Gobbini (03:33:14): > @Andrea Gobbini has joined the channel

Kasper D. Hansen (04:24:43): > There are some long term advantages to storing data in SQLite because it is OS independent and the file format is unlikely to change. In contrast, when we serialize R objects, we need to update them every time we change the class definition. This has started to become more of a burden. Now,@Malte Thodbergsuggestion makes total sense for the R representation of the database.

Kasper D. Hansen (04:27:03): > Having said that, Im not sure@Vince Carey’s suggestion was based on a careful analysis. Finally, there is the alternative to build of GWAS-VCF and use that to store stuff. Personally, I would probably start with writing code for ingesting GWAS-VCF because no matter what you would probably want to be able to access that data and once you have it, you have some example data to play with. But using GWAS-VCF as the defacto file format for this, might not be a great idea.

Malte Thodberg (04:35:32): > I wouldn’t mind something standardized like GWAS-VCF catching on: Most people use their own csv/tsv files. That quickly becomes tedious to work with with GWAS tracks with tens of millions of variants

Malte Thodberg (04:37:25): > Given so many usefull R-packages for post-GWAS analysis, like COLOC/MOLOC, hypRcoloc, susieR and gChromVar, it’s a shame they don’t use the same data representations

Kasper D. Hansen (05:07:13): > The lack of standardization in the GWAS world is surprising to me. However, it is not clear to me that the answer is GWAS-VCF which - only based on the abstract - reads as someone taking a fileformat they already work with and say “hey, let’s expand this”. But perhaps its good? At least I hope the data they provide is great, even if the file format is not.

Kasper D. Hansen (05:08:09): > In general I am a bit skeptic about these file formats. For example, IMO SAM/BAM was not great and I don’t have a great feeling about VCF but honestly I have only used it very little so I have no insight.

Vince Carey (06:11:29): > https://github.com/mrcieu/gwasglue/

Vince Carey (06:11:39): > That looks pretty amazing?

Malte Thodberg (06:11:55): > Also here:https://www.biorxiv.org/content/biorxiv/early/2020/10/23/2020.10.22.351221.full.pdf

Malte Thodberg (06:12:50): > Both these projects seem to tackle the issue of assembling GWAS summary stats + LD information into a single pipeline

Vince Carey (06:16:04): > Let’s deploy on AnVIL.

Malte Thodberg (06:20:11): > Would be super cool if Bioconductor had a parallel to these projects though, with standardized classes for holding GWAS summary stats and LD-information from various populations. Then one could simply plug-and-play whatever colocalization or finemapping tool was best in a given situation

Vince Carey (06:28:11): > Have you used any of these mrcieu resources?

Malte Thodberg (06:38:54): > Looked briefly at echolocator, but since I was already running all the R-packages, using the their wrappers didn’t seem worth the while

Vince Carey (07:30:45): > @Kasper D. Hansenthe gwasvcf doc includes: Querying by rsid is slow. If a large number of queries by rsid are to be performed then it could be worth generating an index which would speed up the querying. This approach uses SQLite to create a local database, linking rsid to chromosome and position. It strips out the ‘rs’ from the rs identifiers to make fast searchers by integer. The concept is based on that developed here: bioforensics/rsidx. … so it looks like this is a hybrid of tabix/bcftools/sqlite…. perhaps a strong standard has not emerged because such a standard would really be the purview of GA4GH (imho) and the sights seem set very high (https://vrs.ga4gh.org/en/stable/releases.html#id1)

Kasper D. Hansen (07:33:08): > Im just going to say that from where Im sitting, the GWAS community has failed to deliver on this for many years, so I would not hold my breath.

Kasper D. Hansen (07:33:44): > A good long term solution might also not be easy, for example with different reference genomes and different populations and structural variants.

Kasper D. Hansen (07:34:30): > But more immediate challenges, for example how to do a co-localization analysis well in R using standardized objects, seems doable.

Kasper D. Hansen (07:35:24): > This may be a situation where you should start with a few reachable and identified goals and focus on that. And not get distracted by a perfect solution until you have some experience with 1 iteration.

Vince Carey (07:50:29) (in thread): > We have to make good choices on representation and storage for the analyses to be performant. But it seems like you@Kevin Bligheare thinking more about individual-level contributions to GWAS. Even the summary statistics are proving to be unwieldy and need benchmarking and standardization. I’ll have a look at RegParallel….

Vince Carey (08:02:32): > The gwasglue seems to depend onhttps://github.com/explodecomputer/genetics.binaRies… which provides plink, bcftools, and gcta in binary format…. unwinding this could be laborious.

Malte Thodberg (08:14:09): > There’s quite a few very useful R packages for analyzing GWAS summary stats: > * COLOC:https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/coloc/vignettes/vignette.html > * MOLOC:https://github.com/clagiamba/moloc > * HyPrColoc:https://github.com/jrs95/hyprcoloc > * susieR:https://github.com/jrs95/hyprcoloc > * mmbr:https://github.com/stephenslab/mmbr > * chromVar:http://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/chromVAR.html > * gChromVar:https://caleblareau.github.io/gchromVAR/articles/gchromVAR_vignette.html

Malte Thodberg (08:15:42): > It seems obvious to use them together with GRanges. Main thing would be a class to store the summary stats, and then sum functions for easier merging.

Malte Thodberg (08:16:18): > For example, merging two GPos based on seqnames/pos/strand and effect/other allele.

Kasper D. Hansen (08:29:06): > There is also the locusZoom script which is widely used but is just one enourmous R script as far as I know

Malte Thodberg (08:31:53): > Never really liked LocusZoom, simply doesn’t produce very nice looking plot. We are currently working on a ggplot-based replacement:https://github.com/MalteThodberg/tidyGenomeBrowser

Vince Carey (09:08:46): > Maybe we should start a new channel for GWAS-related software in Bioc. But I will just dump out this concept: We are looking for a GRanges-oriented interface to an external store with columns of diverse types, IMHO. So really a DelayedDataFrame as the mcols of a GRanges… I don’t think we have that. Maybe recent work in TileDb is relevant; bigrquery also comes to mind. The restfulSE project is somewhat relevant – we have have a RangedSummarizedExperiment with a BigQuery back end … for both the assay and the colData IIRC. But handling the colData is complex. End of dump. bigdata-rep channel very relevant.

Martin Morgan (09:11:27): > @Qian Liuhas worked on DelayedDataFrame and also with gdsfmt ; perhaps she will provide some commentary

Martin Morgan (09:12:07): > http://bioconductor.org/packages/devel/bioc/html/DelayedDataFrame.html - Attachment (Bioconductor): DelayedDataFrame (development version) > Based on the standard DataFrame metaphor, we are trying to implement the feature of delayed operation on the DelayedDataFrame, with a slot of lazyIndex, which saves the mapping indexes for each column of DelayedDataFrame. Methods like show, validity check, [/[[ subsetting, rbind/cbind are implemented for DelayedDataFrame to be operated around lazyIndex. The listData slot stays untouched until a realization call e.g., DataFrame constructor OR as.list() is invoked.

Vince Carey (09:12:25): > right … i googled on that but did not see it … it is low in page rank for now

Qian Liu (09:20:14): > Thanks@Martin Morgangdsarray uses gdsfmt as the back end, which was originally designed by xiuwen Zheng for gwas data lazy representation. Delayeddatadrame is compatible with all delayedarray concepts including gdsarray.

Qian Liu (09:26:27): > Xiuwen has also developed several very useful bioc packages for analyzing big popular genomic data usinggdsfmt(core array). For example,SNPRelatefor analyzing GWAS data, andSeqArrayfor analyzing sequencing data (as an alternative to VCF). A new package calledSCArrayalso suggests that he is working to use gds format to lazily represent the SCRNA-seq data. He is also a co-author ofGWASTools. The series of packages for representing and analyzing GWAS/DNA-seq/scRNA-seq data using GDS format is well worth to explore.

Qian Liu (23:18:45): > Above message updated!

Qian Liu (23:20:36): > TheGDSArraypackage is currently a wrapper of GDS formats that corresponds to theSNPRelateandSEQArrayonly. There are more general gds formats that I will need to update theGDSArrayto accommodate.

Qian Liu (23:23:38): > For@Vince Careyconcept, theDelayedDataFrameis now extending theDataFrameand representsDelayedArraycolumns (e.g.,GDSArray,VCFArray) It should fit as mcols of GRanges.

2021-01-19

Aaron Lun (16:07:05): > Julia’s “all functions are methods” philosophy is pretty interesting. Basically every function is (or has the capability of being) equivalent to an S4 generic in R.

Alma Hernandez de Rojas (16:18:57): > @Alma Hernandez de Rojas has joined the channel

Spencer Nystrom (16:29:05) (in thread): > I spent a lot of time with pre-1.0 Julia and really loved this about it. Alas, the Biojulia project is not keeping pace over time as I’d like.Biocwins again.

Aaron Lun (16:40:49) (in thread): > As a learning experience/proof of concept, I’m going to see if I can replicate some of BioC’s core structures and generics in Julia. Probably will start with the SE and see how far I can go.

Spencer Nystrom (16:48:45) (in thread): > That would be really neat. I’ll be curious to hear your thoughts after you’ve spent some time with it, so do report back. I very much want to jump on the Julia hype train, but I got a little tired of having to implement everything from scratch just to get an analysis done. I’m sure if you get something working well the Biojulia folks would be interested. > > A major annoyance for me was theirGenomicFeatures.jlwhich is their attempt atGRanges, but it uses B-trees and the ergonomics of it aren’t quite the same, but maybe I just never grokked it correctly. Regardless, something more data.frame like in that space could really open up the genomics space.

Aaron Lun (16:54:28) (in thread): > I can already see one advantage of the Julia CLI over python’s; I can indent as haphazardly as I like and it’ll take it.

Spencer Nystrom (16:58:10) (in thread): > Performance of C, flexibility of python, indentation like a madman (if you want).

Hervé Pagès (16:59:28) (in thread): > This will be an interesting experiment@Aaron Lun. I had that itch for a long time now but never got the time to dedicate to it. Let us know how that goes.

Aaron Lun (17:18:37) (in thread): > Trickiest part is how to handle copies, and the fact that the class objects are not mutable by default. A philosophy generally closer to R than Python, I would say.

Michael Lawrence (19:05:22) (in thread): > GenomicFeatures.jlwas developed by Pete Haverty, formerly of our group here at GNE. It was directly inspired by Bioconductor, and made some interesting internal optimizations that we could adopt (not sure about back compat).

Michael Lawrence (19:09:03) (in thread): > Adding more Bioc equivalents is a good idea. Julia generics though are less constrained than S4 generics (the formals do not need to be consistent), which makes them less useful as an abstraction. Of course, a project could voluntarily put that constraint on itself.

Spencer Nystrom (19:09:08) (in thread): > Was that notGenomicVectors.jl?

Spencer Nystrom (19:09:45) (in thread): > I remember pete pointing me towards that one in the early dev phase.

Spencer Nystrom (19:10:11) (in thread): > I recall that one acting more similarly to Granges.

Michael Lawrence (19:10:44) (in thread): > Yea, that’s right, sorry.

Michael Lawrence (19:14:34) (in thread): > Julia would complement R in much the same way that C++ does, as a means of increasing performance, with the added benefit (over C++) of being a simpler high-level language. R would remain the primary user interface. From that perspective, it may be acceptable and even desirable for the Julia equivalent of Bioc to implement mutable data structures.

Aaron Lun (19:15:20) (in thread): > for that, we would need no-memory copies of data structures into Julia.

Aaron Lun (19:15:33) (in thread): > I don’t know whether the RJulia interface really supports that, or whether we have to do it ourselves.

Michael Lawrence (19:15:42) (in thread): > definitely doable; would be fun

Aaron Lun (19:15:49) (in thread): > juliacall

Michael Lawrence (19:21:05) (in thread): > Would still need COW at the interface layer, but mutable after initial (lazy) copy.

Aaron Lun (19:30:50) (in thread): > well, that’s the thing; do we? We could justunsafe_wrapthe input to create a Julia object that is understood by developers to be immutable. Provisioning the output is a bit trickier but could be done by calling into R to allocate the necessary memory.

2021-01-20

Aaron Lun (03:59:27) (in thread): > My god. I finally got this damn thing working. Uses Julia as an embedded language inside the C++ code of an R package.

Aaron Lun (04:05:45) (in thread): > Major hurdles with actual deployment as the embedding relies on dynamic linking. Also I keep on getting stack smashing errors when I close the R session, though that’s probably because I had to do some voodoodlopenwithout adlclosesomewhere.

Kasper D. Hansen (04:13:35) (in thread): > How are Julia doing now with packages? Last time I looked I felt that the package concept (installing from say Github) was much more developer friendly, but I think that is a huge mistake. One of the most powerful thing about R is actually the CRAN/Bioc gatekeeping insisting on minimal (and sometimes not so minimal) requirements.

Spencer Nystrom (07:16:05) (in thread): > My understanding is that what happens a bit is folks will form groups which host packages around a task, like BioJulia and the JuliaImage groups. These groups have their own standards. Of course, as you say, most things are hosted on GitHub, and there is a version of package registration, but nowhere like CRAN or bioc. The package manager, underwent a major rewrite years back to support many backends that are not GitHub.

2021-01-21

Aaron Lun (03:19:29) (in thread): > Still waiting for my SQLDataFrame that’s actually a DataFrame, by the way.

Robert Castelo (07:03:18): > Not sure this is the right channel (tell me otherwise), but is there any documentation out there on best practices in using BiocParallel and basilisk? i want to call a python program from an R/Bioc pkg. the python program takes one BAM file at a time, i want tobplapply()over BAM files recorded in a BamFileList object. i guess the two main options are: > 1. open parallelism before starting the basilisk run > > > bplapply(bfl, function(bf) { cl <- basiliskStart(env) ; out <- basiliskRun(cl, function() { ## call python }) ; basiliskStop(cl) } > > 2. open parallelism within the basilisk run > > cl <- basiliskStart(env) ; out <- basiliskRun(cl, function() { bplapply(bfl, function() { ## call python }) }) ; basiliskStop(cl) >

Aaron Lun (11:18:14): > the former is safer with respect to arbitrary BPPARAMs. The latter only works in shared memory contexts, and might not even achieve parallelization in some cases.

Martin Morgan (11:25:28): > It seems likeSnowParam()is going to be a better bet for these complicated types of situations, which is method 1.

Robert Castelo (11:47:36): > Thanks for the advice, i’ll follow method 1. One further question for@Aaron Lun, i looked up the code ofvelociraptor, expecting to maybe find an example of using BiocParallel andbasilisktogether, which i didn’t, but i noticed that the call tobasiliskRun()is not enclosed by thebasiliskStart()andbasiliskStop()calls. How velociraptor creates the Python environment withoutbasiliskStart()? What is the recommended use ofbasiliskStart()andbasiliskStop()?

Aaron Lun (11:48:17): > oh,basiliskRuncalls start/stop automatically if you specifyenvand don’t pass it aclobject.

Aaron Lun (11:48:38): > The explicit start/stop is useful if you have multiplebasiliskRuncalls in between them.

Robert Castelo (11:55:28): > i see, thanks for the clarification!!:blush:

Román Pérez (15:54:14): > @Román Pérez has joined the channel

Amarinder Singh Thind (20:01:37): > any recommendation of R packages for nanostring analyses

Alan O’C (21:42:32) (in thread): > Been a while but I believe this is well regarded for QC. Once you’ve got normalised expression values it depends what you want to do downstream but should mostly be able to treat it as any other expression matrixhttps://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/NanoStringQCPro.html - Attachment (Bioconductor): NanoStringQCPro > NanoStringQCPro provides a set of quality metrics that can be used to assess the quality of NanoString mRNA gene expression data – i.e. to identify outlier probes and outlier samples. It also provides different background subtraction and normalization approaches for this data. It outputs suggestions for flagging samples/probes and an easily sharable html quality control output.

Amarinder Singh Thind (21:52:12) (in thread): > Thanks for sharing, but I can’t find a relevant article of this method. Recently, the normalization problem was reported in nanostring commercial pipeline.https://academic.oup.com/nar/article/47/12/6073/5494770and I am wondering if anyone has usedNanoNormIterhttps://academic.oup.com/bib/advance-article/doi/10.1093/bib/bbaa163/5891144

The > ensemblVEP package code and documentation have been updated to reflect this change. > Downloads:[http://uswest.ensembl.org/info/docs/tools/vep/index.html](http://uswest.ensembl.org/info/docs/tools/vep/index.html)Complete documentation for runtime options:[http://uswest.ensembl.org/info/docs/tools/vep/script/vep_options](http://uswest.ensembl.org/info/docs/tools/vep/script/vep_options). > html > To test that Ensembl VEP is properly installed, enter the name of the script from the command line: > vep > > This is fromhttp://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/vignettes/ensemblVEP/inst/doc/ensemblVEP.pdf; the README is athttps://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/readmes/ensemblVEP/README. Both are linked from the package landing pagehttps://bioconductor.org/packages/ensemblVEP. > > Maybe a helper functionensemblVEP::valid()could provide diagnostics? - Attachment (Bioconductor): ensemblVEP > Query the Ensembl Variant Effect Predictor via the perl API.

Dario Strbenac (00:00:06): > Does anyone know what happened toBioinformaticsjournal? Its latest issue was four months ago.

Jared Andrews (01:05:31): > My guess is they’re just behind on getting the “proper” issues together since they all get listed as “Advance articles” anyway. Pretty sure their printing/shipping was impacted by COVID. The latest issue was just shipped on January 11th. Pre-COVID, they were shipping every ~2 weeks - that’s fallen to about once a month since mid summer.

Dario Strbenac (22:00:14): > Does anyone have a computationally-efficient R script that groups variables into clusters, such that each cluster contains a group of variables where each variable has correlation of at least some number (e.g. 0.85) with all other variables within the same cluster? If so, may you share it with me?

Aaron Lun (22:01:54): > You could probably do the cosine normalization trick, thenBiocNeighbors::findNeighborsat a distance threshold corresponding to a correlation of 0.85, followed by clique discovery with ****igraph****.

Aaron Lun (22:02:17): > Should work decently well for a few thousand variables, depending on the dimensionality.

Michael Lawrence (11:51:48): > I wonder if there could be a way for Slack (like via some sort of bot?) to recognize when people mention a Bioc package name and automatically highlight it, and make a link to the package page. That might also allow integration of Slack conversations with package metadata somewhere. Like, “what are people saying about this package?“.

Alan O’C (12:20:28) (in thread): > You probably would want some way to tag packages (eg, what R people do on twitter, {pkg}), to avoid the bot searching every word of every message on bioc. I’ve not written slackbots but I imagine it’d be pretty easy for someone (other than me) to do

Sridhar N (12:21:35): > There are quite a few bots that are floating around

Sridhar N (12:21:54): > but integration is PTA IMO

Sridhar N (12:22:11): > unless some one has down time and willing to incorporate it in here

Hervé Pagès (12:46:03): > PTA?

Alan O’C (12:47:20): > PITA?

Hervé Pagès (12:49:11) (in thread): > I agree that without specific tagging, we’d probably get too many false positives.

Michael Lawrence (12:56:15) (in thread): > Specific tagging sounds like a pragmatic solution; is it supported?

Sridhar N (13:07:23): > Yes

Sridhar N (13:07:24): > lol

Hervé Pagès (13:14:21) (in thread): > Don’t know, not a Slack expert. Now how many Slack users are actually going to use the specific tagging is another question.

Alan O’C (13:15:31) (in thread): > A bunch of existing bots filter/process the text of messages so I think it must be. Also agreed, it might be useful for the 5 people who use it (I would probably not bother lol)

Michael Lawrence (14:58:09) (in thread): > Well I’ve noticed people at least using simple markdown on GitHub issues, e.g., for code, so I’m optimistic. If we had special functionality like that, there could be a bot that informs people about it upon first joining the Slack.

> It worked well until I installed the “limma” package to speed up the find clusters, and I selected “Yes” when it asked, Do you want to install from sources the package which needs compilation? (Yes/no/cancel) > > Now I get the following error message when I try to load the Library(Seurat) > > Error: package or namespace load failed for ‘Seurat’:
> object ‘markvario’ is not exported by ‘namespace:spatstat’ > > I uninstalled R and re-installed and I re-installed R studio and re-installed Seurat and re-installed limma, but I’m still getting the error. > > How can I fix this?

Naohisa Goto (00:24:24): > @Naohisa Goto has joined the channel

Dario Strbenac (02:00:06): > I don’t get it. > > > 1+!+1 > [1] 1 >

Martin Morgan (02:03:16): > Nice! > > > !1 > [1] FALSE > > 1 + FALSE > [1] 1 > > and also > > > 1 + ! + 1 + 1 > [1] 1 > > !1 + 1 > [1] FALSE > > ! + 1 + 1 > [1] FALSE > > 1 + ! + ! + 1 > [1] 2 >

Chengyang Ji (02:45:53): > What does “+” do here > > > !+TRUE > [1] FALSE >

Kasper D. Hansen (03:27:47): > probably the same as > > > +1 > [1] 1 >

Chengyang Ji (03:29:18): > Right. > And I think what might be happening here is that~~+~~has the power to convertbooleantype tonumeric, and!can do the opposite. > > > !+1 > [1] FALSE > > +!1 > [1] 0 > > This might be more obvious if parenthesis are used to force operator precedence > > > (1+!)+1 > Error: unexpected ')' in "(1+!)" > > 1+(!+1) > [1] 1 > > And also in javascript, they allow+to convertstringtonumber

Chengyang Ji (03:35:43): > Like this > > Welcome to Node.js v14.15.1. > Type ".help" for more information. > > +"123" > 123 >

Alan O’C (05:50:16) (in thread): > usually - File (PNG): whydoesR.png

Marcel Ramos Pérez (10:13:23) (in thread): > converts to numeric > > > `+`(TRUE) > [1] 1 >

Dipanjan Dey (15:08:34): > @Dipanjan Dey has joined the channel

Wei (23:13:28): > @Wei has joined the channel

Enrica Calura (18:39:16): > @Enrica Calura has joined the channel

2021-04-16

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (10:17:28): > I’m close to solving an issue for theTVTBpackage, but I’m stuck on a ‘basic’ R code issue: > In the example below, the user wants an expression that returnsTRUEfor elements of the list that containany combinationof the target values. > At the moment, it is missing the first element of the listc("a", "b"), which contains both values, and should returnTRUE > > targets <- c("a", "b") > x <- list( > c("a", "b"), > "c", > "d", > "e", > "a", > "b" > ) > x %in% targets > # [1] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE TRUE TRUE > > I thought I’d try this friendly crowd before I head to SO on a Friday:wink:

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (10:18:21) (in thread): > Original issue:https://github.com/kevinrue/TVTB/issues/8 - Attachment: #8 Rules of accepting multiple filters > Hi there, I am new here
> I would like to know if I would like to keep entries in VCF to a specific set of filters.
> For example, I would accept variants with any combinations of the following filters: c("ASSEMBLY_BIAS", "ASSEMBLY_ONLY", "PASS", "PON")
> How should I specify the rules?
> I have tried several kinds of syntax such as > > > all_filters <- c("ASSEMBLY_BIAS", "ASSEMBLY_ONLY", "PASS", "PON") > customFilterRules <- VcfFixedRules( > exprs = list( > all = expression(FILTER %in% all_filters) > )) > > > > However, it doesn’t work.
> Is it required to have another customized function? Thank you.

Charlotte Soneson (10:22:03) (in thread): > vapply(x, function(y) any(y %in% targets), FALSE)?

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (10:22:52) (in thread): > riiiight, thanks! let’s see if that works as aFilterRulesexpression as well

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (10:29:10) (in thread): > Thanks both. My understanding of their issue is that@Charlotte Soneson’sanyis the right solution, not@Kevin Blighe’sall. We’ll see when they answer.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (10:30:03) (in thread): > That said I still haven’t fully converted that into a anS4Vectors::FilterRulesthat behaves well inevalSeparately. I’m just a bit rusty I guess:smile:

Martin Morgan (10:30:52) (in thread): > alsovapply(relist(unlist(x) %in% targets, x), any, logical(1))which I think would be more efficient (maybe not!); in XVector-land you get any(CharacterList(x) %in% targets) which is fast and comprehensible

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (10:33:34) (in thread): > actually,@Kevin Blighe, you’re making reconsider that they were likely after your version, now that I read their post again

Michael Lawrence (11:09:07) (in thread): > > any(CharacterList(x) %in% targets) >

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:10:43) (in thread): > testing now, thanks! (in FilterRules expression)

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:11:27) (in thread): > yay!

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:13:49) (in thread): > Thanks all for your help, here’s my reply to their issue:https://github.com/kevinrue/TVTB/issues/8#issuecomment-821247079 - Attachment: Comment on #8 Rules of accepting multiple filters > Dear @hsiaoyi0504 > > I will split my answer in a few points. I hope they are all clear. > > Origin and solution to the error > > Having cross-posted your issue on the package that TVTB relies on to actually do the work:
> <https://github.com/Bioconductor/S4Vectors/issues/80#issuecomment-820623709|Bioconductor/S4Vectors#80 (comment)> > > I was made aware that the calling environment is not automatically passed to the filtering step. > > The solution to your issue is to pass the global environment (where the object all_filters is defined, to the evalSparately() function call, so that the object all_filters is visible in the environment where the expression is evaluated. > > > all_filters <- c("ASSEMBLY_BIAS", "ASSEMBLY_ONLY", "PASS", "PON") > customFilterRules <- VcfFixedRules( > exprs = list( > all = expression(FILTER %in% all_filters) > )) > summary(evalSeparately(customFilterRules, vcf)) # fails > summary(evalSeparately(customFilterRules, vcf, enclos = .GlobalEnv)) # succeeds > > > > To illustrate the point, I will note that another solution could be to define the vector of values in the expression itself, so that it is defined on the fly when the rule is evaluated. However, that is probably not convenient for a programmatic and dynamic approach. > > > customFilterRules [https://www.internationalgenome.org/wiki/Analysis/vcf4.0/](- VcfFixedRules( > exprs = list( > all = expression(FILTER %in% c("ASSEMBLY_BIAS", "ASSEMBLY_ONLY", "PASS", "PON")) > )) > summary(evalSeparately(customFilterRules, vcf)) > > > > Clarification about “combinations of a set of filters” > > I understand what you mean now. It is possible for the FILTER column to contain multiple values separated by semi-colon, e.g. “q10;s50” (https://www.internationalgenome.org/wiki/Analysis/vcf4.0/)). > > I’m just not sure whether you want: > > • records where the FILTER value contains any one of the target values > • records where the FILTER value contains all of the target values > > As the VariantAnnotation package does not split the FILTER value (it is read as a single string "q10;s50", it is not converted as a vector c("q10", "s50"), you need to do the splitting yourself. > > I have created an example file here: [https://gist.github.com/kevinrue/120d584b07834fc457ec1442fd72c5df](https://gist.github.com/kevinrue/120d584b07834fc457ec1442fd72c5df)
> My example code below refers to this file, if you wish to reproduce my code and outputs. > > First, load the file as a VCF object: > > > library(SummarizedExperiment) > library(TVTB) > > vcf <- VariantAnnotation::readVcf(file = "moderate.vcf") > > all_filters <- c("q10", "q20") > > > > Assuming this is what you want: > >). > > I’m just not sure whether you want: > > • records where the FILTER value contains any one of the target values > • records where the FILTER value contains all of the target values > > As the VariantAnnotation package does not split the FILTER value (it is read as a single string "q10;s50", it is not converted as a vector c("q10", "s50"), you need to do the splitting yourself. > > I have created an example file here: https://gist.github.com/kevinrue/120d584b07834fc457ec1442fd72c5df
> My example code below refers to this file, if you wish to reproduce my code and outputs. > > First, load the file as a VCF object: > > > library(SummarizedExperiment) > library(TVTB) > > vcf <- VariantAnnotation::readVcf(file = "moderate.vcf") > > all_filters <- c("q10", "q20") > > > > Assuming this is what you want: > > ) • records where the FILTER value contains all of the target values > > Then, the code would be: > > > customFilterRules <- VcfFixedRules( > exprs = list( > all2 = expression(unlist(lapply(strsplit(FILTER, ";"), function(x) all(all_filters %in% x)))) > )) > summary(evalSeparately(customFilterRules, vcf, enclos = .GlobalEnv)) > evalSeparately(customFilterRules, vcf, enclos = .GlobalEnv) > rowRanges(vcf)[eval(customFilterRules, vcf, enclos = .GlobalEnv)] > rowRanges(vcf) # compare with > > > > Assuming this is what you want: > > • records where the FILTER value contains any one of the target values > > then, the code would be > > > customFilterRules <- VcfFixedRules( > exprs = list( > all2 = expression(any(CharacterList(strsplit(FILTER, ";")) %in% all_filters)) > )) > > > > Let me know how it goes!

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:14:35) (in thread): > @Michael Lawrencedo you have the equivalent for matching “all” the target values? it’s not as simply as switchinganytoall:innocent:

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:15:29) (in thread): > (i think I almost got it though)

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:17:25) (in thread): > > unlist(lapply( > CharacterList(x), > function(y) all(targets %in% y) > )) > > though I just realise that in that case, it’s literally just converting from one type of list to another

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:18:02) (in thread): > damn, that’s worth a Friday beer:stuck_out_tongue_winking_eye:

Michael Lawrence (11:28:37) (in thread): > sum(unique(CharacterList(x)) %in% targets) == length(targets)

Michael Lawrence (11:31:59) (in thread): > all(CharacterList(targets) %in% CharacterList(x))

Michael Lawrence (11:33:30) (in thread): > That last one is a bit unintuitive because of the behavior ofCharacterList()on a list vs. vector, and then the recycling.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:35:03) (in thread): > :exploding_head:

2021-04-17

Aaron Lun (11:28:01): > no, but I would really like someone to pick up the slack on the mumosa package.

Michael Love (13:44:16): > thanks for the pointer@Lluís Revillaalso just to point out to folks here: it’s community maintained so if you see something missing, PR!

Michael Love (13:45:31): > something that shocked me when i started reading (some) stats multi-omics papers is that there’s not much inter-citation, a random paper by Foobar will just cite groundbreaking work by Foobar (1998) Foobar and Barfoo (2005) and recent developments by Barfoo and Foobar (2019)

Michael Love (13:46:09): > i’ll constantly find new L1 regularized CCA implementations out there

Aaron Lun (13:46:50): > if your group is actively working on any single cell stuff, do you want to take over mumosa?

Michael Love (13:47:08): > haha, not doing sc multi omics right now:slightly_smiling_face:nice try tho

Aaron Lun (13:47:12): > damn

Aaron Lun (13:47:19): > well, that’s an open invitation for anyone.

Michael Love (13:47:23): > MOFA folks could turn it into mumofasa?

Michael Love (13:47:38): > mosafamu

Michael Love (13:47:47): > mo omics mo problems

Kasper D. Hansen (13:52:24): > I think its a common weakness in the stats literature: very light citation practice

Federico Marini (15:13:25) (in thread): > mufasa:lion_face:

Ashwini Shanmugam (16:52:11): > @Ashwini Shanmugam has joined the channel

2021-04-22

Davide Risso (16:08:56): > This R behavior was surprising to me… > > > x <- seq(10) > > x[c(TRUE, FALSE)] > [1] 1 3 5 7 9 > > the surprise was that there is no warning that the two vectors are of different lengths… any idea what is the reason for the lack of warning? Am I missing something obvious?

Davide Risso (16:09:55): > I encounter this while reviewing this code: > > keepgenes <- !isOutlier(sce_cordblood$sum, type="lower", log=TRUE) > sce_cordblood <- sce_cordblood[keepgenes, ] > > Obviously the intent was to filter out cells, but R didn’t complain and the student didn’t realize they filtered out genes instead

Alan O’C (16:11:07): > Vector recycling, R never warns for that

Alan O’C (16:11:47): > Doesn’t even warn forx[c(T, F, T)]

Davide Risso (16:12:41): > yeah exactly, I used a wrong minimal example since 2 is a multiple of 10, but I assumed that when not a multiple of the vector length it would throw a warning, while it doesn’t

Alan O’C (16:12:50): > Well strictly speaking it will~warn~error if you try to add a column to a dataframe and the length of the vector isn’t a factor of the number of rows, and similar cases.

Alan O’C (16:14:20): - File (PNG): whydoesR.png

Davide Risso (16:15:46): > yeah I guess that I knew that R recycles vectors, I just never thought that it could lead to that type of easy-to-make mistake in matrix subsetting

Alan O’C (16:17:08): > That,match, and factors have each caused me immeasurable pain and embarrassment over the years

Hervé Pagès (16:24:07): > Yeah, weird that R doesn’t issue a warning forx[c(T, F, T)], especially since things likerep(TRUE, 10) & c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE)or1:10 + c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE)do issue one. You wish that vector recycling would behave consistently across a wide range of operations. And that would just be the case if the recycling code was implemented in one single place and shared but I’m not aware of anything like that. So it seems that every function needs to re-implement their own little recycling mechanism:disappointed:

Hervé Pagès (16:32:17): > Another famous exception to the rule: > > > paste0(letters, LETTERS[1:3]) > [1] "aA" "bB" "cC" "dA" "eB" "fC" "gA" "hB" "iC" "jA" "kB" "lC" "mA" "nB" "oC" > [16] "pA" "qB" "rC" "sA" "tB" "uC" "vA" "wB" "xC" "yA" "zB" > > No warning! > Also: > > > paste0(letters, LETTERS[0]) > [1] "a" "b" "c" "d" "e" "f" "g" "h" "i" "j" "k" "l" "m" "n" "o" "p" "q" "r" "s" > [20] "t" "u" "v" "w" "x" "y" "z" > > Inconsistent with how binary arithmetic operations treat the zero-length case.

2021-04-23

Robert Castelo (02:11:32): > The only place whereRecycling rulesare referred to in the Rlang manual (here), where it is described that a warning is given when the longer vector is not a multiple of the shorter one, is the section onElementary arithmetic operations, so probably we cannot expect any specific behaviour for non-arithmetic operations. TheR infernofrom Patrick Burns contains another funny edge case of the recyling rule even in arithmetics, wheresomething plus nothing is … nothing! > > > 1 + NULL > numeric(0) >

FelixErnst (03:37:52) (in thread): > I always think ofNULLas a blackhole. It sucks in everything and even though you cannot see it, it is usually somewhere

Robert Castelo (04:15:53) (in thread): > Albert Einstein would have been an enthusiastic R user:zany_face:

FelixErnst (05:19:15) (in thread): > You are onto something:slightly_smiling_face:NAis the empty space: If you add something to that is still vast empty space. That should have added AE’s enthusiasm

Robert Castelo (05:53:21) (in thread): > If physicists would learn about this, they would all quit MATLAB, Maple and Mathematica and move into R:grin:… or maybe that’s the reason why so many physicists move into bioinformatics with R.

Alan O’C (11:36:21): > I’m part of a group in bonnie Scotland developing a short (2-day) carpentries-style course on high-dimensional statistics for biological and health data. If you’d be interested in taking that type of course it’d be helpful if you could fill out this form to give us ideas on what topics to coverhttps://forms.gle/yvA3fKyHkkJhYUTr7 - Attachment (Google Docs): Learners for high dimensional statistics > High dimensional stats in this context refers to methods you might apply (for example) to large sequencing data like RNAseq, scRNAseq, or genetic sequence data particularly of large cohorts. We might cover topics like dimensionality reduction, clustering, multiple testing, and some broader statistical concepts that can be useful for data like these. These are some questions to guide our development of a course on statistics for high-dimensional datasets, to choose what topics to cover and how to cover them. The course will be delivered in the latter half of 2021.

Alan O’C (12:24:36) (in thread): > Should mention: the course materials will be open and we’ll be happily encouraging others to use the materials. We may also be looking for external groups who would like to test out the course in the latter stages of development:slightly_smiling_face:

Henrik Bengtsson (13:59:10) (in thread): > > x[c(T, F, T)] > I’m pretty sure Section ‘Indexing’ in ‘R Language Definition’ (https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/R-lang.html#Indexing) covers that case. We relied on that long section when implementing support for row and column subsetting inmatrixStatsand I remembering it as comprehensive and covering lots of corner cases.

Brandon Le (16:03:07): > @Brandon Le has joined the channel

Wes W (18:39:06) (in thread): > Happy to participate and would love to run your course here in Philly

Haichao Wang (08:51:35): > Hi guys, just to double confirm that pushing bug fix to RELEASE_3_12 is not accepted anymore right?

Haichao Wang (08:52:19): > my pushing to master branch is successful, but RELEASE_3_12 was rejected

Haichao Wang (08:53:42): > I saw the announced schedule here:http://bioconductor.org/developers/release-schedule/

Haichao Wang (08:54:10): > but not sure whether I understand it correctly, as this is my first package ( as a maintainer!)

Lori Shepherd (08:58:18): > yes. We freezed the RELEASE_3_12 branch to no longer accept changes. You may continue to push to master (Devel branch). When the release happens in another two weeks the core team will create the new RELEASE_3_13 branch from the current version of the master branch.

Haichao Wang (09:14:05): > will the RELEASE_3_12 be kept in the git repo as a branch? or it will be wiped completely after 3_13?

Lori Shepherd (09:22:26): > it will be kept for legacy and we keep the versions of the package to be able to download the appropriate version of the package for past versions of R

Lori Shepherd (09:22:45): > but you will not be able to push to ourgit.bioconductor.orgversion of the release_3_12 branch

Mike Smith (09:24:29): > If you look at a package that’s been in Bioconductor for a long time, you can see the available RELEASE branches go back a quite a way e.g.https://code.bioconductor.org/browse/biomaRt/

Pratheepa Jeganathan (10:37:28): > @Pratheepa Jeganathan has joined the channel

Haichao Wang (10:51:48): > thank you both Lori and Mike. still got another Q: will I be allowed delete the old RELEASE branches? (due to some special reasons …)

Lori Shepherd (10:55:27): > normally we would keep all branches ongit.bioconductor.orgfor legacy purposes and not delete anything – it would probably be better to have a private discussion over email if there were special circumstances for this request. I suggest maybe the maintainer atbioconductor.orgemail

Haichao Wang (14:17:58): > thanks Lori, appreciate your suggestion. I will emailmaintainer@bioconductor.orgabout this

MONAH ABOU ALEZZ (09:59:14): > @MONAH ABOU ALEZZ has joined the channel

Alan O’C (10:43:34): > Any ideas what licence GEO data is released under?

Casey Greene (10:46:47): > the “good luck” license

Casey Greene (10:46:50): > > Databases of molecular data on the NCBI Web site include such examples as nucleotide sequences (GenBank), protein sequences, macromolecular structures, molecular variation, gene expression, and mapping data. They are designed to provide and encourage access within the scientific community to sources of current and comprehensive information. Therefore, NCBI itself places no restrictions on the use or distribution of the data contained therein. Nor do we accept data when the submitter has requested restrictions on reuse or redistribution. However, some submitters of the original data (or the country of origin of such data) may claim patent, copyright, or other intellectual property rights in all or a portion of the data (that has been submitted). NCBI is not in a position to assess the validity of such claims and since there is no transfer of rights from submitters to NCBI, NCBI has no rights to transfer to a third party. Therefore, NCBI cannot provide comment or unrestricted permission concerning the use, copying, or distribution of the information contained in the molecular databases.

Casey Greene (10:47:10): > key is:Nor do we accept data when the submitter has requested restrictions on reuse or redistribution.

Alan O’C (10:49:29): > Clear as mud

Alan O’C (10:49:44): > Thanks though, where’d you find it?

Casey Greene (10:50:45): > https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/home/about/policies/

Sean Davis (11:54:45): > Also, this:https://grants.nih.gov/grants/guide/notice-files/not-od-14-124.html - Attachment (grants.nih.gov): NOT-OD-14-124: NIH Genomic Data Sharing Policy > NIH Funding Opportunities and Notices in the NIH Guide for Grants and Contracts: NIH Genomic Data Sharing Policy NOT-OD-14-124. NIH

Alan O’C (12:47:33): > Thanks!

2021-05-15

Belinda Phipson (04:56:49): > Hi! I’m not sure where to ask this question or who to ask, but I was wondering whether it is possible to obtain the number of monthly visitors to a particular package’s page? I’m trying to collate some “impact” statistics about the minfi and missMethyl packages.

Belinda Phipson (07:35:09) (in thread): > Thanks Kevin, I’ve already downloaded all those statistics (avg # distinct IPs per month, rank etc). I guess I was thinking more of whether traffic to package landing pages is tracked.

Martin Morgan (09:35:50) (in thread): > We don’t publish landing page views, sorry. The unique IP download traffic is probably useful in a relative way (my package 1 is downloaded more frequently than my package 2…) but still quite problematic for a measure of ‘use’ for a number of reasons. Web page traffic would I think be even more problematic.

2021-05-16

Belinda Phipson (01:49:43) (in thread): > Thanks for replying Martin. It feels like a tricky task to assess/estimate the user base of software packages.

2021-05-17

Jovana Maksimovic (00:37:35): > Hi all, when/what year did Bioconductor switch from SVN to git? My memory is failing me and my searches of the website aren’t helping.

Charlotte Soneson (01:10:05) (in thread): > I think the timeline is outlined in this bioc-devel thread:https://stat.ethz.ch/pipermail/bioc-devel/2017-August/011280.html

Jovana Maksimovic (01:13:15) (in thread): > Thanks Charlotte:smiley:

Dario Strbenac (02:00:14): > Does anyone have a script for spiltting a FASTQ file by lane and flowcell into separate FASTQ files? The service provider delivered 100x whole genome sequencing samples concatenated into one file per read side, per sample, but I want to have meaningful@RGIDs that we had from the previous sequencing service provider.

Sean Davis (09:37:15) (in thread): > I don’t think there is a standard for how read names are formatted, so you may have to create a quick script yourself. In Bioconductor, take a look atShortRead::FastqStreamer. Inside the yield, you can segregate reads and concatenate to separate files.

Alexander Toenges (09:57:14) (in thread): > Feel free to ask atbiostars.orgincl an example how data look like right now. My gutfeeling says that there is a command from BBtools that can do that efficiently.

Hervé Pagès (14:23:56) (in thread): > Also if you look at the commit history of a long time Bioconductor package likeBiobase, you’ll see that all the commits from the SVN era have agit-svn-idline at the bottom: > > git-svn-id: file:///home/git/hedgehog.fhcrc.org/bioconductor/trunk/madman/Rpacks/Biobase@129350 bc3139a8-67e5-0310-9ffc-ced21a209358 > > The last commit to have this line forBiobaseis commit 536f1371 from May 2, 2017. The next commit (from Oct 30, 2017) no longer has agit-svn-idline.

Henry Miller (15:05:07): > @Henry Miller has joined the channel

Krithika Bhuvanesh (17:22:23) (in thread): > Which machine is this ? I think Illumina may have a tool for this ? They have a tool to convert he BCL files into FASTQ files . You could also reach out to your service provider to provide the FASTQ files in the format you prefer.

Dario Strbenac (19:00:10) (in thread): > Thanks for good suggestions.FastqStreamermight be a convenient high-level function to use in Rscript. I have seena similar question with a similar answeron Biostars but I was not sure how to adapt Frédéric Mahé’s awk code to a more general case since I have only used awk a couple of times before and am very much a beginner. I think Illumina FASTQ files contain fairly standard read names. I checked with the service provider and they didn’t keep the separate FASTQ files, so they would need to convert BCL to FASTQ again and it’s for 100x coverage whole genome sequencing. I think I could split the files quicker than a BCL to FASTQ conversion would take.

2021-05-18

FelixErnst (12:24:05): > assay(se,"name") <- NULLwould be my guess

Aaron Lun (16:23:04): > What on earth happened to our the formatting of code chunks in our Rmarkdown vignettes? It looks hideous, e.g.,https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/vignettes/bluster/inst/doc/clusterRows.html

Aaron Lun (16:23:11): > is this a biocstyle problem?

2021-05-28

FelixErnst (03:17:26): > I don’t see any difference between 3.12 and 3.13. I also did go back to vignettes from other packages from 2019. They all look the same and my browser also doesn’t load external stylesheets…

Mike Smith (04:11:13): > Looks to me like the padding around code blocks has been removed e.g. 3.12 vs 3.13 - File (PNG): image.png

Mike Smith (04:12:54): > I suspect something has changed in the rmarkdown template, which tends to inject CSS into the header of the HTML and override our CSS. This happens semi-frequently e.g.https://github.com/Bioconductor/BiocStyle/issues/86

Aaron Lun (04:44:02): > I hope we can all agree that the one on the right is disgusting

Federico Marini (04:52:09): > It would save space when printing

Federico Marini (04:52:27): > Sometimes the ugliness is tightly related to not-being-used-to…

Lluís Revilla (04:55:13): > I actually like the more compacted one

Lluís Revilla (04:55:38): > I also use collapse = TRUE on my vignettes to “save” more space

Aaron Lun (04:56:33): > seriously guys? you think the one on the right is acceptable?!

Aaron Lun (04:56:51): > I can barely tell the difference between the code and the output

Aaron Lun (04:57:28): > especially when the commented output isn’t even in a different highlighting.

Aaron Lun (04:59:23): > it’s like saying that a circos plot is nice because it has the most information per pixel, that completely misses the point,

Aaron Lun (05:00:55): > strongly advise using knitr’s default athttps://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/knitr/vignettes/knitr-markdown.html, which is much more readable.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (05:30:14): > I do prefer the former one (on the left), which gives a bit more breathing space to the code chunks

Alan O’C (05:54:00): > Output being distinct from code is separate from the padding but I agree it would make things much easier to read

Sharanya Manoharan (06:29:35): > @Sharanya Manoharan has joined the channel

Lluís Revilla (06:34:53): > The output has ## but maybe some class associated with a color or other font or style can be applied to differentiate input and output

Federico Marini (07:55:32) (in thread): > same. Visually, it gives more air to the document. Also cause these html docs are not really born to be printed

Alan O’C (08:10:02): > Yeah the knitr example Aaron linked is much easier to read for me - File (PNG): knitr.png

Federico Marini (08:24:21) (in thread): > “but it is round, and the human eye likes it”

Federico Marini (08:25:10): > I also fancy the fact that one is italic and the other one isn’t

Federico Marini (08:25:41): > it’s a low-effort-hi-impact thing to make the doc accessible, probably

Federico Marini (08:26:05): > And in teaching you can say “when you see something in italic, it is an output”

Jonathan Carroll (09:59:37) (in thread): > (late to the party) FYI:https://github.com/jonocarroll/DFplyr

Henrik Bengtsson (14:16:36) (in thread): > FWIW, you can customize CSS specifically to print media, so can be different from on screen rendering

Sridhar N (11:56:28): > > > package.version('bioMart') > [1] "2.42.1" >

Sridhar N (11:56:57): > have been trying to access mm10 mart and not GRCm39 which gets loaded by default

Federico Marini (12:05:32): > It is down, along with the mirrors, it seems

Sridhar N (12:07:02): > Thanks for checking, is it odd that this won’t return any error

Sridhar N (12:07:03): > > m = useEnsembl("ensembl", > dataset="mmusculus_gene_ensembl", > host ="[http://aug2020.archive.ensembl.org](http://aug2020.archive.ensembl.org)",mirror = "uswest") >

Sridhar N (12:08:56): > i see that usinggetBMonmreturns this > > Error in getNodeSet(html, path = "//div[@class='plain-box float-right archive-box']")[[1]] : > subscript out of bounds >

Sridhar N (12:27:09): > ahh i do see an issue related to thishttps://support.bioconductor.org/p/9135007/#9135008

Dario Strbenac (22:00:20): > A student showed me an example of R consistently crashing. I don’t work with single-cell data, so I don’t have experience with this, but what’s the solution to this? > > > mat <- counts(sceATAC) > > class(mat) > [1] "dgTMatrix" > > keep <- Matrix::rowSums(mat == 0) > 1000 > > ***** caught segfault ***** > address 0x7f123daf63c0, cause 'memory not mapped' > > Traceback: > 1: .Call(dgeMatrix_colsums, x, na.rm, FALSE, FALSE) > 2: .local(x, na.rm, dims, ...) > 3: rowSums(x = x) > 4: rowSums(x = x) >

Dario Strbenac (22:05:36): > I think that addresses a different question. I think she’s looking for rows with at least 1000 zeros. > > > dim(mat) > [1] 190937 34774 >

Dario Strbenac (22:21:08): > temp <- mat == 0only uses 30 GB on a 768 GB RAM server and the error isn’t triggered there. ApplyingrowSumstriggers the error and only 103 GB is used with about 400 GB free. I think I can now make a reproducible example.

Chengyang Ji (23:56:09): > It says herehttps://stackoverflow.com/a/50163562the compiler incompabilities can cause segfault. SinceMatrixis a built-in package, maybe an upgrade could help? - Attachment (Stack Overflow): caught segfault - ‘memory not mapped’ error in R > I have a problem running some R scripts on our cluster. The problems appeared suddenly (all the scripts were working just fine but one day they started giving a caught segfault error). I cannot pro…

2021-06-07

Chengyang Ji (00:00:06): > The souce code for the culprit functiondgeMatrix_colsumslooks really innocent and couldn’t possibly go wrong (I think)https://github.com/cran/Matrix/blob/65c37738919156f6bbb682a1d8198d715a82a19f/src/dgeMatrix.c#L839

Stuart Lee (00:11:59) (in thread): > sparseMatrixStats::rowCountsmight be useful here

Alan O’C (05:26:13): > OOM has never segfaulted for me. I would tend towards updating packages as well, that usually fixes those memory not mapped errors when I’m working on the HPC

Kasper D. Hansen (07:35:29): > Running our of memoery is different from a segfault

Mike Smith (11:17:37): > I agree. If this was an out of memory error I’d expect to seeError: cannot allocate vector of size XX Gb

USLACKBOT (11:20:58): > This message was deleted.

Michael Lawrence (12:06:01): > Probably just integer overflow

Mike Smith (12:35:10) (in thread): > One option might be to dotemp <- as(mat, "lsparseMatrix")which I think creates a logical sparse matrix is the equivalent ofmat == 0and then doingsparseMatrixStats::rowSums2( temp ) > 1000. That way everything should stay sparse.

Hervé Pagès (13:14:18) (in thread): > A program cannot try to allocate memory that is not available. Memory allocation is done via system calls likemalloc(),calloc(),realloc(), and AFAIK these system calls won’t segfault if there’s not enough memory available. What they will do in this case is return a NULL pointer instead of the address of the allocated memory and it’s the responsibility of the program to check that. However poorly written C code doesn’t always check that and then tries to write to the NULL address which causes a segfault. So yeah, using a system with more memory will avoid running into that problem but the program is buggy nonetheless and should be fixed.

Henrik Bengtsson (14:31:11) (in thread): > Please report this to the maintainer ofMatrix(Martin Mächler), because this is a (serious) bug inMatrix- anything that causes R to segfault is a bug. Don’t forget to reportsessionInfo().

Dario Strbenac (17:00:06) (in thread): > I reported it to R-devel yesterday and I guess that he’ll see it if he’s not on holidays.

Mike Smith (08:30:21): > Well, he tweets a lot! But here were are:https://twitter.com/_ColinFay/status/934740425956569088?s=20Definitely not just the last two weeks. - Attachment (twitter): Attachment > @dataandme @juliasilge @drob This is the how it is written on the online R help :) > I find it helpful to make it clear I’m referring to a package :package: https://pbs.twimg.com/media/DPjddcpWsAE0NFh.jpg

GuandongShang (23:40:09): > Hi, I am writing @examples in my packages, and loading TxDb.Athaliana.BioMart.plantsmart28 in examples in every function. But I think each library(TxDb.Athaliana.BioMart.plantsmart28) will take some times, so I am wondering whether some one can give me some advice about it. > > my examples code like it > function 1 > > #' @examples > #' data("RNA_normCount") > #' data("ATAC_normCount") > #' > #' library(TxDb.Athaliana.BioMart.plantsmart28) > #' Txdb <- TxDb.Athaliana.BioMart.plantsmart28 > #' seqlevels(Txdb) <- c(paste0("Chr", 1:5), "M", "C") > > function 2 > > #' @examples > #' data("ATAC_normCount") > #' data("input_genes") > #' library(TxDb.Athaliana.BioMart.plantsmart28) > #' Txdb <- TxDb.Athaliana.BioMart.plantsmart28 > #' seqlevels(Txdb) <- c(paste0("Chr", 1:5), "M", "C") > #' > #' peak_path <- system.file("extdata", "ATAC.bed.gz", package = "FindIT2") > #' peak_GR <- loadPeakFile(peak_path) > #' > #' ChIP_peak_path <- system.file("extdata", "ChIP.bed.gz", package = "FindIT2") > #' ChIP_peak_GR <- loadPeakFile(ChIP_peak_path) > #' ChIP_peak_GR$TF_id <- "AT1G28300" >

2021-06-16

Dario Strbenac (02:00:09): > You shouldn’t immediately load packages in examples because data packages should be inSuggests:part of DESCRIPTION file and might not be installed on a user’s computer unless they did a comprehensive install. Examples should test if the package is available before doing some analysis. > > if(require(dataPackage)) > { > # Do analysis. > } > > See Section 2.8 ofPackage Development Guidelinesfor information.

Mike Smith (04:25:53): > I agree with Dario thatrequire()is more appropriate, and that you want the package load statement in each example to make it clear to the user what’s required. > > I think it’s also worth noting that when the examples are tested inR CMD checkthey’re actually all concatenated together into a single file before being run. So even if you have lots of repeatedrequire()orlibrary()calls across many examples, you don’t suffer from excessive load times during the package check process. > > You can verify this by taking a look in the<PKG>.Rcheckfolder produced byR CMD check, where there should be a file called<PKG>-Ex.Rthat contains all your examples.

GuandongShang (04:26:39) (in thread): > Thanks for your suggestion ! This is helpful. I am correcting it

GuandongShang (04:27:27) (in thread): > got it. Thanks for your reply, I will see the Rcheck.

Kasper D. Hansen (04:27:46): > You could consider the concatenation to be a bug however

GuandongShang (04:29:26): > I am sorry, what’s the meaning ofconcatenation to be a bug?

Kasper D. Hansen (04:33:30): > The fact that R - at the moment - concatenates all examples into a single R which is then executed, is something I claim you could consider a bug or at least not a great design. You can argue that having a fresh environment for each example is much better, but this will also be much slower

GuandongShang (04:35:51): > Thanks, I get it : ).

Kasper D. Hansen (04:36:13): > This is more of a meta comment. What concerns you is that repeated calls does not slow down checking - at the moment.

Kasper D. Hansen (04:36:32): > And you should follow therequireadvice

Mike Smith (05:27:29): > I think what I said previously holds true, but R does run the functioncleanEx()between each example. That function doesn’t seem to be documented anywhere (I think it’s thishttps://github.com/wch/r-source/blob/79298c499218846d14500255efd622b5021c10ec/share/R/examples-header.R#L37-L75), so it’s a bit mysterious. At first read it looks to me like it will remove created variables, and detach any loaded packages, but not unload them.

Kasper D. Hansen (05:38:28): > interesting

Heather Turner (08:18:12): > :wave:Hi Folks! We are looking for volunteers to serve as a Zoom Host at useR! 2021 (5-9 July). > > What does a Zoom Host do? Manage the running of talk or tutorial sessions, to take the technical burden off the session chair or tutor. You will work in pairs and be responsible for a certain time block between you (~8 hours). > > You will be given training by experienced community organizers in the week of June 21st. You need to be available to run conference rehearsals one week before the conference, at exactly the same time as during the conference. > > Since this is quite a commitment, Zoom Hosts are offereda stipend of USD 100for their help. We’re also happy to offer**** conference fee waivers**for volunteers if required (https://user2021.r-project.org/participation/faq/#waiver). > > Interested? Please fill in this doodle asap. It would be helpful if you fill it out with “your name - Bioconductor” to know which community you’re coming from:bioc:

Heather Turner (08:23:32): > We’re looking for ~50 hosts to cover the whole schedule, so please still respond even though several have already volunteered!

Hervé Pagès (12:55:48) (in thread): > I didn’t know about thecleanEx()feature and I wonder how long it’s been around. I remember seeing some packages with examples failing in the context ofR CMD checknot so long ago where trying to run the problematic examples interactively would not trigger the error. After some digging around, this situation turned out to be a side-effect ofR CMD checkconcatenating all the examples in one single file. These events are rare andcleanEx()probably helps making them even more rare but it’s not perfect either. More generally speaking I don’t think there’s an easy way to reset the state of an R session to a clean slate except by restarting R.

2021-06-17

To get this to work withshinyapps.io, as far as I can tell, we need to have a function that hands back the list(ui, server) that interactiveDisplayBase produces. 2021-06-21

Steve (05:55:13): > I built a site where to make it easy for clients or students to book you for consulting or R tuition by the hour. It’s called Hangswith. I’m cultivating a modern and intellectual culture (as opposed to other sites which can be ‘fluffy’ in nature). Pease check it out if you have a moment, feel free to create a profile if you feel so inclined! (it’s completely free to sign up / create a profile).https://www.hangswith.com/All feedback very welcome. I’m learning daily!

Sebastian Worms (06:02:43): > That’s sounds nice! I was considering signing up on upwork to do similar things. What do you see as your platform’s edge? Being more people focued and less task focused?

Steve (06:13:51): > That’s a great question, I’m figuring that out myself:slightly_smiling_face:I’d say the two strongest bits of feedback I’ve had so far is > 1. More honest/transparent pricing than similar sites, which hit both sides of the transaction with hidden fees. > 2. It’s been great in cases where someone receives a lot of inquiries, and ends up doing a lot of unpaid work (via email etc) - hangswith lets you take a booking straight over the web, and have it paid for by the client/student up front. In other words, instead of 10 inquiries leading to 1 hour of actual work, you only get notifiedaftersomeone’s already booked and paid. That puts more responsibility on the consultant/tutor, but relives them of all the rigmarole arranging it. Although they do have to send a zoom link, but that’s not a huge burden:slightly_smiling_face:

Marcel Ramos Pérez (09:58:43) (in thread): > Yes, I can look into this. One thing aboutshinyapps.iois that the monthly compute hours are limited so if the app gets quite some use, we may have to think about alternative solutions for hosting.

Marcel Ramos Pérez (12:28:36) (in thread): > see the source repository herehttps://github.com/LiNk-NY/AnnotationHubShiny

Darin Takemoto (15:52:57) (in thread): > With the disclaimer that I haven’t worked with Sanger sequencing data in over a decade, it would depend on what the issues you are having with variant calling and why. If it is detection of low-frequency variants that don’t fit the normal diploid model, there are variant callers made for cancer genomics and viral sequencing that can detect lower-frequency variants (this white paper suggests down to 5%):https://assets.thermofisher.com/TFS-Assets/LSG/Application-Notes/variant-detection-tumor-ffpe-samples-sanger-sequencing.pdf

Darin Takemoto (15:54:44) (in thread): > If the issue is more heterogeneity, one would have to deconvolute the trace, which is something I personally have never done. Here is a paper describing software to do this for ABI Sanger sequencing data:https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3557274/ - Attachment (PubMed Central (PMC)): Base-Calling Algorithm with Vocabulary (BCV) Method for Analyzing Population Sequencing Chromatograms > Sanger sequencing is a common method of reading DNA sequences. It is less expensive than high-throughput methods, and it is appropriate for numerous applications including molecular diagnostics. However, sequencing mixtures of similar DNA of pathogens …

Darin Takemoto (15:55:44) (in thread): > The BCV software package itself is here:http://bioinf.fbb.msu.ru:8080/bcviss/index/index.gsp

Wanmin Dai (01:08:24): > @Wanmin Dai has joined the channel

Dario Strbenac (02:00:26): > Have I been thinking about S4 method dispatch wrongly all these years? > > setGeneric("aClass", function(x, y, ...) {standardGeneric("aClass")}) > setMethod("aClass", numeric(0), function() {message("Empty")}) > setMethod("aClass", c("numeric", "character"), function(x, y = "G'day") {message("Both")}) > > aClass(9) > Empty > > How do I create the empty constructor for an S4 class that’s only dispatched if the constructor the user specifies isaClass()?

Alan O’C (03:23:22): > missing()?

Alan O’C (03:23:34): > or rather,"missing"?

Dario Strbenac (05:00:05): > Ah, true.

Hervé Pagès (09:18:29) (in thread): > > setMethod("aClass", c("missing", "missing"), function(x, y) message("Empty")) > > 2 notes: > 1. It’s better to not put curly brackets around the call tostandardGeneric(). This allows R to identify the function as astandard generic(with the curly brackets, R identifies the function as a non-standard generic). > 2. All your methods must at least have thexandyarguments. Also, for clarity, the signature of all the methods should be a length-2 character vector.

Jenny Drnevich (10:32:46): > Poll: how do you pronounce “tSNE”? Tiz-ny rhymes with Disney, or tee-sin-ee? I first learned the former but then saw 10X people saying the latter, but I’ve seen more of the former at Bioc2021. Anybody know if there is a definitive authority on the pronunciation?

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (10:35:13): > i think it should really be tee-ess-en-eee even though I tend to say tee-snee

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (10:36:38): > But I’ve heard every possible way I think, and I don’t know where to find the definitive authority:sweat_smile:

Jenny Drnevich (10:38:01): > I could abuse my authority and put up a poll in the Single Cell Genomics Talk but I think we will be short on time.

Alexander Toenges (10:38:03): > tee-snee, that is how the developer pronounces it, hear him at 5:08 ofhttps://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bCkXxaoeFZ8&t=308s - Attachment (YouTube): BioTuring Webinar | Using t SNE for Data Analysis: A Quick Introduction | Laurens van der Maaten

Jenny Drnevich (10:45:46): > Although I admit I find tiz-ny more fun and easier to say.:face_with_hand_over_mouth:

Kevin Blighe (10:58:39): > I usually say T S N E, but just now on the presentation I think that I said tiz-nee:expressionless:

Luke Zappia (11:07:56): > Hidden advantage of UMAP, much easier to pronounce. I am yet to hear someone say um-ap but it’s probably only a matter of time.

Jenny Drnevich (11:11:46): > Sadly, I think Bioconductor people will forever mispronounce BiocSwirl as “bio-cee” is hardwired into our brains. The developers pronounce it “bi-ock” swirl.

Jared Andrews (11:25:23): > I had never heard the “bio-cee” pronunciation until yesterday, I always went with “bi-ock”.

Jenny Drnevich (11:27:10): > Maybe it’s just me??? “bi-ock” is closer to “bio-konductor”

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:49:06): > … but then why is the C capitalised in BioC if not to pronounce bio-cee??:exploding_head:

Charlotte Soneson (11:56:26): > https://support.bioconductor.org/p/130998/

Kasper D. Hansen (11:57:29): > BioC is the conference

Kasper D. Hansen (11:59:13): > That is my impression of the standardization efforts.

Kasper D. Hansen (12:00:19): > You should use Bioc in package names like BiocParallel and Bioconductor when it’s spelled out.

I then launch RStudio to switch to any given project that depends on Bioconductor or not. I thought this was a clever fix! Do you see drawbacks?

Woo (08:12:24): > @Woo has joined the channel

Hervé Pagès (13:54:42) (in thread): > I’m curious what’s the benefits of having this dual setup. Is R 4.1 for M1 really that faster than R 4.1 for intel? Only drawback I see is that you need to download and install many packages twice.

Ambarish S. Ghatpande (14:39:45) (in thread): > It’s not such a big deal at all. I saw a few queries on SO and maybe the bioc support forum with people asking for workarounds. People with m1 macs shouldn’t feel like they can’t use Bioconductor, that’s all. > In terms of difference, the memory use seems to be quite different between the two versions The one needing Rosetta seems to hog much more of it. > Honestly I hadn’t thought of having to install many packages twice, although most R packages seem small in size compared to hard drive capacity.

Hervé Pagès (15:28:04) (in thread): > > People with m1 macs shouldn’t feel like they can’t use Bioconductor, that’s all. > Not sure why people with m1 macs would feel that way. Just to clarify Bioconductor is 100% compatible with m1 macs as long as you install R 4.1 for intel. > > I’m really surprised by your claim that R 4.1 for intel seems to hog much more memory. I would expect maybe a slight difference in speed, even though not something really significant. But a big difference in memory consumption is unexpected and hard to explain.

2021-08-11

Ambarish S. Ghatpande (10:53:00) (in thread): - File (PNG): Screen Shot 2021-08-11 at 10.44.49 AM.png - File (PNG): Screen Shot 2021-08-11 at 10.42.35 AM.png

Ambarish S. Ghatpande (10:57:31) (in thread): > the stats in the 2 screenshots above is what I meant. Note the memory used by the “session” under the x86 platform is close to 1 GB whereas the same under the aarch64 platform is ~ 226 MB. Not really sure we can compare speed since the most relevant packages (Bioconductor based) dont work un the aarch64 platform. Like I said, could be acrcane stuff, not really a big deal for a user like me.

Mónica Padilla Gálvez (19:56:03): > @Mónica Padilla Gálvez has joined the channel

2021-08-12

Hervé Pagès (14:46:15) (in thread): > Yes that’s a big difference! Interesting. It’s still hard for me to believe that this is only due to the different architectures or to a Rosetta inefficiency. I can see a lot of discussion and benchmarks on internet for Intel+Rosetta vs M1 but didn’t see anything about difference in memory consumption. This tends to suggest that maybe what you see is an R/RStudio thing even though I didn’t see anything like this reported on the R-SIG-Mac mailing list (didn’t check the RStudio mailing lists/forums). Could it be that something changed between R 4.1.0 and R 4.1.0 Patched r80661 that explains this? It would certainly deserve a little bit more investigation.

2021-08-15

rohitsatyam102 (11:09:39): > @Levi Waldron@Markus RiesterI came across HGNChelper package today and I was thinking if there is an extensive vignette that demonstrate usage of all functions except this one:https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/HGNChelper/vignettes/index.html

Ava Hoffman (she/her) (11:31:28): > @Ava Hoffman (she/her) has joined the channel

Levi Waldron (17:32:13) (in thread): > That’s all there is, but the pkgdown page (https://waldronlab.io/HGNChelper/) does also have the man pages for the other functions. I think the other functions besides checkGeneSymbols are mostly of historical curiosity, but they also seem harmless enough not to warrant deprecation. I could be convinced to add something to the vignette or to deprecate them if you want to make a case for it… - Attachment (waldronlab.io): Identify and Correct Invalid HGNC Human Gene Symbols and MGI Mouse Gene Symbols > Contains functions for identifying and correcting HGNC human gene symbols and MGI mouse gene symbols which have been converted to date format by Excel, withdrawn, or aliased. Also contains functions for reversibly converting between HGNC symbols and valid R names.
