Stephanie Hicks (06:46:35) (in thread): > Updates in this spacehttps://twitter.com/arfon/status/1428395832030683139 - Attachment (twitter): Attachment > Thanks for all the feedback over the last few weeks :sparkles:. Since @natfriedman’s tweet a few weeks ago, we’ve been making a number of improvements including detecting BibTeX files, R (inst/CITATION) files, and citing papers. Check out the docs to learn more: docs.github.com/en/github/crea… twitter.com/github/status/… - Attachment (twitter): Attachment > We’ve added enhanced citation support so academics and researchers can easily receive acknowledgments for their contributions to software with citation links in the common APA or BibTeX styles. > > github.blog/2021-08-19-enh…

Jenny Drnevich (11:14:40): > Came across this tutorial that almost exclusively uses->instead of<-. It’s breaking my brain:confounded:https://www.bioinformatics.babraham.ac.uk/training/10XRNASeq/seurat_workflow.html

Kevin Blighe (11:27:20) (in thread): > Never even seen that before!

Jenny Drnevich (11:28:23) (in thread): > I knew that->existed but never really saw it in practice before.

James MacDonald (11:29:48) (in thread): > It’s a logical extension of mgrittr (sp?) pipes, where you do > > this %>% that %>% the_other > > And the result is the last thing piped thataway ->

Jenny Drnevich (11:35:47) (in thread): > I agree that it’s a logical extension and even teach it as a “fun fact” in my intro R when I teach pipes. But then I say the convention it to always put the assignment at the beginning of the line of code.

James MacDonald (11:35:55) (in thread): > Although if you want to go full tidyverse, isn’t it really > > huh %<>% this %>% that %>% the_other >

James MacDonald (11:36:34) (in thread): > Which I believe pipes everything back into thehuhobject? Obviously?

Jenny Drnevich (11:36:42) (in thread): > Never go full tidyverse!

James MacDonald (11:37:01) (in thread): > No, I’ve seen Tropic Thunder. I know better than that.

Alan O’C (11:57:29) (in thread): > > apply( > mat, > 2, > function(x) stuff(x) > ) -> variable >

Alan O’C (11:57:48) (in thread): > That is so cursed

Martin Morgan (12:04:18) (in thread): > x <- 2 -> yis also fun for this — kind of splitting a number between two variables, like the atom…

Alan O’C (12:05:06) (in thread): > I prefer to do2 -> x <- 3:laughing:

Simina Boca (13:36:07) (in thread): > I wonder if folks who write right-to-left have a preference? (eg Hebrew, Arabic, Farsi speakers/writers)

Lluís Revilla (14:51:07) (in thread): > The error means that that file is missing. Did you check visually if on that folder do you have your file aug3.1_Ptep_genome.gft?

Rosalina Tincopa Marca (16:38:02) (in thread): > Thank you Lluís, yes you are right. I made a mistake and I put in the Rscript gft instead of gtf as the format of the file is

Lluís Revilla (16:40:35) (in thread): > Great! You found the solution:muscle:

2021-09-08

Mikhail Dozmorov (12:47:00): > I may be missing something obvious, but why the following regexp returns TRUE for the second string? > > strings <- c('abd', 'dabc', 'abcabc') > grepl("a*d", strings) > #> [1] TRUE TRUE FALSE > > And why the following gives all TRUEs? > > grepl("d*a", strings) > #> [1] TRUE TRUE TRUE > > Happened during the live demo in class, and I have no explanation why.

Aaron Lun (12:48:37): > well, the last string doesn’t have ad, but all strings have ana.

Mikhail Dozmorov (13:01:03) (in thread): > I considered “d*a” regexp as a substring starting with “d” and ending with “a”, with any characters in between. Is it not correct?

Alan O’C (13:01:44) (in thread): > d*ameans “zero or more instances of d, followed by one a”

Alan O’C (13:02:40) (in thread): > d.*ais “d, then zero or more of any character, then a”

Mikhail Dozmorov (13:03:33) (in thread): > Ah, the “zero” part is where I blanked:slightly_smiling_face:Lessons learned. All correct, thanks,@Alan O’C!

Alan O’C (13:04:18) (in thread): > No worries, behaviour of*is a bit different in regex vs globs:slightly_smiling_face:

Sean Davis (13:13:06) (in thread): > Great thread. Thanks!

Marcel Ramos Pérez (13:31:56) (in thread): > “starting with d and ending with a with any characters in between” corresponds to"^d.*a$"

Alan O’C (14:39:45) (in thread): > In case anybody is bored, I find this fun (I’m stuck at abba, though)https://alf.nu/RegexGolf

Mikhail Dozmorov (14:59:35) (in thread): > Added to my references: > * Curated Regular Expression Resources > * Learn regex the easy way > * All about Regular Expressions > * Preview your regex > * Play Regex Golf

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (10:14:54): > Writing a side point about bioconductor in a lesson. Does anyone remember what happens upon acceptance of a new package that was submitted with a major version different from 0 ? Does it get incremented by 1 or is the version forced to 1.0.0 ?

Laurent Gatto (10:22:13): > As far as I know, new submissions should always be 0.99.z and any updates increment z.

Kasper D. Hansen (10:25:41): > If you’re teaching this, I think the lesson is this: if you want to use Bioc just accept and use the package version numbering scheme we use. There are many philosophies around version numbers, but forget about it and just embrace the Bioconductor way. Specifically, you should NOT follow the recommendations in “R Packages” by Wickham.

Martin Morgan (10:41:29): > I thinkx.99.zgets added to devel as-is, and bumped tox+1.0.0at the release. Package versions can never go backward, so if say your package had been on CRAN or GitHub or otherwise distributed at version 3.1-2 your only choice is3.99.0… If you use0.99.0theninstall.packages()(which is whatBiocManager::install()uses) would say ’oh, there’s a newer version installed, so I won’t update.

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (10:45:39): > thanks both

Laurent Gatto (11:34:30) (in thread): > Given the name requirement, would it even be possible to submit a CRAN package to Bioconductor?

Martin Morgan (12:35:56) (in thread): > Usually it’s moving a CRAN package to Bioconductor, which involves submitting / review / accepting under Bioconductor standards, then notifying CRAN to remove the package. So it’s available in CRAN but not Bioconductor before acceptance by Bioconductor, and Bioconductor but not CRAN after acceptance. > > This is how it works in practice, but I wouldn’t say that I endorse changing repositories as a good thing to do.

Vince Carey (11:07:49): > pretty quiet here. back to regular expressions? given > > > names(cs) > [1] "Case ID" "Secondary \nor Recurrent" > [3] "Age at \nProcedure" "Gender" > [5] "Path Dx" "MGMT Status" > [7] "2012 \nMethylation\n Class" "G-CIMP\n methylation" > [9] "IDH1\n status" "Expression\nSubclass" > [11] "Therapy Class" "Vital Status" > [13] "OS (days)" "Progression Status" > [15] "PFS (days)" "V16" > > produce > > [1] "Case_ID" "Secondary_or_Recurrent" "Age_at_Procedure" > [4] "Gender" "Path_Dx" "MGMT_Status" > [7] "2012_Methylation_Class" "G_CIMP_methylation" "IDH1_status" > [10] "Expression_Subclass" "Therapy_Class" "Vital_Status" > [13] "OS_days" "Progression_Status" "PFS_days" > [16] "V16" > > My solution involves 2 gsubs.

Alan O’C (11:11:36) (in thread): > I would probably justgsubonmake.names, although that’s probably not in the spirit of the question

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:20:41) (in thread): > @Vince Carey: Can you give the output ofcat(deparse(names(cs)))so that we can recreate the vector easily in an R session please?

Alan O’C (11:21:25) (in thread): > Aye, I was gonna suggestdput

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:21:54) (in thread): > ohh… didn’t know that one!

Vince Carey (11:22:00): > > c("Case ID", "Secondary \nor Recurrent", "Age at \nProcedure", > "Gender", "Path Dx", "MGMT Status", "2012 \nMethylation\n Class", > "G-CIMP\n methylation", "IDH1\n status", "Expression\nSubclass", > "Therapy Class", "Vital Status", "OS (days)", "Progression Status", > "PFS (days)", "V16") >

Vince Carey (11:25:34) (in thread): > @Alan O’CI hadn’t thought of make.names … it is certainly useful but produces lots of dots.

Alan O’C (11:28:45) (in thread): > Yeah, the lazy way of sanitising is to dogsub("\\.+", "_", make.names(x)), although that leaves some trailing_and some leadingXs

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:29:44) (in thread): > with magrittr I got super close, with the issue of the prefixXintroduced bymake.names(): > > make.names(x) %>% gsub("^.", "", .) %>% gsub(".$", "", .) %>% gsub("[.]+", "_", .) > > There is only one failure for this approach: the field “2012_Methylation_Class” because it gets an “X” prefix

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:30:31) (in thread): > my issue with that is that I’m not so keen to trim X’s from the left, since some variables might have a genuine X there

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:32:26) (in thread): > hold that though, I got an idea

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:34:39) (in thread): > arf - not yet, maybe with a bit more time:sweat_smile:

Marcel Ramos Pérez (11:36:27): > gsub("\\s\\((\\w+)\\)|(\\s\\(**|\\s**\\\n\\s*)|(-)", "_\\1", names(cs))

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:39:43) (in thread): > OK, so… I wish I could say “tadaaaa”:tada:but I’ll admit that it is fairly convoluted: > > x %>% > paste0("_", .) %>% > make.names() %>% > gsub("^[.]", "", .) %>% > gsub("[.]$", "", .) %>% > gsub("[.]+", "_", .) %>% > gsub("^X_", "", .) >

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:41:19) (in thread): > Happy to explain the reason for individual bits if needed. I can’t say that I’d be as happy to go down the “single regex” road for that one:laughing:

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:44:06) (in thread): > why do I getcharacter(0)when I run that?

Marcel Ramos Pérez (11:45:15) (in thread): > You can try it on > > cs <- c("Case ID", "Secondary \nor Recurrent", "Age at \nProcedure", > "Gender", "Path Dx", "MGMT Status", "2012 \nMethylation\n Class", > "G-CIMP\n methylation", "IDH1\n status", "Expression\nSubclass", > "Therapy Class", "Vital Status", "OS (days)", "Progression Status", > "PFS (days)", "V16") >

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:45:47) (in thread): > > > cs <- c("Case ID", "Secondary \nor Recurrent", "Age at \nProcedure", > + "Gender", "Path Dx", "MGMT Status", "2012 \nMethylation\n Class", > + "G-CIMP\n methylation", "IDH1\n status", "Expression\nSubclass", > + "Therapy Class", "Vital Status", "OS (days)", "Progression Status", > + "PFS (days)", "V16") > > gsub("\\s\\((\\w+)\\)|(\\s\\(**|\\s**\\\n\\s*)|(-)", "_\\1", names(cs)) > character(0) >

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:45:55) (in thread): > weird, right?

Marcel Ramos Pérez (11:46:34) (in thread): > No, there are no names. I was trying to emulate the original example. But you can also do > > cs <- setNames(nm = cs) >

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:47:15) (in thread): > omg, i’m blind:sweat_smile:

Kevin Rue-Albrecht (11:47:32) (in thread): > working now ^^

Vince Carey (11:54:02): > Wow. I need to study that@Marcel Ramos Pérez. > > > all.equal( gsub("\\s\\((\\w+)\\)|(\\s\\(**|\\s**\\\n\\s*)|(-)", "*\\1", names(cs)), gsub("\\(|\\)", "", gsub("( )**(\\n| |-)( )**", "*", names(cs)))) > [1] TRUE >

Martin Morgan (13:31:09): > sub("*$", "", gsub("[[:space:][:punct:]]+", "*", cs)); these ‘character classes’ are described in?regexand can improve readability — match one or more characters from the set of characters including[:space:]or[:punct:]; the outersub()replaces trailing_in entries likeOS (days)there’s only one end of each entry, sosub()instead ofgsub(). A variant isgsub("[[:space:]]+", "_", gsub("[[:punct:]]+", "", cs))Hmm, and taking@Michael Lawrence’s suggestion to heart, might as well make the intentions explicit, at least in english > > remove_punctuation <- function(x) > gsub("[[:punct:]]+", "", x) > > replace_whitespace <- function(x, with) > gsub("[[:space:]]+", with, x) > > cs |> > remove_punctuation() |> > replace_whitespace(with = "_") >

Michael Lawrence (13:31:44) (in thread): > I think it would be better code to be more explicit about the intent, i.e., usingstrsplit()andunstrsplit().

Peter Hickey (16:16:23) (in thread): > cheating by using a package, butjanitor::clean_names()is great for this sort of task (https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/janitor/versions/1.2.0/topics/clean_names)

takashi watanabe (09:07:22): > @takashi watanabe has joined the channel

Martin Morgan (10:17:28) (in thread): > bummer, I guess one loses the use of[:punct:]and gains the use of escaped parentheses\\( > > remove_parentheses <- function(x) > gsub("[\\(\\)]+", "", x) > > replace_whitespace_and_dash <- function(x, with) > gsub("[-[:space:]]+", with, x) > ## alternative pattern: "[- \n]+" > > cs |> > remove_parentheses() |> > replace_whitespace_and_dash(with = "_") > > Also using the stringr package > > cs |> > str_remove_all("[\\(\\)]") |> > str_replace_all("[- \n]+", "_") > > I guess this is just Vince’s two-gsub solution with maybe a little cleaner(?) regular expressions…

Martin Morgan (10:38:53) (in thread): > I wonder if using R string literalsr"{...}"would make this any more readable? > > > re = r"{\s\((\w+)\)|(\s\(**|\s**\n\s*)|(-)}" > > gsub(re, "_\\1", names(cs)) > > Maybe not!

Gabe Mednick (15:56:39): > Hi folks, > It’s an honor to be here with so many legends of Bioconductor! > I have a question regarding theyeastNagalakshmipackage: > I am trying to create an RNA-seq post using the said data package. I used the yeastTxDbobject to get exons by gene and then used theGenomicAlignments::summarizeOverlaps()to get gene counts. My question is what should the meta data and design arguments look like if I am trying to useDESeq2::DESeqDataSetFromMatrix()? Please correct me if I am not thinking about this in the right way. Also, are there any example posts where theyeastNagalakshmipackage is used? Much thanks in advance.

Marcel Ramos Pérez (19:49:16) (in thread): > I was going for the one liner:stuck_out_tongue:but if it had to go in package code, I would go with your option of making it more readable. The sequence in my mind would be to first remove unwanted characters (\nand()) and then replace all spaces and dashes with_. Thanks for providing a more legible solution

Is this intended? > > Example: > > > fun <- function(v) { > + message("working") ## 10 tasks > + sqrt(v) > + } > > bplapply(1:10, fun, BPPARAM=MulticoreParam(workers=2, progressbar=TRUE)) > iteration: 1working > > 2working > > 3working > > 4working > > 5working > > 6working > > 7working > > 8working > > 9working > > 10working > > ## here comes the result > > Same happens forBPPARAM = SerialParam(progressbar=TRUE)

Francesc Català (06:43:56): > @Francesc Català has left the channel

Chris Vanderaa (10:36:21): > Do you have an explanation for this? > > > any(logical(0)) > [1] FALSE > > all(logical(0)) > [1] TRUE > > I’m completely confused:exploding_head:: > 1. How can an empty vector return TRUE/FALSE? I would have expectedNULLor at leastNA > 2. The above also holds fornumeric(0)(ok, R can interpret/coerce numeric to logical) andcharacter(0)(:dizzy_face:) > 3. How can all be TRUE if any is FALSE ?

Alan O’C (10:43:40) (in thread): > From the documentation, it’s a greedy function. > > The value returned is ‘TRUE’ if all of the values in ‘x’ are ‘TRUE’ (including if there are no values), and ‘FALSE’ if at least one of the values in ‘x’ is ‘FALSE’.

Alan O’C (10:45:33) (in thread): > ie it’s doing something close to!any(!x)

Chris Vanderaa (10:48:30) (in thread): > Ok thanks for the pointer, I hadn’t caught that in the docs! In practice, I find this still very confusing. Luckily, I rarely deal with empty vectors:smile:

Alan O’C (10:49:29) (in thread): > Yeah it’s a nasty one for sure! Length zero (or zero row/column) objects are an endless source of errors

Martin Morgan (15:48:38) (in thread): > I think it’s deeper than a help page or greedy function definition.any()andall()are union and intersection operations on sets of (e.g.,) logical values, respectively. A set containing the single valueTRUE(henceall(TRUE) == TRUE) can be written as the union of the empty set and the setTRUE, soall(union of empty set and TRUE) == TRUE. But for that to be the case, it must be thatall(empty set) == TRUE. It is analogous to role played by 0 in arithmetic operations+and*on, e.g., non-negative integers,0 + 1 == 1and`0 * 1 = 0``

2021-11-27

Chris Vanderaa (17:39:44) (in thread): > OK that makes sense! Haha, this is yet another example where logic and (my) intuition disagree:laughing:. Thanks for the demonstration, I will sleep a little less ignorant tonight:wink:

Castro Mbithi (02:28:51): > @Castro Mbithi has joined the channel

Castro Mbithi (02:39:01): > I wanted to share a little piece we wrote as part of this whole fixed price vs hourly struggle that affects remote work. > > We used to hire developers on Upwork a lot in the past. We always had trouble deciding whether to pick “fixed price” or “hourly”. Fixed price is alluring since you only pay for quality work that you are satisfied with. But, it’s unfair to developers since estimating time precisely is a notoriously difficult thing to do. Moreover, we often found ourselves in the position where we kept having to add little bonuses to keep developers motivated to stick to a project rather than abandon it. So, we switched to hiring people by the hour. But we found developers were annoyed that the time tracking software was capturing their screen. And, some developers tracked exaggerated amounts of time to game the system. > > Inspired by the Pomodoro technique, we came up with a new technique of tracking time, that works well with our existing tools, namely Slack. I wanted to share the idea and get the community’s feedback on it:https://blog.learningdollars.com/2020/03/31/developer-time-tracking-using-slack-beyond-fixed-price-vs-hourly/ - Attachment (LD Talent Blog): Developer Time Tracking using Slack - beyond Fixed Price vs Hourly > Slack Work Sessions are a novel method for Developer Time Tracking. It resolves 2 issues: Developers prefer hourly pay, but clients want to pay for results.

Castro Mbithi (02:39:35) (in thread): > The second half of this video talks about how to execute projects on tight budgets - I hope it helps:https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iuEKqJbm9O8(9 mins @ 2X speed) - in particular pay attention to the slack based work sessions for transparency part. The “transparent work sessions” part of the GIF is the TL;DR. - Attachment (YouTube): LD Talent - Find and Manage Vetted Remote Developers - Stanford Team Disrupts Upwork and Recruiters - File (GIF): aldgif-2FPS.gif

2021-12-08

Sean Davis (13:31:48): > https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/publication/the-influence-of-organizational-structure-on-software-quality-an-empirical-case-study/ - Attachment (Microsoft Research): The Influence of Organizational Structure On Software Quality: An Empirical Case Study - Microsoft Research > Often software systems are developed by organizations consisting of many teams of individuals working together. Brooks states in the Mythical Man Month book that product quality is strongly affected by organization structure. Unfortunately there has been little empirical evidence to date to substantiate this assertion. In this paper we present a metric scheme to quantify […]

Hervé Pagès (14:09:19) (in thread): > From a friend of mine who worked on Windows Vista at the time: “Windows Vista was intentionally engineered to perform poorly on the average PC of the time to force people into buying new machines. It was a deal between Microsoft and some hardware manufacturers.” Too bad those guys didn’t factor this into their study on software quality:laughing:

Leonardo Collado Torres (23:40:30): > https://twitter.com/paleogenomics/status/1468799893779238914?s=21 - Attachment (twitter): Attachment > Today we filed the response to the lawsuit that the sexual harasser in this story filed against me (yes, he sued me for defamation!). This fight has a long road ahead, but I’m hopeful that justice will be served & look forward to the day I can go back to be in peace. #MeToo 1/n https://twitter.com/paleogenomics/status/1443769016854659072 - Attachment (twitter): Attachment > This story by @emiliano_mega in @ScienceMagazine is finally out. It describes my experience confronting a sexual harasser, along with that of other brave scientists who were victims/survivors. 1/6 > https://www.science.org/content/article/women-accuse-famed-plant-geneticist-sexual-harassment

Leonardo Collado Torres (23:41:24): > https://twitter.com/paleogenomics/status/1468799897210134531?s=21 - Attachment (twitter): Attachment > For those who have asked how to support, my extraordinary friends and colleagues @daniela_oaks and @mgschiavon put together this fundraiser to help cover my legal costs: > https://donadora.org/campanas/help-maria 2/n

Krithika Bhuvanesh (12:11:16): > Hello everyone ! If you have an interest inHEALTH DATA SCIENCE ,this one-year career-ready Masters program may be of interest to you. We offer part time and full time options. More details and our brochure below:The ******Health Informatics and Data Science (HIDS)******* Masters program at *******Georgetown University******* is designed to help students develop core competencies in data science, health-related data, predictive analytics, machine learning (ML), Artificial Intelligence (AI), and other advanced technologies. Through the program, students will be prepared to successfully take on a wide range of opportunities in health care organizations and related industries including, health technology developers, device manufacturers, pharma/biotech, academic medical centers, and management consulting firms. We help our students build critical skills for this growing sector to lead the evolution of healthcare.*Join one of our virtual information sessions in the coming weeks and talk to us about how the Georgetown-HIDS program fits into your academic and professional goals. > February 25, 10:00 am ESThttps://buff.ly/3KXLrsj - Attachment (gradapply.georgetown.edu): Georgetown University Health Informatics & Data Science Virtual Info Session > - File (PDF): 2022_Georgetown-HIDS_Brochure_sharing.pdf

Kozo Nishida (18:09:21): > Is there a full-text search system (like Google) for******all******Bioconductor vignettes? > I tried to see if the search box at the top right ofhttps://bioconductor.org/had that feature, but it didn’t seem to be the case.

Lluís Revilla (18:21:02): > Perhaps this is helpfulhttps://code.bioconductor.org/search/? - Attachment (code.bioconductor.org): Bioconductor Code: Search > Search source code across all Bioconductor packages

Kozo Nishida (18:45:12): > Thank you for information. However, it seemed that only the code was searched. - File (PNG): image.png

Lluís Revilla (19:17:09): > You can restrict to file type/folder such as usingfile:vignettes/* file:Rmd metagenomic microbiome species

Kozo Nishida (19:30:55): > Thank you for information. But it doesn’t seem to return Google(page-rank?)-like search results. > It seems difficult to use it instead of a full-text search system. - File (PNG): image.png

Alan O’C (19:33:51): > If you want google results you can probably use google to searchcode.bioconductor.org. I say “probably” but I mean “definitely”

Kozo Nishida (19:41:56): > Do you intend to search below? > This did not return any search results. - File (PNG): image.png

Kozo Nishida (19:52:05): > If we want to do a full-text search in natural language, we may need some kind of intelligent system. > (And Bioconductor vignettes contain a lot of useful information, so it may be worth considering it.)

Kozo Nishida (19:56:01): > I thought it fromthis. - Attachment: Attachment > @mirna pathview is not in metagenomics biocViews, but it may be suitable for you. I found the following in the vignette of pathview. > Notebaly, it can be directly used for metagenomic, microbiome or unknown species data when the data are mapped to KEGG ortholog pathways (Subsection 8.5). > I think there are other cases like this, so it would be nice to have some advice from other people.

Alan O’C (20:10:11): > Odd, maybe can be indexed

Alan O’C (20:10:35): > If you’re looking for a string like that, you should quote it, otherwise you’re searching the individual words

Alan O’C (20:11:01): > iefile:vignettes/* file:Rmd "the sentence I'm looking for"

Alan O’C (20:14:20): > ah, interesting. Even with quotes it doesn’t like linebreaks either.

2022-02-11

Kozo Nishida (02:34:24): > Thank you for information. > But beginners may find it difficult to do a pro-like search. > (There are many things to consider, such as that Rmd is not the only text source for vignette. etc.)

Mike Smith (05:25:34): > You can combined search terms using regular expression syntax e.g. to find files that have extension .Rmd or .Rnw you can usefile:(Rmd|Rnw)$

Kozo Nishida (05:59:48): > Thank you. However, the problem I feel is not only that, but there is no system for natural language search that users can use without explicitly specifying it. > (It’s like we don’t use Google that way.)

Kozo Nishida (06:14:43): > @Mike SmithWhat is used for full-text search oncode.bioconductor.org? > Is it using a full-text search engine like Elasticsearch/Lucene/Solr or something? > If such a full-text search engine is already in use, I feel that I have said something impossible. > If not, I would like to create a full-text search system for vignette pdf and html. (not for code.) > (This is hope, and I doubt if I can make time for it …)

Mike Smith (07:05:52) (in thread): > The code search is powered byhttps://github.com/google/zoektI haven’t made any modifications to the tool, just added a new theme to the results, but if you can see that it does what you want with some tweaks or alternative settings then I’m happy to update the site.

Sean Davis (14:48:56) (in thread): > Just a note that the Search Box on the Bioconductor website does search all the stuff on the Bioconductor website. The search string you used,@Kozo Nishida, pulls up the pdf vignette as the top hit. (http://bioconductor.org/help/search/index.html?q=Notebaly%2C+it+can+be+directly+used[…]data+are+mapped+to+KEGG+ortholog+pathways+(Subsection+8.5)./)

Kozo Nishida (15:15:42) (in thread): > Exactly. Thank you for letting me know. > Sorry, I may have misrepresented the search accuracy.

rohitsatyam102 (16:18:50): > Hi everyone. I was trying to make a table withformattablein R but couldn’t get rid ofNAas converting NA to zeros will makex>0condition true thereby adding:arrow_up:arrow to these cells. Any thoughts?? > > formattable(tt, list( > area(col = clus_6:clus_2) ~ formatter( > "span", > style = x ~ style(color = ifelse(x < 0 , "red", "green")), > x ~ icontext(ifelse(x < 0, "arrow-down", "arrow-up"), x)) > ) > - File (PNG): image.png

Gene Cutler (16:27:31) (in thread): > Instead ofifelse, try using a 3-case test: > > case_when( > x < 0 ~ "arrow-down", > x > 0 ~ "arrow-up", > is.na(x) | x == 0 ~ "" > ) >

Gene Cutler (16:28:29) (in thread): > I don’t know if the empty string is what you want for the blank, so you can play with that.

2022-02-22

rohitsatyam102 (02:27:43) (in thread): > Wow, it worked like magic. I almost forgot aboutcase_whencoz I useifelse/if/forstatements in routine and using multiple if else here was only evaluating first two conditions here. Many many thanks for saving the day. Here is the code that worked > and here is how the output looks like. > > formattable(tt, list( > area(col = clus_6:clus_2) ~ formatter( > "span", > style = x ~ style(color = case_when( > x < 0 ~ "red", > x > 0 ~ "green", > x == 0 ~ "white" > )), > x ~ icontext(case_when( > x < 0 ~ "arrow-down", > x > 0 ~ "arrow-up", > is.na(x) | x == 0 ~ "" > ), x)) > ) > - File (PNG): image.png

2022-02-23

Susanna (12:06:30): > Hi! Kind of a long shot, but does anyone know of a method to predict whether a regulatory variant impact gene expression?

Jared Andrews (12:09:56): > Tough question, but funseq2 may be useful to you:http://funseq2.gersteinlab.org/

Susanna (12:13:39) (in thread): > Thanks for the suggestion, but unfortunately funseq2 is for cancer data only and that’s not what I’m working on

Jared Andrews (12:15:32) (in thread): > Well, kind of a few steps removed, but you could consider using something like motifbreakR to see if a variant impacts TF motifs, which may help prioritize those that disrupt regulatory potential.https://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/motifbreakR.html

Susanna (12:20:44) (in thread): > Yes, that’s a good one. I’ve used it already but wondered if there was something else I could try

Aaron Lun (12:20:43): > hm. if you have the environment object, you could dobasilisk:::.obtainEnvironmentPathto get the path, and from there you should be able to find and execute the relevant binaries. If you’re lucky you won’t have to do any environment activation, otherwise you’ll have to protect yoursystemcalls withactivateEnvironmentanddeactivateEnvironment.

Jovan Isma (08:17:58): > @Jovan Isma has joined the channel

Susanna (08:25:55): > Hi there! I’m working on gene expression data and wonder if anyone know how to convert RPKM to TPM? Thank you!

Alexandru-Ioan Voda (09:01:55) (in thread): > It’s probably easier to convert raw read counts to TPM? Do you only have access to RPKM?

Rob Patro (09:16:41): > TPM is just normalized RPKM * 1000000, so TPM_i = ( RPKM_i / sum_j RPKM_j ) * 1000000.

Rob Patro (09:17:26): > Of course, there is the standard question of why you specifically want to convert RPKM to TPM here.

Susanna (09:52:43) (in thread): > Yes, I’m afraid I only have RPKM

Alexandru-Ioan Voda (10:02:49) (in thread): > sorry I was mistaken when I said “easier”, Rob Patro’s answer is correct and is just as easy as anything else

Alexandru-Ioan Voda (10:03:15) (in thread): > Good luck with work:four_leaf_clover:^_^

Artem Sokolov (10:31:38): > Keep in mind that if your gene expression data was collected by a 3’ sequencing method [e.g.,https://www.nature.com/articles/nmeth.f.376], you do NOT want to normalize by gene length. 3’-seq methods produce a single read per transcript, and normalization will skew gene expression values.

Alexandru-Ioan Voda (10:36:05) (in thread): > Although I assume that if Susanna had access to some RPKM matrix from public sources (since there was no access to raw counts, maybe just BAMs), that the sources didn’t publish an RPKM-norm of 3’-seq.

Susanna (13:00:19) (in thread): > Thank you, Artem. > Correct Alexandru:+1:

Alexandru-Ioan Voda (15:37:37): > Hi all! Curious, is there a package or suggested-R-function that can simulate having lower sequencing depth (lower library size, by subsampling), given a count matrix?

Marc Elosua (15:46:47) (in thread): > Maybe you can tryDropletUtilsdownsampleReads()!

Peter Hickey (17:38:46) (in thread): > scuttle::downsampleMatrix()will do what you describe

2022-03-29

2022-04-05

Alan Murphy (07:41:31): > Hey! Apologies but not sure where to ask this question regarding reference genome SNP databases on Bioconductor: > I’m the creator of theMungeSumstatspackage on Bioconductor for the standardisation and processing of GWAS sumstats. I currently use Bioconductor packages derived from dbSNP to infer missing RS IDs. I use SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP144.GRCh38 and SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP144.GRCh37 dependent on the genome build. Does anyone know if there are later versions of dbSNP available than 144 on Bioconductor? I know there is SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP151.GRCh38 for GRCh38 but I can’t seem to find similar for GRCh37? Also how will these different versions compare to other reference genome datasets in Bioconductor (I also use BSgenome.Hsapiens.1000genomes.hs37d5, BSgenome.Hsapiens.NCBI.GRCh38 for different checks)? For example, is BSgenome.Hsapiens.NCBI.GRCh38 up-to-date with SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP151.GRCh38?

Vince Carey (08:49:15): > @Hervé Pagès^^

2022-04-06

Martin Rodriguez Nuñez (16:24:36): > @Martin Rodriguez Nuñez has joined the channel

2022-04-07

Hervé Pagès (10:26:15) (in thread): > Hi Alan, > I started to look into building new SNPlocs packages for dbSNP155. Unfortunately I can no longer use my scripts for this because they are relying on the ASN.1 files that dbSNP no longer provides. Now they provide JSON and VCF files instead:https://ftp.ncbi.nih.gov/snp/latest_release/(Note that they made that switch about 4 years ago, around dbSNP152, seehttps://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/mailman/pipermail/dbsnp-announce/2018q4/000193.html) > The JSON files are huge and hard to parse. I came up with some naive code that I’ve run on the JSON files for chromosomes MT, Y, and 22. It’s very slow:disappointed:I estimate it would take about 2 weeks to run it on all chromosomes! I should probably try to parse the VCF files instead. > > For example, is BSgenome.Hsapiens.NCBI.GRCh38 up-to-date with SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP151.GRCh38? > TheSNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP.GRCh38packages only contain SNPs located on the chromosomes (SNPs located on scaffolds or alt/patch/fix sequences are omitted) so are compatible withBSgenome.Hsapiens.NCBI.GRCh38(contains the original GRCh38 assembly) andBSgenome.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg38***(based on the GRCh38.p13 assembly).

Alan Murphy (10:34:12) (in thread): > HeyHervé! Ah okay thanks for the explanation, that’s good to know! Yeah I would guess the VCFs are easier to work with, there are quite a few packages in R read them in, including MungeSumstats. Are you working in R for this? Do you have a sample of a format are you trying to get them to?

Sarath Kumar Murugan (10:41:09): > @Sarath Kumar Murugan has joined the channel

Hervé Pagès (10:46:18) (in thread): > I have a pipeline. The first step and most CPU-intensive is to convert the JSON files into simple tab-delimited files with 6 columns and one RefSNP id per line. Seehttps://github.com/hpages/SNPlocsForge/blob/273c0ecc0bb01984783747e4744d6ad1aa8b538c/man/extract_raw_snps_from_json.Rd#L46-L51The code is written in R. Then I feed these tab-delimited files to the rest of the pipeline. > I’ll try to generate the tab-delimited files from dbSNP’s VCF files instead.

Alan Murphy (10:50:02) (in thread): > That makes sense, thanks! If I get some time I’ll do the same to see if I can come up with something to speed it up. Creating the tab-delimited file is the time intensive step too yeah?

Hervé Pagès (11:18:09) (in thread): > yep, that’s the most time intensive step

2022-04-11

William Rodriguez (15:53:57): > @William Rodriguez has joined the channel

2022-04-12

Hervé Pagès (14:33:01) (in thread): > @Alan MurphySNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP155.GRCh38is ready (BioC 3.15 only).

2022-04-18

Alan Murphy (14:16:05) (in thread): > @Hervé Pagèsthis is amazing, thank you very much! Did you end up using the VCF? Will a version of dbSNP 155 also be made available for GRCh37? Both would be massively useful for MungeSumstats

Hervé Pagès (16:55:37) (in thread): > The JSON files. Using brute force to process the 25 JSON files in parallel on a powerful server took about 2 days. The source tarball of the GRCh38 package is about 6 GB. The package for GRCh37 could be made lighter by reusing the rs id, chrom, and alleles columns from the GRCh38 package, so it would actually only need to contain the positions. This should make it between 3 and 4 times smaller. Or maybe it’s easy enough to use something likertracklayer::liftOver()to translate between GRCh38 and GRCh37 positions, in which case the package for GRCh37 wouldn’t even be needed. I’ll look into this.

2022-04-19

Alan Murphy (02:49:37) (in thread): > Thank you!

Anna Quaglieri (she/her) (00:17:19): > hey there! What’s the suggested way to, given a list of human proteins/genes map them to the latest protein/Gene Ids? Sometimes names changes or synonims are used.

Lluís Revilla (03:19:25) (in thread): > I think the easiest would be: > > library("Org.Hs.eg.db") > select(Org.Hs.eg.db, keys = my_genes, keytype = "SYMBOL", columns = "GENEID") > > If you want the NCBI gene ID. There are several solutions, using other annotation packages if you want the ENSEMBL ids. I would recommend to look at the select help page, as there are arguments to decide what to do with synonyms (not sure about name changes). You could also use biomaRt to retrieve this kind of information.

Anna Quaglieri (she/her) (05:27:51) (in thread): > Thanks a lot@Lluís Revilla! I will try this option:+1:

James Ward (17:57:38): > I asked on Twitter, and curious if it’s suitable to post here also.“using SummarizedExperiment: > I want something like assayData() to hold tabular data about each matrix in assays(), one row per assay. > When storing more than one assay matrix, I encode too much into the assay name.Has this idea been discussed?”I see curatedTCGAData uses custom functions as a work-around, their experiment names are things like > “ACC_CNASNP-20160128” > If someone wants to filter experiment name they use a TCGA function. > I basically want an empty DataFrame on slot se@assayData with one row per entry in se@assaysThen I’ll add columns to that DataFrame as needed.

2022-05-02

Fabricio Almeida-Silva (10:58:11): > Hi, everyone! Does anyone know if there’s any PAL2NAL equivalent in R or a package to convert protein alignments to codon alignments?

2022-05-03

Vince Carey (10:45:14) (in thread): > Have you considered using the metadata slot of SummarizedExperiment?

Vince Carey (10:46:09) (in thread): > @Laurent Gatto… perhaps you have worked on this?

James Ward (10:55:59) (in thread): > Thanks for the response! That’s a potential workaround I thought about and may try in the meantime. I could store an assays DataFrame there with info. The step that isn’t ideal is subsetting assays, I was hoping for something that would also subset the assays DataFrame.The metadata slot is useful but is sort of like using S3 object attributes, it doesn’t stay in sync with the main object. At least it isn’t dropped during subsetting as with S3 object attributes!The rowData and colData are useful to keep data in sync with those dimensions. I was thinking of assays as another dimension.As I think it out, a driver of the use cases appears to be the subsetting step. (Also the addition of new assay matrix. Ahhh.) Even if the DataFrame is not subset, the rownames could be used to sync with what assayNames are present.

Vince Carey (10:58:46) (in thread): > I’d suggest filing a feature request on the SummarizedExperiment github issues page. The more details you can give on the strength of the use case the greater the likelihood there will be discussion and code produced in response.

James Ward (11:04:02) (in thread): > Silly question, but is this the URL? It looks active with Hervè Pagès making commits, that’s a good sign.https://github.com/Bioconductor/SummarizedExperiment/issues

Vince Carey (11:05:57) (in thread): > That’s the place.

Laurent Gatto (11:33:44) (in thread): > No, sorry, I can’t help.

Laurent Gatto (11:33:53) (in thread): > (Thanks for tagging me though)

James Ward (11:34:09) (in thread): > https://github.com/Bioconductor/SummarizedExperiment/issues/62

Fabricio Almeida-Silva (15:26:13) (in thread): > Thanks anyway!

Anusha Nagari (14:59:00): > Hi everyone! Are the commits made to the release branches (ex: RELEASE_3_15) are reflected real time or will it take the usual 24-48 hours to propagate the changes like in dev version

Marcel Ramos Pérez (15:00:07): > They should be reflected close to real time athttps://code.bioconductor.org/browse/and expect the changes to propagate via the BBS on M, W, and Fhttps://bioconductor.org/checkResults/

Anusha Nagari (15:03:00) (in thread): > @Marcel Ramos PérezThank you, I am trying to push the changes to RELEASE_3_15 and following the steps fromMerge changes to the current release branch, however having issues with the changes not being pushed to. Could you please let me know if I am missing something?

Marcel Ramos Pérez (15:07:03) (in thread): > I’d recommendhttps://contributions.bioconductor.org/git-version-control.html#bug-fix-in-release-and-devel

Anusha Nagari (15:08:56) (in thread): > I did not realize that I have to change the release version as well and already made the changes to devel branch. In that case can I follow the above thread?

Marcel Ramos Pérez (15:10:39) (in thread): > If it is a bug fix, you should cherry-pick the commits over to the release branch

Marcel Ramos Pérez (15:10:42) (in thread): > and yes

Anusha Nagari (16:05:12) (in thread): > I followed the steps and merged updates resolving conflicts. But getting the error: ! [remote rejected] master -> master (pre-receive hook declined) > > Attached is the screenshot. git status shows the working tree is clean

Anusha Nagari (16:05:16) (in thread): - File (PNG): image.png

Marcel Ramos Pérez (16:08:26) (in thread): > It looks like the conflicts weren’t fully resolved, committed, and were caught by the git hook

Anusha Nagari (16:19:37) (in thread): > Got it. I am unable to find the conflicts how do i go about this?

Marcel Ramos Pérez (16:33:08) (in thread): > Did you try using grep? e.g., grep -rn ‘===+’

Marcel Ramos Pérez (16:34:30) (in thread): > Also seehttps://contributions.bioconductor.org/git-version-control.html?q=merge%20conflig#resolve-merge-conflicts - Attachment (contributions.bioconductor.org): Chapter 21 Git Version Control | Bioconductor Packages: Development, Maintenance, and Peer Review > The Bioconductor project is maintained in a Git source control system. Package maintainers update their packages by pushing changes to their git repositories. This chapter contains several…

Alexandru-Ioan Voda (07:56:29): > Say I want to download a dependency, a baseline-model for a package that needs it to run specific analyses on new datasets. > > How would I download the data to<package_install_path>/extdata/using****BiocFileCache****and****usethis****? I was told to use these but the docs are a bit convoluted for me > > I understand that****usethis****is useful because it can help format Windows-like paths (with\instead of/like we do in Linux or Macs) for system calls, but the docs of****usethis******** and ********BiocFileCache******** ****seem very long and address many use-cases that I don’t need?

Vince Carey (09:13:02): > You should not operate on<package_install_path>… use BiocFileCache. Maybe there are enough hints in > > > get_cached_model > function(path = "/tmp/beta.csv", cache=BiocFileCache::BiocFileCache()) { > chk = BiocFileCache::bfcquery(cache, "beta.csv") > if (nrow(chk)==0) { > tag = names(BiocFileCache::bfcadd(cache, path, action="copy")) > } > else tag = chk$rid > cache[[tag]] > } > > to get you going. This handles caching a file on disk. The man page for bfcadd has indications on ways of handling something on the web. If the value to be cached is small, download it to the cache. Only R itself should be writing into<package_install_path>

Alexandru-Ioan Voda (09:19:30) (in thread): > Thank you for your reply! > > The data is quite large, which is why I don’t put it inextdatadirectly with documentation, and I’d prefer to have it in a non/tmp/-like path, since it should be perma-stored as long as the package is installed/used > > Thanks for the code snippet, I’ll try this out:relaxed:

Alan O’C (10:05:05) (in thread): > The file cache is kept in the users home directory rather than/tmpeg I have~/.cache/R/ExperimentHubetc

2022-05-23

Dora Zvjerković (09:58:07): > @Dora Zvjerković has joined the channel

2022-05-24

Kristian Ullrich (04:42:27) (in thread): > Yes, it is, see here:

Kristian Ullrich (04:42:28) (in thread): > https://gitlab.gwdg.de/mpievolbio-it/crbhits/-/blob/master/R/cds2codonaln.R - Attachment (GitLab): R/cds2codonaln.R · master · mpievolbio-it / CRBHits > CRBHits is a reimplementation of the Conditional Reciprocal Best Hit algorithm crb-blast in R.

Kristian Ullrich (04:53:04) (in thread): > However, I need to say, that my implementation starts directly from CDS. This is because in a lot of times even on “good” resources the CDS and protein sequences do not match perfectly and PAL2NAL will also through an error for this situations. Of course there are cases of post-transcriptional AA changes which will be hard to be back-translated into codon alignments anyway.

Kristian Ullrich (04:55:03) (in thread): > Thehttps://github.com/drostlab/orthologrpackage has a wrapper to PAL2NAL already implemented.

Hervé Pagès (15:49:44) (in thread): > Major surprise is that some rs ids have been placed on a different chromosome in GRCh37 vs GRCh38 (e.g. rs183986666 was on chr10 in GRCh37 and is now on chr9 in GRCh38, seehttps://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/snp/rs183986666#variant_details). This breaks the naive assumptions I made when I wrote the scripts to extract SNP placements from the RefSNP json files e.g. snps inrefsnp-chr9.json.bz2are not necessarily on chr9 in GRCh37. Had to refactor things substantially but now extraction is even slower. Will take days before completion…:disappointed:

Ariel Hippen (15:35:47): > In a SingleCellExperiment object, is there a way to subset based on gene expression values?

Ariel Hippen (15:35:52): > Say I wanted to filter down to cells that express EPCAM. I could make a subset with just EPCAM and pass it to scater’saddPerCellQCfunction, then subset onsce$subsets_epcam_sumbut it seems like there must be a more direct route

kent riemondy (16:35:52) (in thread): > You can subset more directly by examining the count matrix, e.g.: > > #BiocManager::install("scRNAseq") > library(scRNAseq) > sce <- FletcherOlfactoryData() > > # if you want to select cells positive for 1 gene, e.g. Epcam > epcam_positive <- which(assay(sce, "counts")["Epcam", ] > 0) > epcam_pos_sce <- sce[, epcam_positive] > > assay(epcam_pos_sce, "counts")["Epcam", 1:5] > #> OEP01_N706_S501 OEP01_N701_S501 OEP01_N707_S507 OEP01_N705_S501 OEP01_N702_S505 > #> 80 203 40 736 3 > > # if you want to select cells positive for more than 1 gene > genes_to_check <- c("Msi2", "Epcam", "Trp53") > positive_cells <- which(colSums(assay(sce, "counts")[genes_to_check, ] > 0) == length(genes_to_check)) > triple_pos_sce <- sce[, positive_cells] > > assay(triple_pos_sce, "counts")[genes_to_check, 1:5] > #> OEP01_N706_S501 OEP01_N703_S501 OEP01_N704_S503 OEP01_N704_S506 > #> Msi2 18 22 115 65 > #> Epcam 80 228 317 111 > #> Trp53 741 42 1 1 > #> OEP01_N708_S506 > #> Msi2 3 > #> Epcam 12 > #> Trp53 5584 >

Ariel Hippen (16:42:52) (in thread): > This works, thank you!

2022-06-17

Jenny Drnevich (10:10:36): > Any one else noticed/irritated by RStudio’s recent change to their Help tab? The Usage section for any function’s help page is now in a separate text box that has it’s own scroll bar at the bottom of the Usage section, which often isn’t visible when there are lots of functions in the Usage. Makes it almost impossible to scroll and see a function at the same time, without make the Help pane extreme large!:rage:

Marcel Ramos Pérez (10:24:39) (in thread): > That does sound frustrating. Can you provide an example function that has that? I haven’t encountered one with that feature (I’m running RStudio 2022.02.3 Build 492). If you create an issue athttps://community.rstudio.com/and link back here, we can upvote:slightly_smiling_face:

Jenny Drnevich (11:09:47) (in thread): > I am also running 2022.02.3 Build 492; for Windows, so maybe it’s different on Mac.?limma::goanais one and?gplots::heatmap.2has an egregiously long Usage section, but at least most of that only has one argument per line so scrolling isn’t needed. I’ll try to find some others later to create the RStudio issue.

Sebastian Wolf (06:36:49): > @Sebastian Wolf has joined the channel

SFS Worms (08:52:15): > @SFS Worms has joined the channel

SFS Worms (08:56:00): > Having some issue trying to extract specific sequences from NGS set (I have one randomised region that I want to extract from amplicon reads). My code run, but it takes about 1hour for 60*10^6 reads. I’m thinking trying to run things with parallel processing might help, but I can’t figure it out, does anyone got good ressources on using parallel processing with multiple alignments?

Kasper D. Hansen (11:04:13): > Taking advantage of multiple processors efficiently is pretty hard. However, is your problem inherently embarrasingly parallel? Then you should get tons of milage out of additional processors. I am pretty unclear on what you’re trying to do.

SFS Worms (11:41:03): > Sorry, I was pretty unclear, so I have a bunch of sequences from a drift experiment in a fastq file. All the sequences have a conserved sequence followed by a randomized sequence that I am interested in, let’s say ATGGTAGTNNNGTGA or whatever. I want to extract all the NNN sequences. Right now I’m doing it by aligning TAGT to sequence to see where the NNN is and then subsetting it with subseq, but the alignment provedure is extremely slow and I was hoping to parallellize it.

Rob Patro (11:45:32): > So you want to extract all (say 3) nucleotide sequences that occur downstream of aTAGT?

Rob Patro (11:45:44): > and only in the forward or also in the reverse complement?

SFS Worms (11:46:33): > Only in the foward seq would do

SFS Worms (11:47:00): > And I’m reasonably certian thatTAGTonly occurs once in the sequence in my case

Rob Patro (11:55:04): > Have you tried something straightforward like the following? > > gunzip -c <fastq> | grep -oP "TAGT\K([ACGT]{3})" >

Rob Patro (11:55:12): > grep can be super fast

SFS Worms (11:56:04): > Mmmmmmh, no, I’ll try now

SFS Worms (11:56:06): > Thanks!

Rob Patro (12:24:04): > you’re welcome!

Rob Patro (12:24:41): > I’ll note thegunzip -c <fastq> |part is only if your fastq is gzipped, if it’s already uncompressed it’s justgrep -oP "TAGT\K([ACGT]{3})" <fastq>

SFS Worms (13:19:11): > i’m not familiar with regex, but what’s the role of ? Not take the things before?

Martin Morgan (13:19:36): > In R Ithinkthis could be implemented as > > library(Biostrings); library(stringi) > reads = readDNAStringSet("<fastq>") > stri_extract(reads, regex = "(?<=TAGT)[ACGT]{3}") > > This uses ‘lookbehind’(<=TAGT)(?<=says start the match after the string in parentheses). If the fastq file where large one could iterate through in chunks (e.g., of 10’s of millions of reads at a time) as illustrated on the help page?readDNAStringSet.

Rob Patro (13:21:01): > Ingrep,@Martin Morgansuggests,I.e.printoutonlythecapturegroup.

SFS Worms (13:21:19): > Thanks!

SFS Worms (13:32:59) (in thread): > This works fine, but somehow stri extract is even slower than my previous method, Ill look into it tomorrow

Martin Morgan (14:41:38) (in thread): > It will be slow if you run out of memory; in that case you will need to iterate through your files in chunks.

2022-06-28

Lea Seep (03:01:56): > @Lea Seep has joined the channel

Arvid Hutzfeldt (04:54:55): > @Arvid Hutzfeldt has joined the channel

Moni (07:06:04): > @Moni has joined the channel

SFS Worms (09:10:58) (in thread): > So I’m not sure I understand why, but on 100k sequence > > extract.peptide2 = function(dnaSeq, regexPattern = "(?<=TGGCTTCATTGCGAGCAAT)[ACGT]{24}"){ > seq = stri_extract(dnaSeq, regex = regexPattern) %>% DNAStringSet() > return(seq) > } > > runs in 5.7 sec. > > extract.peptide3 = function(dnaSeq, regexPattern = "(?<=TGGCTTCATTGCGAGCAAT)[ACGT]{24}"){ > x <- str_locate(dnaSeq, "(?<=TGGCTTCATTGCGAGCAAT)[ACGT]{24}") > peptideList <- subseq(x = dnaSeq, start = x[,1], end = x[,2]) > return(peptideList) > } >

SFS Worms (09:11:05) (in thread): > Second on in 0.47

SFS Worms (09:11:22) (in thread): > And > > extract.peptide4 = function(dnaSeq, regexPattern = "TGGCTTCATTGCGAGCAAT", pepSize = 24){ > x <- str_locate(dnaSeq, regexPattern) > peptideList <- subseq(x = dnaSeq, start = x[,2]+1, end = x[,2]+pepSize) > return(peptideList) > } >

SFS Worms (09:11:52) (in thread): > In 0.17, so it seems stri_extract is less efficient that subseq() by a lot

SFS Worms (09:12:03) (in thread): > Thanks a ton for the help, really pushed me in the right direction

Martin Morgan (10:36:05) (in thread): > Building on your last example, because theregexPatternis actually fixed, I think you can usevmatchPattern()followed by a little massaging and thensubseq(), maybe something like > > match <- vmatchPattern(regexPattern, dnaSeq, fixed=TRUE) > found <- lengths(match) == 1L > dnaSeq1 <- dnaSeq[found] > subseq( > dnaSeq1, > min(width(dnaSeq1), unlist(end(found)) + 1L), > min(width(dnaSeq1), unlist(end(found)) + pepSize) > ) > > this should be fast because there is no coercion fromDNAStringSettocharacter(), and becausesubseq()actually creates ‘views’ on to the DNA sequence, rather than creating a new DNAStringSet.

SFS Worms (10:44:43) (in thread): > Oh nice, will give it a try!

Hervé Pagès (02:36:44) (in thread): > I’ve just pushedSNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP155.GRCh37_0.99.22.tar.gz(929,496,192 SNPs) andSNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP155.GRCh38_0.99.22.tar.gz(949,021,448 SNPs) to BioC 3.16. The 2 packages should become available viaBiocManager::install()in the next 3 hours or so. > Sorry for the delay, had to completely refactor the pipeline I use to forge these packages. My previous pipeline missed a few SNPs that are included in this new version ofSNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP155.GRCh38.

Alan Murphy (02:37:52) (in thread): > Amazing news, thank you so much for this I really appreciate it!!

Hervé Pagès (02:40:14) (in thread): > You’re welcome. Also FYI I removed the following packages from BioC 3.16 to make room for these 2 new packages: > - SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP.20101109 > - SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP.20120608 > - SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP141.GRCh38 > - XtraSNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP141.GRCh38 > - SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP142.GRCh37 > - SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP151.GRCh38 > All of them are currently deprecated in BioC 3.15.

Alan Murphy (02:43:50) (in thread): > That’s all good with me, the only other dbSNP versions I use are: > > SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP144.GRCh37, > SNPlocs.Hsapiens.dbSNP144.GRCh38 > > which I’d like to keep as users of the package have expressed a need for the older version of dbSNP too

Amos Schorr (05:51:50): > Not related to bio, but hopefully y’all might know some ppl you can share this with. A super awesome org that teaches ppl data science and software engineering in places that don’t offer many opportunities is hiring a couple ppl (IT Dev, Director of Technology) and they really need help getting the word out. They’re fully remote, high impact jobs with explicit salary ranges in the JD. > > 1. IT dev. You need to be able to work with Docker, Redis, PostgreSQL, AWS, jQuery, bootstrap, select2, Linux, Docker, AWS. > > 2. Dir. of Technology. You need some leadership experience, experience developing tech and data products, managing infrastructure on AWS, open source experience, etc.https://carpentries.org/jobs/ - Attachment (The Carpentries): Job Opportunities with The Carpentries > The Carpentries is a fiscally sponsored project of Community Initiatives, a registered 501(c)3 non-profit organisation based in California, USA. We are a global community teaching foundational computational and data science skills to researchers in academia, industry and government.

Greg Fragin (21:26:47): > @Greg Fragin has joined the channel

2022-09-07

Laura Marcos-Zambrano (04:55:35): > Hi all:grinning:I will need your advice > I’m preparing a teaching course about R and Bioconductor basics for undergrad students. Everyone is going to bring their own laptop. What will be the best way to prepare everything for solving installation, and dependencies issues in each different computer?

Luke Zappia (05:55:51) (in thread): > From experience teaching Software Carpentry workshops the biggest issue was institutional laptop where things were locked down (stuff like the default home directory being on a shared drive you can’t access). Hopefully that should be ok if people are using personal laptops. > > I would send out a super detailed set of instructions for what to download and install including Windows/MacOS/Linux differences (preferably with pictures). It can seem obvious but just saying “install from this website” is often hard for people to follow. People also often struggle with the difference between R and RStudio and that they need to install both. Check if any of the packages you want to use have extra system dependencies but if you are sticking to common packages the normal install system should be able to handle things. It can also be helpful to get people to install packages in advance to avoid waiting for things to compile and to find any major issues. > > I’m sure people with have other suggestions I have forgotten. > > Good luck with the course!

Laura Marcos-Zambrano (07:15:43) (in thread): > Thank you Luke:slightly_smiling_face:I will go with the “super detailed” set of instructions:stuck_out_tongue:and hope everyone can follow it.

Laurent Gatto (08:04:54) (in thread): > Here’s what we have for the workshop we are going to run next week:https://carpentries-incubator.github.io/bioc-intro/setup.html

Laura Marcos-Zambrano (09:38:58) (in thread): > Thanks!!:slightly_smiling_face:

Kasper D. Hansen (10:22:52) (in thread): > In my experience, a very useful thing to do is to make a script thatcheckswhether the dependencies are installed and tell them to screenshot the output and send it. That way you know it “works” and you know how many people you will need to help.

Federico Marini (12:05:55) (in thread): > On a similar line to@Kasper D. Hansen- for a summer school we recently ran, I specified the requirements in a page, telling them what to do to check if all runs, and then if problems arise, we offered some kind of troubleshooting on day 0. Can be (if not too much going on) on starting time -1 hour:wink:

Ana Beatriz Villaseñor Altamirano (17:50:08) (in thread): > If you have time (and or TAs), you can schedule 1 hr for solving setup issues before starting classes. > Also you could use Rstudio Cloudhttps://www.rstudio.com/resources/webinars/rstudio-cloud-in-the-classroom/as far as I remember it was free for teaching, maybe I’m wrong - Attachment (rstudio.com): RStudio Cloud in the Classroom > Much has been written in the statistics and data science education literature about pedagogical tools and approaches to provide a practical computational foundation for students.

Sean Davis (21:15:57) (in thread): > @Laura Marcos-ZambranoI’m happy to host on Orchestra (http://orchestra.cancerdatasci.org) if you’d like (it’s free). The one caveat is that there is no persistent storage between 8-hour sessions, so students need to download their work. > > If you are teaching a semester course, go through the challenges of getting software installed on their personal laptops. If just a few limited sessions, then Orchestra might be a good fit. I will often do both under the assumption that someone will have problems during coding sessions; it is good to have a backup like Orchestra just in case that you can launch in a couple of minutes for a student.

Sean Davis (21:17:35) (in thread): > Orchestra has the advantage of being able to preinstall software and to include course materials inside the environment.

2022-09-08

Laura Marcos-Zambrano (03:53:30) (in thread): > Thank you everybody:slightly_smiling_face:… It’s a semester course@Sean Davis, so I think I will go trough installation in their laptops. I’m taking a look at Orchestra and I think it’s pretty nice also

Laura Marcos-Zambrano (03:55:52) (in thread): > @Ana Beatriz Villaseñor AltamiranoI was looking Rcloud, but I’m not sure if the free account will be enough for all the classes:thinking_face:

Tom Dean (05:40:23): > @Tom Dean has joined the channel

Abdul Saboor Khan (17:52:17): > Hello everyone, can anyone tell me how to resolve this issue ” Error in eigen(input) : infinite or missing values in ‘x’” , yes I have NAs in my matrix file, I am making the phenotype network using this code “cormat2 <- cor(network_nem[1:16], method =”spearman”, use = “na.or.complete”) ” , and then this qgraph(cormat2,graph = “cor”, layout = “circle”, sampleSize = nrow(network_nem), alpha = 0.3, cut = 5, bonf = T, title = “Phenotype Network of A. nemorensis”, title.cex = 2, vsize = 7, usePCH = T, details = T, threshold=“sig”, height = 30, width = 40, label.scale = T, theme =‘TeamFortress’) . I have one column as a character and I have to keep it in the network but it doesn’t accept other than numeric. If you have the solution please tell me.

Hervé Pagès (18:48:28) (in thread): > Please ask your question again on our support site:https://support.bioconductor.org/Also please make sure to read the posting guide before you post. In particular the code you show should illustrate the problem and others should be able to run it. Format your post by putting code between triple backticks. This helps with readability and it makes it easier for others to copy/paste it. Also please provide yoursessionInfo(). Thanks!

2022-09-11

Janani Ravi (18:15:46): > R@IISAR Workshops by Ayush Patel and @Heather Turner**** in Bengaluru, India, Dec 30, 2022** > > This is the second in the series of R-related conferences and workshops offered as part of the biannualInternational Indian Statistical Association meeting. The first was held inMumbai in 2019. The 2022 day-long workshop will take place at theIndian Institute of Science, Bangaloreon December 30, 2022. This workshop has been endorsed by theR Foundation. This is a call for participants throughout India. Apply to participant using the form on the website: > More info:https://r-iisa2022.rbind.io/| Register here:https://bit.ly/Riisa2022More on Twitter:https://twitter.com/AsiaR_comm/status/1569085556734435328

2022-09-12

Jan Stanstrup (03:35:41) (in thread): > I did a script that checks the R version, installs packages and checks if they installed OK. then writes in the console “good to go!” or “XX is missing”. Also might be good to give them some material to prepare on. It is a mouthful to start R from zero. I collected some videos here:https://nexs-metabolomics.gitlab.io/INM/inm-booklet/0203-R_intro.html - Attachment (nexs-metabolomics.gitlab.io): 2.3 Introduction to R (self-study) | Multivariate analysis with PLSDA > 2.3 Introduction to R (self-study) | Multivariate analysis with PLSDA

Jan Stanstrup (03:36:18) (in thread): > they generally request more info on ggplots since it is used so much. so make sure to find the time for that

That means that you’ll need to install R 4.2 on your computer from CRAN https://cran.r-project.org/ > > For macOS users, you might want to use to install :package: deps for compiling > > sudo R > source(“https://mac.R-project.org/bin/install.R”) > install.libs(“all”) > > #rstats https://twitter.com/Bioconductor/status/1519428806150443014 - Attachment (twitter): Attachment > Bioconductor 3.15 is released! 2022-10-26

Nicholas Cooley (09:24:29): > Hi everyone, I have a sort of under the hood question aboutmclapply, which to my understanding, works by forking. How do the forks inmclapplyget assigned their memory limits? i.e. do they inheritR_MAX_VSIZEfrom the parent session? Do they initialize their own limits from what’s available? Thanks!

Martin Morgan (11:12:33) (in thread): > I’d guess yes, they inherit from the parent session (because the environment variable is assigned to in C to global variable, and the global variable is inherited by the forked process. But you’d get a more definitive answer on theR-devel mailing list.@Henrik Bengtssonmight also have something to say…

Henrik Bengtsson (13:14:56) (in thread): > Like Martin, I’d also guess forked child processes inherit this from the parent process, but that’s just my best guess. > > BTW, if you’re running into memory issues withmclapply(), please know that forked processing is tricky in R (and sometimes unstable). Although it might spare some memory because of how forking works, eventually child processes might start updating the memory so that the OS eventually end up with make copies. I haven’t formally investigated it, but I can see how this could happen “at random” as R’s garbage collection starts running in the different forked processes.

Nicholas Cooley (14:25:46) (in thread): > Thanks both of you! It’s mostly a curiosity question, because i was running into some instability when the forks were consuming large amounts of memory. I just ended up asking for less cores and things turned out ok.

2022-12-06

Alan O’C (10:30:47): > Any clues as to why I see ggplot2 deprecation warnings (sigh,aes_string…) when running tests locally, but not on the build machines? Both running ggplot2 3.4.0 apparently

Marcel Ramos Pérez (11:00:04) (in thread): > Have you tried to reproduce inside the Bioc container? My guess is that the deprecation warning shows up once per session and gets triggered in another package first?:thinking_face:

Alan O’C (11:57:12) (in thread): > Ah!This warning is displayed once every 8 hours.

Hervé Pagès (13:34:45) (in thread): > Is this a case of over engineering? I understand that they don’t want to make too much noise but OTOH this is not great for reproducibility.

Alan O’C (13:37:19) (in thread): > It’s kind of antisocial imo. two of my packages had a tonne of warnings that don’t show up on the BBS, and these functions are deprecated since 3.0 apparently. Not sure if CRAN has the same issue, but it does probably mean when they make them defunct then half of Bioc will be in the red

Hervé Pagès (13:43:08) (in thread): > Maybe they could mitigate the problem by turning this into a hard warning ifIS_BIOC_BUILD_MACHINE=true?

Hervé Pagès (13:43:52) (in thread): > and something similar whenR CMD checkruns on a CRAN machine

Mike Smith (14:09:10) (in thread): > Switching around the effort, I think they provide the option for setting the frequency as a global option i.e.https://rlang.r-lib.org/reference/abort.html#muffling-and-silencing-conditionsPerhaps that could be set on the build machines? - Attachment (rlang.r-lib.org): Signal an error, warning, or message — abort > These functions are equivalent to base functions base::stop(), > base::warning(), and base::message(). They signal a condition > (an error, warning, or message respectively) and make it easy to > supply condition metadata: > Supply class to create a classed condition that can be caught > or handled selectively, allowing for finer-grained error > handling. > Supply metadata with named … arguments. This data is stored > in the condition object and can be examined by handlers. > Supply call to inform users about which function the error > occurred in. > Supply another condition as parent to create a chained condition. > > Certain components of condition messages are formatted with unicode > symbols and terminal colours by default. These aspects can be > customised, see Customising condition messages.

Hervé Pagès (14:47:28) (in thread): > Maybe. But if the goal is reproducibility, an environment variable that we can add tohttps://master.bioconductor.org/checkResults/3.17/bioc-LATEST/Renviron.biocwould be nice.

Hervé Pagès (15:50:39) (in thread): > Issue opened forlifecycle:https://github.com/r-lib/lifecycle/issues/150

2022-12-07

Zainab Ashimiyu-Abdusalam (04:41:00): > @Zainab Ashimiyu-Abdusalam has joined the channel

Amos Schorr (10:52:08): > Hey folks! I work for Norn Group, a nonprofit in the bio of aging research field (we’re best known for our Impetus Grants program). Because this is such a new field, it’s fragmented and hard for everyone to navigate. So we’re building out an opt-in database for ppl who have or want careers in aging. It will NOT be a community you need to participate in. We’ll just be using this to make connections and help people build out labs, companies, and careers. > > We’re launching today and as of now have ~70 signups of people at all levels and in all kinds of roles. If this intrigues you, get more info and sign up if you likehere! It’s free and could be useful for you. > > Additionally, if you’d be so kind as toretweet/share our postson LinkedInannouncing it, that would be just darling of you:heart:My linkedin profile picture is of me moderating a panel with a dog on my lap. Or, more accurately, an adorable dog that’s sitting on my lap not giving a damn about the awesome conversation we were having. So it’s worth sharing my post if only for that.

Nicholas Cooley (09:38:31): > Hi folks, I’ve got a question that I’ve been unable to find a satisfying answer to: > Given a fasta file, and a gff, can I generate a gbk file for region subsets? I haven’t been able to find any tools that will do this.

Spencer Nystrom (11:05:11) (in thread): > I think something in theembosstoolkit can do this.https://emboss.sourceforge.net/docs/

Spencer Nystrom (11:05:57) (in thread): > seqretI think can do what you want.

Spencer Nystrom (11:06:36) (in thread): > I’m not familiar with Bioc support for gbk files though.

Nicholas Cooley (11:15:51) (in thread): > Thanks! I found a github repo for a package calledbiofilesthat seems to almost work, but it doesn’t appear to be currently supported.

Spencer Nystrom (11:30:43) (in thread): > Yeah, it doesn’t help that genbank is notorious for being the worst to parse.

Jennifer Gerbracht (08:30:21): > @Jennifer Gerbracht has joined the channel

Levi Waldron (11:06:35): > I asked theopenai.comAPI playground some questions about Bioconductor using their Q&A endpoint: > > Human: What do you know about the Bioconductor project for open-source bioinformatics? > AI: I understand that the Bioconductor project is an open-source software platform for analyzing and visualizing biological data. It provides a comprehensive suite of software for basic and advanced analyses of high-throughput data in genomics and proteomics. It also provides tools for common data-mining tasks. > Human: What is the Bioconductor project good for? > AI: The Bioconductor project is best known for its ability to facilitate a wide range of genomic data analysis tasks. It offers tools for gene expression, proteomics, high-throughput screening, sequence analysis, and many more. It is also used to integrate data from diverse sources and visualize it in meaningful ways. > Human: What are the weaknesses of the Bioconductor project? > AI: The main weakness of the Bioconductor project is that it can take a lot of time and effort for users to learn how to use all the tools available. Additionally, the project does not provide a unified user interface, so users must be familiar with different programming languages and learn the commands associated with each tool. > Human: How do you get involved in the Bioconductor project? > AI: To get involved in the Bioconductor project, you can join their community and take part in discussions. You can contribute to the open-source software by developing new features or fixing bugs. Additionally, you can also participate in workshops and educational events related to the project. > Human: How has Bioconductor changed over the years? > AI: Bioconductor has evolved significantly over the years, incorporating more user-friendly features, offering an updated suite of tools, and providing better support for different platforms. It has become an indispensable resource for bioinformatics researchers and is now used in many different fields. > Human: Why is the Bioconductor project important to science? > AI: The Bioconductor project is important to science because it provides a comprehensive suite of tools for analyzing and visualizing biological data. It allows researchers to make sense of large amounts of genomic or proteomic data, enabling them to uncover novel insights. It also helps to bridge the gap between wet lab studies and computational research. > Human: How does the Bioconductor build system work? > AI: The Bioconductor build system is based on a dependency graph of the various packages. It builds each package by downloading source code, compiling it, and running tests. If the build is successful, it will then generate binaries which can be used to install the package. The system also allows developers to manage the dependencies of their packages. > Human: What are the most important Bioconductor libraries? > AI: The most important Bioconductor libraries are the Biobase, BiocGenerics, and BiocParallel packages. These libraries provide essential functions for data analysis, such as importing and manipulating the data, selecting the appropriate statistical tests to use, and running multiple analyses in parallel. Additionally, they also provide essential support for creating visualizations, developing new packages, and managing dependencies. >

Krithika Bhuvanesh (11:09:57) (in thread): > Interesting . I didn’t think it would be so verbose. Thanks for sharing !

Sean Davis (11:29:29) (in thread): > There is no question the world changed with the introduction of ChatGPT.

Nitesh Turaga (11:37:31) (in thread): > I wonder how it generated this ?? > > Human: What are the weaknesses of the Bioconductor project? > AI: The main weakness of the Bioconductor project is that it can take a lot of time and effort for users to learn how to use all the tools available. Additionally, the project does not provide a unified user interface, so users must be familiar with different programming languages and learn the commands associated with each tool. >

Claire Seibold (15:48:19): > @Claire Seibold has joined the channel

Lambda Moses (17:55:19): > Just wondering, I know that Bioconductor counts the number of downloads and number of distinct IP addresses downloading each package each month. Does that include downloads by CIs like GitHub Actions, say used by the package authors themselves or by authors of other packages that depend on those packages?

Sean Davis (19:58:21): > It includes all downloads but not git checkouts and does include instances where the package is a dependency and not a direct install.

Hervé Pagès (20:19:45): > Yes, to be precise, we only count downloads fromhttps://bioconductor.org/packages/, which is where the standard installation method (BiocManager::install()) picks Bioconductor packages from. Whether the package was explicitely requested or got downloaded to satisfy a dependency is counted the same way, as 1 download (or 1 hit).

Lambda Moses (20:21:03): > Thanks!

Lambda Moses (20:22:36): > I don’t think anyone is actually doing it. I’m just wondering what if someone artificially increases the package download ranking by automatically downloading the packages by CIs run several times a day.:zany_face:

Hervé Pagès (20:28:23): > That will only increase the “Nb of downloads” but not the “Nb of distinct IPs” (unless they manage to automatically download each time from a distinct IP). We’ve seen something like this in the past where some package would suddendly get repeatedly downloaded thousands of times per month by some script running somewhere on a cluster (some sys admin probably left a rogue script running unintentionally). This is why we’ve switched to only consider the “Nb of distinct IPs” for the package ranking. This is a more meaningful/more reliable metrics.

Lambda Moses (20:35:29): > Right, almost forgot. I heard about that happening from a blog post analyzing CRAN package download data. GitHub Actions runners don’t have a fixed IP address and there’re ranges of IP addresses. Hence it’s possible to artificially increase the number of distinct IP addresses downloading a package by repeatedly running a GitHub Action, but that’s limited by the number of IP addresses Microsoft’s data centers have. OK, thanks! Won’t waste more of your time.
