Y-h. Taguchi (12:16:04): > There seems to be some problem in Bioconductor Single Package builder,http://bioconductor.org/spb_reports/TDbasedUFEadv_buildreport_20230318114618.html > > Error in library(testthat) : there is no package called 'testthat' > there is no package called Biobase > > I believe that these should not happen, Actually, Mac in Bioconductor Single Package builder does not report these errors. Is it possible for you to fix? Or have I done something wrong?

Suhana (12:24:22): > @Suhana has joined the channel

Andres Wokaty (23:40:55) (in thread): > We updated R on the devel builders Thursday and Friday, so it usually takes a two runs to get back to normal, but this might be due to a change on our linux builder on Friday to help identify missing dependencies:https://github.com/Bioconductor/BBS/issues/248. - Attachment: #248 WISH: Check with *R_CHECK_SUGGESTS_ONLY*=true to identify missing package dependencies > (This replaces invalid feature requests #242, #243, and #244.) > > Background > > Using *R_CHECK_SUGGESTS_ONLY*=true will run R CMD check with sandboxed an R library, where on declared package dependencies in DESCRIPTION + recursive hard dependencies are included. If an example, package test, or a vignette needs a package that is not in this set of dependencies, it will not be available to be loaded and result in an error. > > Wish > > Add *R_CHECK_SUGGESTS_ONLY*=true to Renviron.bioc to detect missing package dependencies. > > R CMD check sets up a sandboxed R library for it’s life span. It <https://github.com/wch/r-source/blob/8bf881e5da4da60f3aaad1b1812924817906bb29/src/library/tools/R/check.R#L138-L277|uses symbolic file links to do this>, so the performance overhead for doing this is really small. I could not detect a performance penalty (see below for details). > > Examples > > I ran the following examples using Renviron.bioc for Bioconductor 3.17: > > > wget [https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Bioconductor/BBS/master/3.17/Renviron.bioc](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Bioconductor/BBS/master/3.17/Renviron.bioc) > > > I also ran with: > > > export *R_CHECK_TESTS_NLINES*=17 > > > to make it clear what one of the ERRORs is about. > > Example 1: Package bnem (missing RUnit for tests and BiocStyle for vignettes) > > Downloading bnem package source tarball: > > > $ wget [https://bioconductor.org/packages/devel/bioc/src/contrib/bnem_1.7.0.tar.gz](https://bioconductor.org/packages/devel/bioc/src/contrib/bnem_1.7.0.tar.gz) > > > Current Bioconductor 3.17 checks > > When checking with: > > > $ R_CHECK_ENVIRON=Renviron.bioc R --vanilla CMD check bnem_1.7.0.tar.gz > > > package passes with a single NOTE. > > * using log directory ‘/tmp/bioc/bnem.Rcheck’ > * using R Under development (unstable) (2022-11-30 r83391) > * using platform: x86_64-pc-linux-gnu (64-bit) > * using session charset: UTF-8 > * checking for file ‘bnem/DESCRIPTION’ … OK > * checking extension type … Package > * this is package ‘bnem’ version ‘1.7.0’ > * package encoding: UTF-8 > * checking package namespace information … OK > * checking package dependencies … OK > * checking if this is a source package … OK > * checking if there is a namespace … OK > * checking for hidden files and directories … OK > * checking for portable file names … OK > * checking for sufficient/correct file permissions … OK > * checking whether package ‘bnem’ can be installed … OK > * checking installed package size … OK > * checking package directory … OK > * checking ‘build’ directory … OK > * checking DESCRIPTION meta-information … OK > * checking top-level files … OK > * checking for left-over files … OK > * checking index information … OK > * checking package subdirectories … OK > * checking R files for non-ASCII characters … OK > * checking R files for syntax errors … OK > * checking whether the package can be loaded … OK > * checking whether the package can be loaded with stated dependencies … OK > * checking whether the package can be unloaded cleanly … OK > * checking whether the namespace can be loaded with stated dependencies … OK > * checking whether the namespace can be unloaded cleanly … OK > * checking loading without being on the library search path … OK > * checking dependencies in R code … NOTE > Namespace in Imports field not imported from: ‘rmarkdown’ > All declared Imports should be used. > * checking S3 generic/method consistency … OK > * checking replacement functions … OK > * checking foreign function calls … OK > * checking R code for possible problems … OK > * checking Rd files … OK > * checking Rd metadata … OK > * checking Rd cross-references … OK > * checking for missing documentation entries … OK > * checking for code/documentation mismatches … OK > * checking Rd sections … OK > * checking Rd contents … OK > * checking for unstated dependencies in examples … OK > * checking contents of ‘data’ directory … OK > * checking data for non-ASCII characters … OK > * checking data for ASCII and uncompressed saves … OK > * checking installed files from ‘inst/doc’ … OK > * checking files in ‘vignettes’ … OK > * checking examples … OK > * checking for unstated dependencies in ‘tests’ … OK > * checking tests … > Running ‘runTests.R’ > OK > * checking for unstated dependencies in vignettes … OK > * checking package vignettes in ‘inst/doc’ … OK > * checking running R code from vignettes … > ‘bnem.rmd’ using ‘UTF-8’… OK > NONE > * checking re-building of vignette outputs … OK > * checking PDF version of manual … OK > * DONE > > Status: 1 NOTE
> See

Hervé Pagès (17:05:54) (in thread): > @Y-h. TaguchiThis should be fixed now. See Lori’s announcement on the bioc-devel list:https://stat.ethz.ch/pipermail/bioc-devel/2023-March/019536.htmlYou should be able to trigger a new build for your package by bumping its version.

Y-h. Taguchi (19:21:07): > I know that Core team work hard and it is unrealistic to ask them something more. But I wonder if we can access single package builder during development before we start issue at github of bioconductor. I have foundherethatCheckTimeis doubled in Mac compared with other platforms. Since I do not have mac box, only opportunity for me to check how long CheckTime in Mac is only after the submission. Then I was forced to struggle with shortening CheckTime with including rewriting application completely. It might not be very efficient.

Vince Carey (19:38:19): > @Y-h. Taguchithis is a good question. I think you would be able to get a handle on performance in the mac domain using github actions. It is not straightforward to perfectly emulate the bioconductor build system but it would be possible to get a reasonable approximation. We have a group working on github actions recommendations for bioconductor packages and the slack channel#github-actions. In the long run we would not want to have individual developers using SPB but would prefer to have valid SPB emulators available. Have a look at the actions channel and consider using biocthis or rworkflows to build a .github/workflows entry for you. Let us know how it works out.

Alex Mahmoud (13:32:52): > Repost from CZI Open Sci slack in case it is interesting to people::loud_sound:Maybe of interest to folks here, and anyone from the R community! > At *******10am UTC+1 this Thursday ,*The Turing Way will be hosting Julia Silge from Posit (formerly RStudio) and Ella Kaye (Warwick University) to hear about their experience developing open source tools and communities with the R project. > Please sign up via theEventbrite pageif you are interested. - Attachment (Eventbrite): The Turing Way hosts: Julia Silge and Ella Kaye > The Turing Way welcomes Julia Silge and Ella Kaye to exchange insights from community building with R/Python and Open Source tools.

Lori Shepherd (14:14:23): > Nominations for Bioconductor 2023 Awards are now open. Bioconductor is excited to announce an opportunity to recognize those making significant outstanding contributions to the Bioconductor community. The Bioconductor project is announcing the call for Bioconductor Awards, honoring various forms of contributions to the project. Four awardees will be selected, each having contributed to the project in an outstanding way based on one or more of selection criteria. More information and details foundhttps://bioconductor.org/about/awards/and the nomination form can be found athttps://forms.gle/gPzmH8ewHNHWckdv6. The deadline for nominations is May 31, 2023. The awardee’s will be recognized at the annual Bioc2023 conference August 2-4. - Attachment (Google Docs): Bioconductor Awards 2023 > Nomination form for the Bioconductor Awards (see https://bioconductor.org/about/awards/ for more details). > > Please fill out all the fields in the following sections. > > In the Nominator section, please provide your own contact information in case we need to contact you with any question. > > In the Nominee section, please indicate how the nominee provides outstanding contributions for each of the selected nomination criteria. Four awards will be given using the selected criteria as justification.

Kozo Nishida (11:58:09): > Hi all, > I am interested in [re]implementing some Bioconductor packages in Python, would that be considered a different idea thanhttps://github.com/BiocPy? > (I would like to contribute to BiocPy if the idea is not different.)

Jayaram Kancherla (12:12:21) (in thread): > I would love to have more contributions from the community (need to work on advertising this effort more). > > what packages are you planning on implementing?

Kozo Nishida (12:33:06) (in thread): > There are two that have already been implemented, not planned. > * https://github.com/cytoscape/py4cytoscape(that is Python equivalent ofhttps://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/RCy3.html > * https://github.com/kozo2/pywikipathways(that is Python equivalent ofhttps://bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/rWikiPathways.html) > (However, py4cytoscape was implemented by a Cytoscape community member who is not me.) - Attachment (Bioconductor): RCy3 > Vizualize, analyze and explore networks using Cytoscape via R. Anything you can do using the graphical user interface of Cytoscape, you can now do with a single RCy3 function. - Attachment (Bioconductor): rWikiPathways > Use this package to interface with the WikiPathways API. It provides programmatic access to WikiPathways content in multiple data and image formats, including official monthly release files and convenient GMT read/write functions.

Kozo Nishida (12:34:57) (in thread): > In addition to the above, I would also like to implement all Cytoscape-related Bioconductor packages in Python as well.

Kozo Nishida (12:36:33) (in thread): > I am very interested in a Python implementation of AnnotationHub in case of non-Cytoscape related packages.

Kozo Nishida (12:40:09): > I wonder if this is related to the above, but how should we understand the difference between rOpenSci and Bioconductor package review? > I am asking this because I have recently started working with the pyOpenSci community (I am also active in the PyData community). > I’m wondering if the Python implementation of Bioconductor would now go to pyOpenSci if the BiocPy management policy is not well known.

Leopoldo Valiente (12:48:19): > @Leopoldo Valiente has joined the channel

Martin Morgan (12:58:14) (in thread): > This might be interesting with respect to AnnotationHub –https://github.com/scverse/genomic-features– a (very much in ‘beta’) Python package to make EnsemblDb objects from AnnotationHub available to Python users.

Jayaram Kancherla (12:58:47) (in thread): > AnnotationHubwould be an awesome contribution to the BiocPy org, taking advantage of the data representations already in biocpy. > > In addition, I was considering how to make Bioconductor-[like, ish] packages more discoverable on PyPI. One way of improving the search is by addingBioconductoras a custom classifiers (https://pypi.org/classifiers/) in PyPI. Haven’t gotten around to submitting this yet, but I will

Martin Morgan (13:20:46) (in thread): > One maybe mistaken reservation I have about BiocPy is that it seems to capture the ‘implementation’ details, rather than the ‘interface’ to the object, e.g., for GenomicRanges one only needs two of three of start / end / width, with the specific choice an implementation detail. If all that is available is the implementation, then the Python community has to understand the internal representations and then re-implement the user interface (including all the edge cases accumulated over the years in Bioconductor!)

Isaac Virshup (16:39:04) (in thread): > Hey Kozo, I haven’t found the python ecosystem to be anywhere near as centralized to the R one. > > For the cytoscape stuff, I would suggest asking the biocypher people? They’re interested in bio knowledge graphs generally and could point you in the right direction. > > I’d really like to see more reuse of annotation hub in the python ecosystem (I’m working on thegenomic-featurespackage Martin mentioned, and am involved in scverse), so could be interested in collaborating there.

Franck RICHARD (17:10:11): > @Franck RICHARD has joined the channel

2023-05-04

Hassan Kehinde Ajulo (11:38:03): > @Hassan Kehinde Ajulo has joined the channel

sophy (13:02:58): > @sophy has left the channel

Vince Carey (15:28:16) (in thread): > @Isaac Virshupthanks for chiming in. I am very interested in helping to ensure that scientists can use the language of their choice to do the analyses they want to do. How to synchronize efforts in Bioc and scverse is a basic concern. I started trying to understand some of the issues involved by writing scviR which is in Bioc 3.17. I am sure I am behind the development cycle of scvi-tools, and catching up will take nontrivial effort. We are also discussing how to enhance AnnotationHub and we wouldn’t want to break things that you might be working with in genomic-features. (I think the risk is quite low but you never know). I think Bioconductor has benefited greatly by a) having the clearly identified, ~6 month parallel release (no API changes) and devel (can break with deprecation process) cycles, and b) limiting dependencies to bioc and CRAN packages. These approaches have their costs but I think they have been outweighed by benefits. Do you see any tendencies towards this kind of centralization/periodicity in scverse?

Vince Carey (15:33:29) (in thread): > @Kozo NishidaI don’t know how we should proceed as BiocPy matures. I will note that we can have vignettes for R packages that use python chunks; in BiocHail I get python/spark via basilisk. One way to think of Bioc for python is that we think of PyPi as a kind of CRAN, and anyone wanting to provide python capabilities for Bioconductor users would just have to write a basilisk-based package that pins the desired versions of exported python functionality. A thorough review might require python expertise – but if there is a review/credentialing/regular testing process underlying the PyPi component, maybe we would only be concerned with the R code defining the interface.

Vince Carey (15:49:51) (in thread): > And maybe we need to start a new channel? “BiocPython”?

Vince Carey (15:52:51) (in thread): > Just one more point. Wanted to mention the alabaster.* packages as tools for converting Bioc data structures like SingleCellExperiment to JSON+HDF5 (e.g.) for ingestion in other languages/systems. Getting experience with the metadata and construction of nicely usable instances in other languages will be worthwhile. alabaster.ranges for GRanges, for example.

Theary Chhim (19:03:50): > @Theary Chhim has joined the channel

2023-05-05

Kozo Nishida (00:34:05) (in thread): > Thanks for the info.@Vince CareyI understand your thoughts on BiocPy. > Thanks for the info on BiocHail. I am a hail user but did not know BiocHail. > I will try to use basilisk.

Kozo Nishida (00:35:18) (in thread): > I started a new channel#biocpython.

Isaac Virshup (07:47:57) (in thread): > @Vince Carey > > We are also discussing how to enhance AnnotationHub and we wouldn’t want to break things that you might be working with in genomic-features. > Would be happy to be a part of this conversation! I talked about this a little bit with@Martin Morganat the recentscverse hackathon. It could be useful to include people fromopen2cin this as well depending on which area of annotationhub. - Attachment (Twitter): scverse on Twitter > Many exciting things – and intense coding – at the scverse hackathon this week hosted by @OliverStegle and @saezlab in Heidelberg! >
> Our focus was on interoperability across languages and knowledgebases. Read on to see what we made! 1/n

Isaac Virshup (07:49:28) (in thread): > > I think Bioconductor has benefited greatly by a) having the clearly identified, ~6 month parallel release (no API changes) and devel (can break with deprecation process) cycles > This has been a thing we’ve discussed a bunch, which I’ll try to summarize here. It’s also something that we touched on in the scverse announcement publication (rdcu.be/c9Bx6) and during the review process with Wolfgang. > > I don’t think we’ll go for big parallel releases. We definitely don’t have the resources to arrange this for the whole community at the moment. > > What we are doing/ going to do is: > * Make release candidates for packages with many dependents (e.g. scverse core) > * Make it easy for downstream packages to have a CI job that tests against these (e.g.this CI job(which needs better documentation)). We would like to monitor these for failure after making a release candidate, but are still figuring that out. > > * We may add this as requirement to be listed as a “scverse ecosystem package” (https://scverse.org/packages/#ecosystem) > The core packages also follow semantic versioning w.r.t. API changes. I’d encourage downstream packages to do the same, but we’re not taking steps to enforce that. > > b) limiting dependencies to bioc and CRAN packages. > Does this essentially mean: “no installing dependencies from github”? Or is it more than that? > > Do you see any tendencies towards this kind of centralization/periodicity in scverse? > Periodicity: Not on the same scale. We would like to have more regular releases of tools, but are still figuring out greater development resources first. Even then, it’d be a goal, not necessarily a requirement. > > Centralization: Yes, but with a lighter touch. Essentially, we have restrictions on packages that we’ll advertise/ give a badge to. But we don’t want to go so far as having our own package distribution.

Vince Carey (08:07:25) (in thread): > Right – we do not permit dependence on github-only packages, and if a package is removed from CRAN the dependent package needs to adjust or motivate the CRAN contributor to fix to get it back into CRAN. Since your ecosystem requirements include PyPi availability, it may not be such a stretch to enlarge the scope of Bioconductor software contributions to explicitly support interop with scverse via basilisk. But further discussion will be needed.

Vince Carey (08:10:42) (in thread): > @Alex Mahmoud@Brian Schilderthere is a link above to agithub actionfor CI in scverse. Seems like it connects with the concerns we have in the cloud working group/rworkflows…

Brian Schilder (08:53:48) (in thread): > will give some thoughts on the new#biocpythonchannel

Shixiang Wang (23:12:07): > May I ask if there are any Ph.D. positions in tumor bioinformatics that are suitable for my junior colleague currently available? He recently completed his master’s degree and is looking for opportunities to pursue a Ph.D. His skills and capabilities are impressive, so any recommendations or assistance would be greatly appreciated.:pray:

Shixiang Wang (23:12:49) (in thread): > I have attached his CV here for someone interested in. - File (PDF): wanggsh_CV_en.pdf

2023-05-16

Laurent Gatto (05:58:19) (in thread): > Here are two open position for proteomics/bioinformatics PhD students in my group [https://lgatto.github.io/spatprot-job-2023/,https://lgatto.github.io/scp-job-2023/]. Even though not explicit in the advertisement, application to tumour biology are possible (especially given the focus of many labs in the institute). He’s welcome to contact me directly. - Attachment (~/): Open position in the CBIO lab: spatial proteomics and PTMs > A position for a PhD student (4 years) or a research assistant (1 yearcontracts, renewable) is open in the CBIO lab focusing on proteinsub-cellular localisation (spatial) proteomics, post-translationalmodification and machine learning. - Attachment (~/): Open position in the CBIO lab: single-cell proteomics > A fully-funded position for a PhD student (4 years) or a post-doctoralresearcher (2 - 3 years, depending on experience) is open in theComputational Biology and Bioinformatics lab (CBIO) at the de DuveInstitute, UCLouvain in Brussels, Belgium.

Shixiang Wang (06:18:43) (in thread): > @Laurent GattoThank you Laurent. I will transfer the good message to him :).

Sarvesh Nikumbh (07:26:42) (in thread): > Searching on Twitter with keywords “tumor bioinformatics phd positions”, some of the links on this webpage came up. May be some of these are interesting:http://www.begenomics.com/phdscholarship/ - Attachment (BeGenomics): PhD scholarship in Bioinformatics and Genomics > PhD scholarship in Bioinformatics, Genomics, Life Sciences and Machine learning

Kasper D. Hansen (17:31:06): > Have you looked at the schedule:https://bioc2023.bioconductor.org/schedule/ - Attachment (bioc2023.bioconductor.org): Schedule > Schedule

Kasper D. Hansen (17:31:35): > It seems that the keynotes have no names attached but they will be from the speakers onhttps://bioc2023.bioconductor.org

Kasper D. Hansen (17:31:55): > I am assuming that the organizers are still working on finalizing who talks when

Kasper D. Hansen (17:33:14): > although confusingly I count 6 speakers and 5 keynote slots

2023-06-27

Louis Le Nézet (04:52:55): > Hi everyone, > i don’t really know where to post this question, so feel free to redirect me to the correct channel:slightly_smiling_face:I was wondering how is the S3 and S4 methods supported in bioconductor and how to nicely write them. > In my case, I’m writing different functions that either take a dataframe and a list of columns names or a list of vector. > The aim would be to do the same as withboxplot. > Should I write something like the following: > > test <- function(x, ...){ > UseMethod("test") > } > > test.default <- function(x, x1, x2, ...){ > print("Is default with multiple vector") > } > > test.data.frame <- function(x, colnames, ...){ > print("Is data.frame") > print(colnames) > } >

Lluís Revilla (05:23:20) (in thread): > Bioconductor doesn’t have a special support for S3 and S4 methods than R. You can use other books likehttps://adv-r.hadley.nz/s4.htmlto learn how to write them. The special case for Bioconductor is that whenever possible you should reuse classes to benefit from their methods. But this is a nice question for the Bioconductor working grouphttps://github.com/Bioconductor/BiocClassesWorkingGroup(I’ll log it so that we can answer them in future materials)

Asier Ortega (06:16:21): > @Asier Ortega has joined the channel

Yoon Ji Jung (06:58:02): > @Yoon Ji Jung has joined the channel

Louis Le Nézet (07:13:37) (in thread): > Thanks for the answer:slightly_smiling_face:

Marjani Hall (11:09:28): > @Marjani Hall has joined the channel

Laurent Gatto (11:52:20) (in thread): > And following up from Lluís comment, most of the methods are S4, because the Bioconductor classes are exclusively S4. The important point is thus the data that you are handling and want to write a method for.

Hervé Pagès (13:59:17) (in thread): > Yes, Bioconductor recommends defining an S4 generic and methods for this. No need for an S3 generic and methods. So: > > setGeneric("foo", function(x, ...) standardGeneric("foo")) > > setMethod("foo", "ANY", > function(x, ...) { > all_vectors <- list(x, ...) > stopifnot(all(sapply(all_vectors, is.vector))) > ... > ... > } > ) > > setMethod("foo", "data.frame", > function(x, colnames) { > ... > ... > } > ) >

2023-06-28

Lluís Revilla (10:01:29): > I’m interested in theFEM packageremoved in 3.12. According togit.bioconductor.orgI see it listedR packages/FEMbut when I try to clone it or see it via thecode browserit doesn’t appear. I could download the source code, but while checking the package I was surprised to see that it still hasdownloadsas of this year. Are the downloads numbers across all versions (Bioconductor & package) but without the mirrors?

Helge Hecht (22:41:18): > There will be a mass spectrometry BoF at the Galaxy Community Conference - meeting details are in the following document:https://docs.google.com/document/d/1G_k4xtvaNkXkVOFiN22vahx_iryNeRaBj5JaeZuclmI/editIf you’d like to learn about the state of MS tools in Galaxy or would like to learn more about the activities this meeting is open for everyone to join and express their interest!

2023-07-11

Akewak Jeba (02:19:37): > @Akewak Jeba has joined the channel

Harvinder Singh (02:26:13): > @Harvinder Singh has joined the channel

Mike Smith (10:56:11) (in thread): > Don’t take this as gospel, but I’ll try to answer the bits I know. > > AFAIK a deprecated package is never actually removed from the git server. In fact, if you try this command you can still cloneFEM: > > git clone[https://git.bioconductor.org/packages/FEM.git](https://git.bioconductor.org/packages/FEM.git) > > When a package is removed from Bioconductor, it’s actually removed from the “Manifest”, the master list(s) of all packages in the current devel branch. (I think) these drive the build system, package propagation, landing package creation etc. You can actually download and look at the manifest files yourself, they’re also in a git repository: > > git clone[https://git.bioconductor.org/admin/manifest.git](https://git.bioconductor.org/admin/manifest.git) > > I use the software package list to determine what’s available in the code browser. If a package get’s deprecated and removed from the manifest, it disappears from the code browser. That’s probably not ideal, but when I was developing the site the manifest seemed a good way to easily obtain a comprehensive list of packages and to know when a new package was added to Bioc that I needed to add to the browser. > > I presume the list of packages in a particular release is just whatever is in the Manifest on the date of the release freeze - but I don’t actually know that for sure. There’s not anyRELEASE_X_Ybranches in the manifest repo that pinpoint those moments, but there are commits where multiple packages are removed around past release times. > > I don’t know much about the download counts, but I suspect you’re correct that the download counts are across all versions of the packages, andFEMstill gets a small number of download for the old versions. Maybe@Robert Shearknows more, I believe he’s spent some time looking at the download statistics and might know how the counts are aggregated.

Lluís Revilla (12:13:19) (in thread): > Thanks Mike, I am not sure what happened, because when I tried git clone ... yesterday it didn’t clone the repo. Many thanks for maintaining thecode.bioconductor.orgsite and a glimpse behind the curtains on how it works.

Robert Shear (12:13:41) (in thread): > It appears as though FEM was removed in the 3.12 release, so the last published release is 3.11. You can access it athttp://www.bioconductor.org/packages/3.11/bioc/html/FEM.html. If you want to see all the packages in 3.12, you can visithttp://bioconductor.org/packages/3.12/. - Attachment (Bioconductor): FEM > The FEM package performs a systems-level integrative analysis of DNA methylation and gene expression data. It seeks modules of functionally related genes which exhibit differential promoter DNA methylation and differential expression, where an inverse association between promoter DNA methylation and gene expression is assumed. For full details, see Jiao et al Bioinformatics 2014.

Lluís Revilla (12:14:18) (in thread): > I was surprised because it is removed and it has almost the same amount of downloads as one of my packages (I was thinking this could be used to control for “background”/automatic downloads of Bioconductor). Thanks!

Lluís Revilla (12:15:31) (in thread): > That’s what I did@Robert Shear! A publication used FEM and I wanted to avoid rewriting it from scratch. Thanks!

Robert Shear (12:16:26) (in thread): > All of the packages accessed viawww.bioconductor.orgorbioconductor.orgare in the download count. As you suspect, nothing from the mirrors is counted. Access viagit.bioconductor.orgis not counted.

Caterina (15:34:49): > @Caterina has joined the channel

Amos Schorr (17:13:09): > Hey folks! If you’re curious about what the near future of bio looks like in the wetlab, boy do I have a treat for you! We’ve got 4 leaders talking IN A REALLY GROUNDED WAY about what these changes might be and what you might want to think about to best equip yourself to do goddamn brilliant work on July 12th. Free for student & academics. Pay what you wish for everyone else.https://www.eventbrite.com/e/near-future-skills-gaps-14-the-wetlab-tickets-639557713337 - Attachment (Eventbrite): Near Future Skills Gaps (1/4): The Wetlab > A virtual panel of leaders in aging biology discussing neglected topics central to doing good work & building a career.

Amos Schorr (05:04:41): > Hi! We’ve got 2 virtual events coming up that I’d love you to join and share with good folks you know. They’re focused on near-future skill gaps in aging biology research. On July 26th, we’ll be discussing CompBio with Jacob Kimmel (co-founder @ NewLimit), Ravi Pandya (Sr. Director, ML & Computational Platform @ Altos Labs), and Jarod Rutledge (co-founder @ Teal Omics, a Wyss-Coray lab spinout). And on August 9th, we’ll be discussing software & ML with Joe Betts-LaCroix (CEO @ Retro Bio), Luca Naef (CTO, VantAI), and our very own @Andy Lee (CTO, Vincere Biosciences).https://www.eventbrite.com/e/near-future-skill-gaps-24-computational-biology-tickets-665910445067https://www.eventbrite.com/e/near-future-skill-gaps-34-software-ml-tickets-677946495217 - Attachment (Eventbrite): Near Future Skill Gaps (2/4): Computational Biology > A virtual panel of leaders in aging biology discussing neglected topics central to doing good work & building a career. - Attachment (Eventbrite): Near Future Skill Gaps (3/4): Software & ML > A virtual panel of leaders in aging biology discussing neglected topics central to doing good work & building a career.

Abdullah Al Nahid (10:49:31): > Hi everyone, I created a simple chrome extension to access PDF files directly, to avoid waiting for this screen (screenshot attached) to load. > It will inject a floating PDF icon in the bottom right corner of your screen when you visit such a paper with epdf like in ASM, Willey. > > Here’s the downloadable zip file and the source code of the extension that you can unzip and load into your browser:https://github.com/nahid18/journal-epdf-downloaderFeel free to star the repo if you like it - File (PNG): image.png

Dirk Eddelbuettel (14:06:25): > My google-fu is failing me. I had glanced at, and quite enjoyed, JJ’s keynote at this year’s BioC Conf about quarto, manuscripts and all that jazz and have now tried twice to share them but … can no longer find it. Is there a public repo with his slides including the short embedded screen capture videos at the end?

Hervé Pagès (15:12:04) (in thread): > Don’t know about the slides but the video of his presentation is available for replay on Hopin athttps://app.hopin.com/events/bioc2023/replay

2023-08-11

Sarah Parker (12:36:23) (in thread): > Here are the slides! He used a quarto presentation–https://jjallaire.quarto.pub/reproducible-manuscripts-with-quarto/#/title-slideYou can find all his quarto pubs athttps://jjallaire.quarto.pub/

Dirk Eddelbuettel (12:37:55) (in thread): > My google-fu and memory are clearly bad because I once knew that too, of course. Silly me looked for his GH repos and found nuttin and was perplexed. Quarto domain it is, of course. Thanks a million!

Chahat Bhatia (05:08:17): > @Chahat Bhatia has joined the channel

rohitsatyam102 (15:45:36): > Hi Every. Can anyone here tell if there is an R package for calculating dN/dS or pN/pS based on SNPEff annotated VCF and reference FASTA and GTF file. I am aware that there is a package called dNdScv but it is highly biased toward using annotation from Ensembl and idk how to calculate CDS start and End per transcript from GTF file.

Amanda Hiser (16:41:24): > @Amanda Hiser has left the channel

Abdullah Al Nahid (21:27:43): > @Abdullah Al Nahid has left the channel

Hervé Pagès (22:53:09) (in thread): > > idk how to calculate CDS start and End per transcript from GTF file. > Import the file as a TxDb object withGenomicFeatures::makeTxDbFromGFF(), then do (assumingtxdbis your TxDb object): > > tx <- transcripts(txdb) > > ## Add 'cds_start' and 'cds_end' metadata columns: > > tx_lens <- transcriptLengths(txdb, with.cds_len=TRUE, > with.utr5_len=TRUE, > with.utr3_len=TRUE) > # sanity check > stopifnot(identical(tx_lens$tx_id, mcols(tx)$tx_id)) > > cds_start <- start(tx) + > ifelse(strand(tx) == "+", tx_lens$utr5_len, tx_lens$utr3_len) > cds_end <- end(tx) - > ifelse(strand(tx) == "+", tx_lens$utr3_len, tx_lens$utr5_len) > > no_cds_idx <- which(tx_lens$cds_len == 0L) > cds_start[no_cds_idx] <- NA > cds_end[no_cds_idx] <- NA > > mcols(tx)$cds_start <- cds_start > mcols(tx)$cds_end <- cds_end > > This is whattxlooks like with the 2 additional metadata cols: > > > tx > GRanges object with 29173 ranges and 4 metadata columns: > seqnames ranges strand | tx_id tx_name cds_start > <Rle> <IRanges> <Rle> | <integer> <character> <integer> > [1] chr2L 7529-9484 + | 1 FBtr0300689 7680 > [2] chr2L 7529-9484 + | 2 FBtr0300690 7680 > [3] chr2L 7529-9484 + | 3 FBtr0330654 7680 > [4] chr2L 21952-24237 + | 4 FBtr0309810 <NA> > [5] chr2L 66584-71390 + | 5 FBtr0306539 67116 > ... ... ... ... . ... ... ... > [29169] chrYHet 319739-320997 - | 29169 FBtr0114244 <NA> > [29170] chrYHet 327052-328489 - | 29170 FBtr0114245 <NA> > [29171] chrUextra 523024-523048 - | 29171 FBtr0330363 <NA> > [29172] chrUextra 523024-523086 - | 29172 FBtr0330361 <NA> > [29173] chrUextra 523060-523086 - | 29173 FBtr0330362 <NA> > cds_end > <integer> > [1] 8610 > [2] 9276 > [3] 8610 > [4] <NA> > [5] 70895 > ... ... > [29169] <NA> > [29170] <NA> > [29171] <NA> > > I wish there was a simpler way though. I’ll think about adding something toGenomicFeatures. > Note that this is the kind of question you would typically ask on our support site.

2023-10-18

Daniel Niiaziiev (07:07:42): > @Daniel Niiaziiev has joined the channel

Jenea Adams (09:39:43): > Hi there! We’re (BWCB) on a mission to capture >= 100 responses from computational biologists in our first CompBio Perspectives Survey. We’d like to understand perceptions of resources and opportunity from computational biologists globally. It takes just ~2 minutes to complete and you can find ithere. Feel free to also pass along to others in your network. - Attachment (Google Docs): CompBio Perspectives Survey > WHAT IS THIS SURVEY? > We’re on a mission to learn from at least 100 computational biologists’ professional experiences. > > This is a brief survey The Black Women in Computational Biology Network (BWCB) is deploying to understand the distribution of resources and support across our diverse scientist community. > > This survey should take about 2 minutes. > > WHO SHOULD TAKE THIS SURVEY? > All computational biologists are welcome to complete this survey! If you’re not already registered as a BWCB supporter, you’ll be directed to do so at the end of this survey if you wish. However, prior BWCB affiliation or knowledge is not a prerequisite for this survey. > > WHO SHOULD NOT TAKE THIS SURVEY? > :exclamation:If you’re an active BWCB member (Black women in compbio) do NOT complete this survey! Please check your email inbox for the link to your personalized survey or email mailto:info@blackowmencompbio.org|info@blackowmencompbio.org if you can’t find it :exclamation: > > HOW WILL THIS INFORMATION BE USED? > Your answers to the experience and demographic sections will be anonymized and analyzed in aggregate. This information will provide valuable insights into the challenges and opportunities faced by individuals in the computational biology field. The data will be used to inform strategies for improving inclusivity, diversity, and support within the computational biology field. > Rest assured, your responses are completely anonymous. No personally identifiable information will be linked to your survey data. > As we strive to capture the richness of experiences, we may include individual quotes in the final report. However, these quotes will be anonymized, and any identifying details will be removed to ensure confidentiality. > Your participation is instrumental in shaping the future of computational biology, and we appreciate your candid and thoughtful responses. > Feel free to share with other computational biologists in your network. > > Questions? email us mailto:info@blackwomencompbio.org|info@blackwomencompbio.org

Thanks! - Attachment (Meetup): R-Ladies Rome | Meetup > ****R-Ladies Rome (rladiesrome.quarto.pub/website/) is a local chapter of R-Ladies Global ([rladies.org](http://rladies.org)), a global organization dedicated to promoting gender diver

Devon Kohler (08:52:16): > Dear friends and colleagues: > > The May Institute on Computation and Statistics for Mass Spectrometry and Proteomics, taking place on April 29 – May 10, 2024 on the campus of Northeastern University in Boston MA, is now accepting applications. The application deadline is February 15, 2024. > > Instructors are leading experts, who contributed numerous experimental and computational methods and software. The target audiences are both beginners and experienced scientists, with both experimental and computational expertise. > > Participants can select a subset of the following programs: > > * April 29 - May 1, Quantitative proteomics with Skyline. Lead instructors: Tina Ludwig, Brendan MacLean and Lindsay Pino > * May 1 – May 3, Beginners R and beginners statistics. Lead instructor: Ryan Benz > * May 1 – May 3, Intermediate R, data visualization and statistics. Lead instructor: Kylie Bemis > * May 6 – May 8, Statistics for quantitative mass spectrometry: methods and case studies with MSstats. Lead instructors: Olga Vitek and Devon Kohler > * May 6, Analysis of mass spectrometry images with Cardinal. Lead instructor: Kylie Bemis > * May 7, Modern software development practice with Python. Lead instructor: Charles Tapley Hoyt > * May 8, Scientific writing. Lead instructor: Alicia Williams > * May 8 – May 10, Interpretation of proteomic experiments in the context of biomolecular networks with INDRA. Lead instructor: Ben Gyori > * May 10, Barnett Institute 50th Anniversary and Karger Medal Celebration. Featured speakers: Jenny Van Eyk and Nikolai Slavov > > A limited number of registration fee wavers will be available for students and postdocs affiliated with academic or nonprofit institutions in the US. > > Please share this widely. More information is athttps://computationalproteomics.khoury.northeastern.edu/

Steve Lianoglou (18:07:49): > Today I was working up an analysis where I needed to export some genomic ranges as a *.bed file … > > I thought I’d leverage thertracklayer::export.bed()function to make my life just a bit easier to do that, and when R complained on my call tolibrary(rtacklayer), I figured this is a package that needs making to help with the DIY projects around the homestead …

2024-02-02

Alan O’C (11:21:18): > Has anybody here submitted + revised an F1000 paper? We submitted a latex document originally, and in our post-review revisions are now being asked to track changes their docx with the revised version, which would take… far too long… and be completely useless, in terms of references/figures/basically everything. Is it worthwhile trying to convince them to take the sensible path?

Lena (11:29:15) (in thread): > they don’t let you track changes usinglatexdiff?

Alan O’C (11:30:08) (in thread): > The direct quote (after I said we previously submitted a latex doc) was > > Unfortunately, we do require all version submissions to be made on the word doc of the published article

Alan O’C (11:30:47) (in thread): > But thanks! I’ll suggest that as an alternative

Lena (11:31:03) (in thread): > sorry, I have no experience with them:confused:it does sound unreasonable

Alan O’C (11:33:50) (in thread): > Wondering if@Aaron Lunremembers if he went through this for v2 ofhttps://f1000research.com/articles/5-2122/v2, if so I am more impressed by this than any amount of research or software output since - Attachment (f1000research.com): F1000Research Article: A step-by-step workflow for low-level analysis of single-cell RNA-seq data with Bioconductor. > Read the latest article version by Aaron T.L. Lun, Davis J. McCarthy, John C. Marioni, at F1000Research.

Aaron Lun (11:48:46) (in thread): > My submission was pure latex IIRC, no docx involved.

Kasper D. Hansen (12:03:51) (in thread): > In my understanding this has changed substantially because F1000Res internal workflow has changed

Kasper D. Hansen (12:04:36) (in thread): > It used to be all latex. I have no recent experience, but you’re not the first one mentioning word and that obviously makes it a somewhat less appealing publishing venue

Alan O’C (12:09:30) (in thread): > Thanks all. Yes, there’s no way I would’ve submitted knowing this in advance. For reproducible manuscripts it makes zero sense > > Also should mention the conversion introduced syntax errors first time around so it’s not even working as intended

2024-02-05

Courtney Zirkle (08:34:07): > @Courtney Zirkle has joined the channel

2024-02-06

Yanina Bellini Saibene (11:34:20): > rOpenSci January news!:rocket:The R-Universe now builds MacOS ARM64 binaries for use on Apple Silicon (aka M1/M2/M3) systems!:calendar:Upcoming coworking sessions: R-universe in February, dates/times/timezones in March.:package:New (or re-reviewed!) packages {GLMMcosinor}, {rangr}, {comtradr}:computer:Package news:spiral_note_pad:4 blog posts including our 2023 Code of Conduct Transparency Report:toolbox:Package Development cornerhttps://ropensci.org/blog/2024/01/25/news-january-2024/ - Attachment (ropensci.org): rOpenSci News Digest, January 2024 > MacOS ARM64 binaries for use on Apple Silicon (aka M1/M2/M3) through R-universe; coworking; new packages and package news

2024-02-07

Lluís Revilla (06:46:10): > I’m having trouble posting tosupport.bioconductor.org. I think I saw a message about it somewhere ( a channel or the mailing list) but I cannot find it or post my question but I see other people posting. Is it experiencing some problems?

Lluís Revilla (06:47:12) (in thread): > I either get a problem with the tags (sometimes it tells me that I need to place a comma to create a tag, other’s just nothing) or a problem with the text of my question (without any information about what is wrong).

Vince Carey (07:19:46) (in thread): > Sorry@Lluís Revillawe have made some changes to deal with spam and there are going to be some bumps. Will be back in touch.

Vince Carey (07:25:24) (in thread): > have you tried clearing browser cache and perhaps using incognito mode? i had no problem with a new post with fresh auth via google.

Lori Shepherd (07:36:30) (in thread): > its not a spam issue its a special character issue most likely if you are copying and pasting text from terminal. we lightened the constraints except for a validity check on ascii characters; we haven’t been able to narrow down what browswer or OS is the root cause but that is likely the cause. Also tags are required there are generic tags and all the packages show up as suggestions but we do require a tag. The default can be Bioconductor

Lluís Revilla (07:40:56) (in thread): > I tried different browsers but it might be a character issue. A warning or message of what is wrong would be helpful to know what to fix… > The problem with tags was not that I didn’t use them but that the tags I added aren’t allowed somehow, even if they appear in the menu when I start writing it.

Lluís Revilla (07:42:04) (in thread): > My OS is ubuntu 22.04 on Firefox 122.0.1 or on Chromium Version 121.0.6167.139 (Official Build) snap (64-bit)

Lori Shepherd (07:44:25) (in thread): > You should still be able to use any free form tag you want but you still need to add at least one tag suggested/approved - if it was not having to do with a package, “Bioconductor” is the catch all, there is also “release” , “devel” , I think we added things like “conferences” , etc…

Lluís Revilla (07:54:19) (in thread): > I desist to post the question here. I upgraded the computer, restarted, cleaned the cache and used several browsers the tags are now valid (at the third try) but no clue what/how to fix the text. > For some more context I copied the output of reprex…

2024-02-11

Siraj Farhan (07:54:50): > @Siraj Farhan has joined the channel

2024-02-13

Maria Doyle (08:25:05) (in thread): > @Lluís Revillawere you able to post at support site? I’m having a similar issue trying to post the message in screenshot below on behalf of CoC committee. It won’t post and I can’t see what the issue is. I’m on Mac and have tried Chrome and Firefox (freshly installed). Can anyone tell what may be wrong? - File (PNG): Screenshot 2024-02-13 at 13.22.36.png

Lori Shepherd (08:27:22) (in thread): > can you send me your text so I can try to reproduce

Maria Doyle (08:28:50) (in thread): > yes below, thanks for taking a look > > Hello Bioconductor Community > > > > It is important to reflect often on the importance of upholding [Bioconductor values][1], our commitment to an open approach to science, collaboration, diversity, inclusivity, and a welcoming environment is at the core of our community. > > > > The Bioconductor Code of Conduct serves as a guiding light, ensuring that we maintain a supportive space for all. Let’s continue working together to nurture an environment that encourages the exchange of ideas and fosters collective growth. Everybody can reach the [Code of Conduct committee][2] if you consider that some situation doesn’t reflect these values. > > > > Thank you for your dedication to the Bioconductor community. > > > > > > [1]:https://bioconductor.github.io/bioc_coc_multilingual/en-US.html[2]:https://bioconductor.github.io/bioc_coc_multilingual/

Lori Shepherd (08:31:25) (in thread): > Its the issue with special characters — when I paste into a text editor, the apostrophe for Let’s and doesn’t ends up as non ascii characters. And we have a validity check that checks for this.

Lori Shepherd (08:31:43) (in thread): > Let�@~Ys and doesn�@~Yt

Lori Shepherd (08:39:30) (in thread): > so i can look into a solution – were you copying and pasting that from somewhere else or typing directly into the support site block?

Maria Doyle (08:41:24) (in thread): > thaks for find that! I copied and pasted from the message I was sent in Slack

Lluís Revilla (10:48:20) (in thread): > I was not able to post to the support site (or Biostars), there is another question in the support site with the same problem. It might be an encoding issue but it would be nice if it would report that to the user so that we can fix and post

Lori Shepherd (11:20:12) (in thread): > yes looking into if its possible to have an informative message when the encoding validation is triggered

Jenea Adams (13:51:17): > Hello all, I have two announcements fromBWCB: > * On February 28th, 2024 we are hosting a public townhall centering community-building around exploring opportunities for allyship in the computational biology community. We will begin by sharing some insights from our survey results capturing the experiences of various communities within the computational biology field. Many of you here participated in that survey, and we appreciate your input! We welcome you to join us for a conversation that will create space to engage critically together on what we’re learning. > > * RSVP here:https://www.blackwomencompbio.org/event-details/townhall-on-opportunities-for-allyship-in-computational-biology > > * We have just released applications for our 2024grant programs. If you work with colleagues or trainees who could benefit from becoming a member applying, I appreciate you sharing this with them. Applications close on March 1, 2024. - Attachment (BlackWomenCompBio): Townhall on Opportunities for Allyship in Computational Biology | BlackWomenCompBio > All in the STEM community are welcome to join us for flash talks an open discussion on the the current state of opportunities for allyship in the field of computational biology. We will also introduce the BWCB Connect program, an initiative to spark connection and networking for all in the field. - File (PNG): 2.png

Hervé Pagès (19:27:44) (in thread): > This apostrophe is UTF-8. UTF-8 characters are very common, especially in R output (e.g. fancy quotes). Any chance the support site can accept them? The pre-rendering accepts them so it’s kind of unexpected that when I press “Save” my answer (which looked fine in the preview) just disappears and seems to be lost forever (I might have spent a lot of time writing it:sweat:).

2024-02-14

Alan O’C (11:06:40) (in thread): > Clicking the workspace name gives me a dropdown - File (PNG): Screenshot from 2024-02-14 16-06-14.png

Louis Le Nézet (11:08:07) (in thread): > Thanks a lot for the quick answer !

2024-02-15

David Rach (09:55:37): > What is your favorite/most-useful R:books:book? I am on a reading binge this year, currently on the last couple chapters of Advanced R, and looking for additional book suggestions:book::worm:

Jacques SERIZAY (13:07:05) (in thread): > Not as well organized as Hadley’s books, butComputational Genomics with Rfrom Altuna Akalin covers most aspects of bioinfo analysis for bulk assays. It has not been updated for quite some time though, and it doesn’t cover at all the tidyomics principles:scream:. - Attachment (compgenomr.github.io): Computational Genomics with R > A guide to computationa genomics using R. The book covers fundemental topics with practical examples for an interdisciplinery audience

Jacques SERIZAY (13:16:16) (in thread): > While this thread is open, any suggestion forpythonpackage development? Package dev approaches in python are a lot more diverse than in R. Any suggestion for a good, clear guide for package dev (not coding)?

Martin Morgan (15:51:03) (in thread): > Since you’re at the end of r4ds, I’d suggest ‘The Art of R Programming’ from Norman Matloff. Most of the book is kind of an anti-r4ds, emphasizing an understanding of the language foundations. Chapters 9 (object-oriented programming) and 13+ (debugging, performance, interfacing to other languages, parallel) are pretty unique (‘dated’ if looked at now, ‘visionary’ if at the time of writing). Maybe especially interesting (and challenging ‘exercises for the reader’) are when Matloff says that something is one way (forloops scale quadratically, so are slow for big data,1:100000allocates a lot of memory) but nowforloops are not particularly slow, and1:100000actually occupies very little space. What changed inR? What lessons can we as (advanced)Rprogrammers learn?

Lluís Revilla (18:11:50) (in thread): > For python package development I recommend to create an R package and wrap it with rpy2:wink:. For R you can read everything on the Big Book of R:https://www.bigbookofr.com/I think there are more than 200 books. - Attachment (bigbookofr.com): Big Book of R > 400+ Free R programming books

Jayaram Kancherla (18:32:44) (in thread): > @Jacques SERIZAYwe put together a guide on how we develop packages in biocpy -https://github.com/BiocPy/developer_guide

Charlie George (05:22:01): > Hi everyone, I’ve been involved in a projectwww.x-net.bioto research interdisciplinary career research barriers in the UK and propose recommendations to address them, much of our research focused on those at the intersection of biomedical science and computation/statistics such as yourselves. We’ve just launched our series of 13 recommendations to UK funders which hope to support and insentise a more collaborative and supportive research culture for all. You can see the tweetorial summarising the efforts herehttps://x.com/CGATist/status/1764926690126401926?s=20please retweet so it reaches as many colleagues as possible. Many thanks:slightly_smiling_face: - Attachment (X (formerly Twitter)): Chris Ponting :blue_heart: (@CGATist) on X > NEWS:tada: Today X-Net launched recommendations to @UKRI_News to tackle barriers for #InterdisciplinaryResearch careers > :point_down:we explain how these were developed with researchers, public & industry > ​https://t.co/Plrs5pyTIZ > Collaboration among @mrc_hgu @MRC_WIMM @mrcppu @KavliOxford 1/12

Precious Orakwe (13:09:32): > please can someone guide me on how to start contributing

Aleru Divine (13:11:45) (in thread): > Hi@Precious Orakweplease join the#bugsigdbchannel

Marcel Ramos Pérez (13:12:20) (in thread): > Hi Precious, you can join the#outreachychannel for more guidance. Also of relevance:https://community-bioc.slack.com/archives/C35G93GJH/p1709746183127649 - Attachment: Attachment > Welcome Outreachy applicants! Please join #bugsigdb for Microbiome Study Curation (BugSigDB) discussions. We also have an Outreachy-specific channel #outreachy. Project mentors are on those channels and we can better support you there. This channel #general alerts the entire community so it’s better to do introductions on #outreachy. Thank you and good luck!

Yanina Bellini Saibene (14:50:10): > From a Researcher (Ashley Juavinett): > > We are conducting a study to understand professional experiences in computing within the context of biology.:computer::herb::microscope:If you are in a biology-related field AND use some amount of computing (e.g. writing code) in your current role, indicate interest in a 1-hour interview with $25 compensation here:https://forms.gle/YXozM6Wx2ZwmruVp8The study is open undergrad/grad students, postdocs, faculty, & folks in industry. > > Please share!:pray: - Attachment (Google Docs): Sign-Up Form: Computing Paths in Biology Interview > Thank you for your interest in participating in an interview to help us learn more about computing paths in biology! Please fill out the following information to indicate your interest. If selected to participate, Austin Zuckerman (Ph.D. student working with Ashley Juavinett) will reach out to you to officially schedule a time and date! The interview will last approximately one hour and you will receive a $25 gift card for participating.

2024-04-13

Yanina Bellini Saibene (14:49:06): > I have the pleasure of chatting at the Sustain Podcast:slightly_smiling_face: > > Yani Bellini Saibene explores funding challenges, sustainability in volunteer models, diversity in open source, addressing global disparities, and community empowerment. > Listen athttps://podcast.sustainoss.org/228If you like it, please share it!:pray:Here are the links: > * LinkedIn > * Discourse > * Mastodon > * Bluesky - Attachment (Sustain): Yani Bellini Saibene on better scientific coding communities > Yani explores funding challenges, sustainability in volunteer models, diversity in open source, addressing global disparities, and community empowerment.

Siraj Farhan (15:19:03): > Hey all! > > I’m the founder ofSynthia Labs, excited to share our AI agent (like ChatGPT but for Bioinformatics) we’ve been developing toaccelerate bioinformatics tool development. It features capabilities likedirect editing & executing of scripts(Python, R, bash),installing packages,handling large datasetsandintegrating custom Docker imagesCheck out our3-min demohere:https://www.loom.com/share/e94083ed1c354dc1abd32e46c68c3624We’re offeringearly access! Please fill out thisquick formto register your interest:https://forms.gle/b2rV4AnPyGRACMVB6Thanks for helping us advance bioinformatics! - Attachment (Loom): Synthia | An AI Scientist - Attachment (Google Docs): AI in Bioinformatics > Your input will help us understand the current use of language models in bioinformatics and identify the key challenges faced by the community. > Thank you for your valuable insights and support in advancing AI in bioinformatics! > The Synthia Labs Team

2024-04-24

Izabela Mamede (08:26:36): > Hello Bioconductor community, > I’m interested in exploring RNA modifications from nanopore data. Could anyone recommend tools and packages specifically designed for this purpose? Are any inside BioConductor? Additionally, I’m curious if there are any streamlined workflows available for this analysis, or if any research teams are actively working on developing them. Any insights or recommendations would be greatly appreciated. Thank you!

Lance Zhang (09:35:09): > @Lance Zhang has joined the channel

Changqing (19:52:12) (in thread): > NanoMethViz?

2024-04-25

Nan Deng (01:20:57): > @Nan Deng has joined the channel

Izabela Mamede (07:08:49) (in thread): > When I used this for the last time it worked perfect for DNA data, not so much for RNA (good for m6a not m5c etc), and only for methylation. But thanks for the suggestion, I will check if they made any new releases.

AbuZar Khan (07:52:13): > @AbuZar Khan has joined the channel

Peter Hickey (21:07:37) (in thread): > @Shian Su^^^

Shian Su (21:24:33) (in thread): > This is something I’d like to support, but it’s a bit difficult without test data. In theory NanoMethViz works for any kind of modification mapped to a reference. In practice the plots are calibrated for m5c in DNA. I think Nanopore’s ModKit is probably your best bet for some exploratory analysis. > > If you could describe any specific issues you’re encountering I am happy to try and help. I’m personally working on some m6a C.elegans DNA data right now and the data is simultaneously too dense (too many A sites compared to CG motifs I’ve calibrated to) and too sparse (the prevalence of methylation is generally <5%).

2024-04-26

Joel Nitta (01:19:35): > @Joel Nitta has joined the channel

Martin Emons (06:41:51): > @Martin Emons has joined the channel

Izabela Mamede (08:30:17) (in thread): > I am trying ModKit right now! For now I have only one test data of RNA of chikungunya infection in human cells, the ideia would be to see what i can get from it and if it is worth it to sequence more samples/other infections. I really appreciate your suggestions, I am having a similar problem, since NanoMethViz is not fully calibrated for other mods/ or RNA.

Stevie Pederson (10:12:26) (in thread): > OMG!!! Their system literally removes all line breaks from your code!!! Their solution is to ask you to re-submit each code chunk as an individual, standalone Word document so they can see the formatting, despite having all of the code correctly formatted (& tested) in the original submission. Thanks for warning me@Alan O’Cand I very much wish I’d listened. Although I must admit, no-one in their right mind would’ve imagined a system that does what they’re doing

Yanina Bellini Saibene (08:43:42): > rOpenSci May news!:sparkles::label:rOpenSci in the Research Organization Registry:star-struck:What are the rOpenSci Champions up to now?:calendar:Upcoming coworking sessions: “R in the Wild” in June, “Git and Github” in July:deciduous_tree:New package {dendroNetwork}:package:Package news:spiral_note_pad:Communication Tips for your Open-Source Project:wave:Calls for contributions:toolbox:Package Development cornerhttps://ropensci.org/blog/2024/05/24/ropensci-news-may-2024/ - Attachment (ropensci.org): rOpenSci News Digest, May 2024 > Champions program update, metadat, new packages and package news

何璇 (03:26:58): > @何璇 has joined the channel

Nicolai Karcher (08:31:14): > @Nicolai Karcher has joined the channel

2024-05-28

Jenny Drnevich (11:28:01): > For anyone with experience with NIH’s biosketch format - the instructions explicitly say we can cite research products like educational curricula or software, but “Use of hyperlinks and URLs to cite these items is not allowed.”. How in the world are we supposed to cite things like this that solely exist on line and are not published?! Even more confusing is the link they give to the FAQ about how to cite interim research projects in the proper format says to use a DOI and gives a URL in their example! I thought people here would likely have software that fits in this category and could share how they cite it in NIH’s biosketch.https://grants.nih.gov/grants/how-to-apply-application-guide/forms-h/general/g.240-r&r-seniorkey-person-profile-(expanded)-form.htm#Instructions

Marouen Ben Guebila (11:37:06) (in thread): > Hey Jenny, I think it is ok to use URLs in the new Biosketch format. Hyperlinks however are not allowed because they may be used to trace back reviewers. (https://netzoo.github.iovs https://netzoo.gitub.io)

Jenny Drnevich (11:51:48) (in thread): > I could see why they don’t want embedded hyperlinks but the instructions also say no URLs. This is the first time my biosketch has gotten flagged and I’ve had them in for years, so I wondered if this was a new requirement?

Marouen Ben Guebila (12:03:42) (in thread): > Oh this seems new then, I’ve gotten away with URLs without hyperlinks in the past, so they may be enforcing it now.

Frederick Tan (12:04:42) (in thread): > Comments in this post suggest there is some enforcement variability between institutes > * https://www.nia.nih.gov/research/blog/2021/04/missing-links-why-nih-doesnt-allow-hyperlinks-grant-applications

Jenny Drnevich (12:07:08) (in thread): > Thanks, Marouen and Fred!

Jenny Drnevich (12:14:24) (in thread): > And of course there is conflicting information from NIH about this! “Douse hyperlinks in relevant citations and publications included in biosketches and publication list attachments”https://nexus.od.nih.gov/all/2019/05/13/the-dos-donts-of-hyperlinks-in-grant-applications/ - Attachment (nexus.od.nih.gov): The Do’s & Don’ts of Hyperlinks in Grant Applications > It would be hard to read more than a couple paragraphs on the internet these days without encountering a hyperlink to a definition or additional clarifying information. Hyperlinks are everywhere. So, why does NIH limit the use of hyperlinks in grant applications?

2024-05-29

Ezekiel Ekanem (16:02:35): > @Ezekiel Ekanem has joined the channel

2024-05-30

Aedin Culhane (04:42:29) (in thread): > Bioconductor packages all have DOI.So can we just provide the doi but not hyperlink

Jenny Drnevich (08:22:13) (in thread): > Update: turns out my Grants & Contracts just required me to take out embedded links but plain text to the URL was fine. Giovanni Palla (11:17:29): > :star2:Join Us at the Scverse Conference 2024!:star2::spiral_calendar_pad:Dates: September 10-12, 2024:round_pushpin:Location:Munich, Germany:memo:Details & Registration:Scverse Conference 2024Scverse is hosting its first conference in Munich. It will hosts talks, poster sessions and workshop on open source software for single cell and spatial genomics.Speakers: > * Fabian Theis- Helmholtz Munich > * Alex Wolf- Lamin > * Angela Pisco- Insitro > * Rob Patro- University of Maryland > * Christina Leslie- MSKCC > * Maria Brbic- EPFL > :earth_africa:Travel Grants Available: Need support to attend? We’re offering travel grants. Don’t let anything hold you back from this opportunity!:rocket:Call for Participation: We’re still accepting abstract submissions and workshop proposals! Contribute your ideas and engage with peers and leaders in the field.:link:Submit Your Ideas:Abstracts & WorkshopsMark your calendars and get ready for an enlightening experience. We’re excited to see you there!:loudspeaker:Spread the Word: Help us make Scverse Conference 2024 a milestone event by sharing this announcement with your colleagues and across your networks! > > #scverse #CompBio #OpenSource

Jared Andrews (17:19:52): > TheKIDS24 BioHackathonsignup is now OPEN!:date:Event Date:September 4-6, 2024:round_pushpin:Location:St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital - Memphis, TN:computer:Participation:In-person and Virtual > Join us for three days of intensive coding full of innovation, collaboration, and fun! Whether you’re attending in person or joining us virtually, this is your chance to make a difference and showcase your skills. Don’t miss out on this incredible opportunity to be part of something impactful.Register now and secure your spot!Register HereIf you have any questions please emailbiohackathon@stjude.orgWe can’t wait to see you there!:rocket:I am involved in planning this event at St. Jude, and we’re looking for enthusiastic folks to take a look. Come help build something cool and useful, see what St. Jude is about, and learn some new skills! Feel free to post here or DM me with any questions. > > Students and trainees are very welcome, so if anyone in your group/institution may be interested, please pass it along! Limited travel awards will be available.

Bianka Berki (05:46:03): > @Bianka Berki has joined the channel

Danielle Callan (11:48:47): > I’d like to share something I’m excited about: > > TheMicrobiomeDBteam recently developed a suite of R packages to accompany the website (if you aren’t familiar, check it out!). It supports the same types of analyses (alpha diversity, beta diversity, differential abundance, correlations, etc) as the site, via an API thats designed to be user-friendly and question-centric. Its built on a custom data container that is flexible enough to support a wide variety of data, while performant enough to also serve as the backbone of the site. You can import your own data from TreeSummarizedExperiments (and thanks to the great folks building mia, various other formats as well) or work with our curated data. > > Find more details here:https://microbiomedb.github.io/MicrobiomeDB/We’d love feedback! So don’t hesitate to reach out. There is aDiscord Server, as well, if anyone were interested.

2024-06-27

Steffen Neumann (07:05:25): > Hi, we have a not-really BioC question, more R basics: We develop a package which depends on another one. Duh. > The dependency ishttps://github.com/PecanProject/pecan/tree/develop/modules/rtmbut also onhttps://pecanproject.r-universe.dev/PEcAnRTMOur github actions fail because it can’t resolve this package. > We tried Remotes: PEcAnRTM=github::PecanProject/pecan/modules/rtm/. (without the trailing dot you get a 404, now we getdeps::.: ! pkgdepends resolution error for deps::..Note that the directory name != package name). > While you can install.packages('PEcAnRTM', repos = '[https://pecanproject.r-universe.dev](https://pecanproject.r-universe.dev)'), theDESCRIPTION/Additional_repositoriesis only supported in R CMD check, not when actually installing.

Kerry (10:06:23) (in thread): > this is github package issue 404 issue with the credentials. > usethis::create_github_token() > > I tried to install the PEcAnRTM and failed to compile, so I think there may be an issue with the package

Kerry (10:13:59) (in thread): > devtools::install_github(“PecanProject/pecan”, subdir=“modules/rtm”, ref=‘develop’,auth_token = “addintokenhere”) > > however, I’m getting the zero-exit status as I’m missing two dependencies ERROR: dependencies ‘PEcAn.logger’, ‘PEcAn.assim.batch’ are not available for package ‘PEcAnRTM’

2024-08-16

Camila Pereira Perico (19:13:20): > Hello bioconductor community. > I have a question regarding the versions submitted to bioconductor. > I submitted a version of my package via git. Shortly afterwards, I added some changes and submitted a new version. > The old version had a ‘check’ error and I received an e-mail informing me of the error. Unfortunately, I can’t upload the fixes for the old version and I keep getting emails informing me of the error. > > Would it be worth contacting bioconductor to remove the old version? Could you help me?

Lori Shepherd (19:52:46) (in thread): > You should be able to update with the newer version of the code to Bioconductor. If you have trouble pushing to git we can assist. Keep in mind for changes to take place you need a valid version bump. Also keep in mind there are two active branches at all times, release and devel. Is it possible you only updated one branch and are still getting emails for the other branch?

Camila Pereira Perico (20:01:44) (in thread): > Hi Lori, thank you very much for your reply. > I don’t know if this is the problem. The package is rSWeeP. The release version is 1.16.0. The faulty version is 1.16.1 and the latest version (devel) that is working correctly is 1.17.2. > I’m just worried that I’m still getting notifications about this old version, because version 1.17.2 has been checked and is OK. > Do I need to take any action?

Lori Shepherd (20:35:56) (in thread): > You should push changes to fix release to the RELEASE_3_19 branch and bump to 1.16.2.

Camila Pereira Perico (20:49:38) (in thread): > Got it. Thank you so much for your help Lori.

Lori Shepherd (20:51:15) (in thread): > Let us know if you have any other questions or concerns.:grin:

2024-08-17

Vince Carey (10:23:56): > https://developers.slashdot.org/comments.pl?sid=23440588&cid=64753016 - Attachment (developers.slashdot.org): VS Code Fork ‘Cursor’ - the ChatGPT of Coding? - Slashdot > So I pasted it into Claude and got this and this test case, from which I get this output.You’re the one tasking this problem, so you tell us whether it works and whether you want improvements anywhere. This took seconds, and even if you’re not happy with it, it’s bare minimum a framework (and reque…

2024-09-02

Laurent Gatto (03:20:21): > The package landing pages have a ‘In Bioconductor since’ field that gives the years since a package is in Bioc and it’s fifst Bioc and R versions. I can get all sorts of information about a package with biocViews package, but apparently not this one. Does anyone know of the ‘In Bioconductor since’ is available programmatically, or is it somehow derived from the version (and if so, how).

Laurent Gatto (03:32:37) (in thread): > BiocPkgTools doesn’t have this information either, as far as I can see.

Laurent Gatto (03:35:22) (in thread): > Progress: I can extract the first download stats (which should reflect month/year of first release) fromBiocPkgTools::biocDownloadStats()

Laurent Gatto (04:45:42) (in thread): > Although these only go until 2009.

Lori Shepherd (06:24:36) (in thread): > I can look tomorrow since today’s the holiday but yes it’s programmatically. I think it’s a cross from when it’s in the manifest and the release dates. Which is why I think at some point we can only do a year+ from when we started using manifests. I’ll look officially tomorrow tho

Laurent Gatto (06:49:05) (in thread): > Thank you

Lluís Revilla (10:57:24) (in thread): > The download include the devel branch. If a package is accepted after a new Bioconductor version it might have more months than being published. Maybe you could check the commit date of version changes in the major.minor

2024-09-03

Lori Shepherd (09:00:39) (in thread): > confirmed it does a cross check against the release dates and manifests

2024-09-04

Gobi Dasu (18:23:49): > I wanted to share this and get some feedback:https://blog.ldtalentwork.com/2019/03/13/how-to-integrate-scraped-products-into-your-woocommerce-site/. - Attachment (LD Talent Blog): How to integrate scraped products into your Woocommerce site - LD Talent Blog > What is scraping ? Web scraping is a process through which certain websites are crawled or scraped and important information extracted from them according to pre-defined specifications. This information is then formatted and stored in a database for business applications. What’s woocommerce ? WooCommerce is an open-source e-commerce plugin for WordPress. It is designed for …

Sean Davis (16:26:23): > A little off-topic, but here is a set of medical-student-curated AI in medicine resources that my students chose to engage with (self-directed learning) during our AI in medicine course. It includes ethics, legal, clinical, research, technical topics in the form of videos, papers, podcasts, blog posts, etc. Particularly for clinical colleagues asking you about AI, this list (though long) has been through at least one medical student’s hands.https://bit.ly/ai-med-resources - Attachment (Google Docs): Contributed AI Learning Resources

Yanina Bellini Saibene (07:06:21): > Nice to close the year accomplishing objectives :-):sunglasses::grin:Very proud of this guide that we generated in rOpenSci: everything we developed and learned during the translations and localizations of our content is detailed in this book: the processes, the roles, and the technical tools. > > We hope it will be helpful to other groups and communities!:writing_hand:Blog post ByYanina Bellini Saibene,Maëlle Salmon,Pao Corrales,Elio Campitelli– Edited bySteffi LaZerte:https://ropensci.org/blog/2024/12/17/localization-guide/:toolbox:rOpenSci Localization and Translation Guidelines. Why and how we localize and translate our resources:https://translationguide.ropensci.orgBoth content are in English and Spanish. Feedback is appreciated.:slightly_smiling_face:

Maria Doyle (07:17:48) (in thread): > Congrats on the guide, Yani! Thanks for sharing – it looks like a great resource. It might also be worth posting in#documentation-translation!

Yanina Bellini Saibene (07:20:52) (in thread): > Thanks@Maria Doyle! Shared there too:slightly_smiling_face:

Yanina Bellini Saibene (11:10:56): > [Blog post] Open Science and Open Source only with Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility by The rOpenSci Team. > > Including all of humanity is and always will be at the heart of open science.https://ropensci.org/blog/2025/02/05/no-science-without-deia/ - Attachment (ropensci.org): Open Science and Open Source only with Diversity, Equity, Inclusion, and Accessibility > Including all of humanity is and always will be at the heart of open science.

Akriti (21:37:14): > @Akriti has joined the channel

Ouma Ronald (01:58:14) (in thread): > hi@Yanina Bellini Saibene. Is this also another opensource community under microbiome? Just wanted to know.

Yanina Bellini Saibene (03:35:14) (in thread): > Hi Ouma, Idon’tthink so, what microbiome is?

Yanina Bellini Saibene (03:37:28) (in thread): > We are fiscal sponsored by NumFOCUS if that helps with your question

Ouma Ronald (03:39:38) (in thread): > I read the message but didn’t get it right and that’s why I asked if its another community but under community-bioc

Maria Doyle (05:42:59) (in thread): > Hi Ouma, > Just to clarify, rOpenSci is a separate open-source community focused on general R packages. You can learn more about them athttps://ropensci.org/. Bioconductor focuses on bioinformatics and computational biology, including microbiome research. Both communities share a commitment to open science and open source. > Hope this helps clear things up! - Attachment (ropensci.org): rOpenSci > Open Tools and R Packages for Open Science

2025-03-02

Sean Davis (07:10:16): > Over the last 48 hours, critical biomedical data infrastructure such as pubmed, NCBI, and some NIH institutional sites have been either down or only intermittently functional. I put together a status page here:https://stats.uptimerobot.com/Zrqh8AhvKnto track a few of these resources. I can add up to 40 more sites. Let me know (in a thread, please) if you have additional US government sites that are critical to your work that you want to track. And, of course, feel free to share with colleagues and politicians for visibility and awareness.

Izabela Mamede (09:24:03) (in thread): > Hey! Thanks for the resource, it is very useful. Could you add blasthttps://blast.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov?

Vince Carey (10:10:15) (in thread): > the uptimerobot system may be inaccurate concerningreporter.nih.gov… which is slow but seems to be up at this time

Sean Davis (10:12:06) (in thread): > Thx,@Vince Carey. I adjusted the timeout.

Martin Morgan (12:29:17) (in thread): > I noticed thathttps://ccr.cancer.govis tagged as ‘forbidden’, but is accessible via my browser. Also, I wonder if the time zone could be changed to EST (or dynamic based on origin of request) to reflect the US basis of the results. And perhaps there are pages to be linked to where planned / known service interruptions are provided for some of these sites?

Godfrey Wagutu (13:10:21): > @Godfrey Wagutu has joined the channel

isoah gracious (13:29:09): > @isoah gracious has joined the channel

Sean Davis (13:34:59) (in thread): > I’ve gotten a TON of interest from folks about this coffee-time project. The infrastructure is actually a commercial service, so I don’t have much control over it. Given the interest, urgency, and importance, I’ll probably put in a little time to work on a more configurable, open version.

Sean Davis (12:55:44): > The Cold Spring Harbor Lab course,https://meetings.cshl.edu/courses.aspx?course=C-DATA, has extended the deadline for application to March 15. There is a cost associated with the course, but there are funds for financial hardship or need available. - Attachment (meetings.cshl.edu): Statistical Analysis of Genome Scale Data > Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory Meetings & Courses – a private, non-profit institution with research programs in cancer, neuroscience, plant biology, genomics, bioinformatics.

Julien Roux (01:28:38): > The abstract call for the BC2 conference (Basel, Switzerland, 8-10 Sept) is closing tomorrow, March 6th. There are quite some sessions of this bioinformatics-oriented conference that could be a good fit for people here - Attachment (bc2.ch): Scientific programme > The [BC]2 Basel Computational Biology Conference is Switzerland’s main event in the domain of Computational Biology and one of the major events of its kind in Europe. The conference unites scientists from academia, industry and healthcare.
